title: Title and Extent of Operation of the Code
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
officialText: This Act shall be called the Indian Penal Code, and shall extend to the whole of India.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the name of the law and specifies that the Indian Penal Code applies throughout India.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A criminal offence committed in any State of India is governed by the IPC.
The IPC serves as the primary criminal law code across India.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 1?
faqs.answer: It provides the title of the Code and specifies its territorial extent.

faqs.question: Does the IPC apply throughout India?
faqs.answer: Yes, it extends to the whole of India.

title: Punishment of Offences Committed Within India
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
officialText: Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within India.
simpleExplanation: Anyone who commits an offence within India is punishable under the IPC, regardless of nationality unless specifically exempted by law.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence committed.
examples: A foreign tourist committing theft in India can be prosecuted under the IPC.
An Indian citizen committing assault in India is punishable under the IPC.

faqs.question: Does the IPC apply to foreigners in India?
faqs.answer: Yes, offences committed within India are generally punishable under the IPC.

faqs.question: What does this section establish?
faqs.answer: It establishes the territorial application of the IPC.

title: Punishment of Offences Committed Beyond, but Which by Law May Be Tried Within, India
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
officialText: Any person liable, by any Indian law, to be tried for an offence committed beyond India shall be dealt with according to the provisions of this Code as if the act had been committed within India.
simpleExplanation: This section allows India to punish certain offences committed outside India when Indian law permits trial within India.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence committed.
examples: An Indian citizen commits an offence abroad that is triable under Indian law.
A crime committed outside India affecting Indian interests is prosecuted in India where permitted.

faqs.question: Can offences committed outside India be punished under the IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes, if Indian law permits the offence to be tried in India.

faqs.question: Why is this provision important?
faqs.answer: It extends criminal liability to certain acts committed outside India.

title: Extension of Code to Extra-Territorial Offences
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
officialText: The provisions of this Code apply also to offences committed by any citizen of India in any place without and beyond India; any person on any ship or aircraft registered in India wherever it may be; and certain offences targeting computer resources located in India.
simpleExplanation: This section extends the IPC to specific offences committed outside India by Indian citizens, on Indian ships or aircraft, and in certain cases involving Indian computer resources.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence committed.
examples: An Indian citizen commits a criminal offence in another country.
An offence is committed on an Indian-registered aircraft flying overseas.
A cybercrime targets a computer resource located in India.

faqs.question: Does the IPC apply outside India?
faqs.answer: Yes, in situations specifically covered by this section.

faqs.question: Does it cover offences on Indian aircraft?
faqs.answer: Yes, offences committed on Indian-registered ships and aircraft are covered.

title: Certain Laws Not to Be Affected by This Act
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
officialText: Nothing in this Act shall affect the provisions of any Act for punishing mutiny and desertion of officers, soldiers, sailors, or airmen, or any special or local law.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that the IPC does not override special laws or laws dealing with specific matters such as military offences.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Military offences may be governed by special armed forces laws instead of the IPC.
A special law may apply even where the IPC contains similar provisions.

faqs.question: Does the IPC override all other laws?
faqs.answer: No. Special and local laws continue to apply where relevant.

faqs.question: Why is this section included?
faqs.answer: To preserve the operation of special laws and military laws.

title: Definitions in the Code to Be Understood Subject to Exceptions
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Every definition in this Code of an offence, every penal provision, and every illustration of every such definition or penal provision, shall be understood subject to the exceptions contained in the Chapter entitled 'General Exceptions', though those exceptions are not repeated in such definition, penal provision, or illustration.
simpleExplanation: This section states that all offences and punishments in the IPC must be read along with the General Exceptions provided later in the Code.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An act that appears to be an offence may not be punishable if covered by a General Exception.
Self-defence provisions can apply even if not mentioned in a specific offence section.

faqs.question: Why must offences be read with General Exceptions?
faqs.answer: Because the IPC intends General Exceptions to apply throughout the Code.

faqs.question: Are exceptions repeated in every offence section?
faqs.answer: No. Section 6 makes them automatically applicable.

title: Sense of Expression Once Explained
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Every expression which is explained in any part of this Code is used in every part of this Code in conformity with the explanation.
simpleExplanation: Once a term is defined in the IPC, the same meaning applies throughout the Code unless the context requires otherwise.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A term defined in one chapter retains the same meaning elsewhere in the IPC.
Courts use consistent interpretations of defined expressions.

faqs.question: Why is consistency important in legal definitions?
faqs.answer: It ensures uniform interpretation of criminal law.

faqs.question: Does a definition apply throughout the IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless the context indicates otherwise.

title: Gender
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The pronoun 'he' and its derivatives are used of any person, whether male or female.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that masculine pronouns used in the IPC apply to all persons regardless of gender.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An offence described using the word 'he' may be committed by a man or a woman.
Legal interpretation is not restricted by grammatical gender.

faqs.question: Does the word 'he' refer only to men?
faqs.answer: No. It includes both males and females.

faqs.question: Why is this clarification included?
faqs.answer: To avoid ambiguity in legal interpretation.

title: Number
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Unless the contrary appears from the context, words importing the singular number include the plural number, and words importing the plural number include the singular number.
simpleExplanation: This section provides that singular words may include plural meanings and vice versa, unless the context indicates otherwise.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A reference to 'person' may include multiple persons where appropriate.
A provision mentioning 'documents' may apply to a single document.

faqs.question: Can singular words include plural meanings?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless the context requires a different interpretation.

faqs.question: Why is this rule necessary?
faqs.answer: To ensure flexibility and avoid technical loopholes.

title: Man, Woman
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'man' denotes a male human being of any age; the word 'woman' denotes a female human being of any age.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the terms 'man' and 'woman' for purposes of interpretation under the IPC.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A male child falls within the definition of 'man' under this section.
A female child falls within the definition of 'woman' under this section.

faqs.question: Does 'man' mean only an adult male?
faqs.answer: No. It includes a male human being of any age.

faqs.question: Does 'woman' include female children?
faqs.answer: Yes. It includes a female human being of any age.

title: Person
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'person' includes any Company or Association or body of persons, whether incorporated or not.
simpleExplanation: A person under IPC is not limited to an individual human being. It also includes companies, associations, organizations, and groups of people.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A private company can be treated as a person under the IPC.
An unregistered association may also be considered a person for legal purposes.

faqs.question: Does 'person' only mean an individual human being?
faqs.answer: No. It also includes companies, associations, and bodies of persons.

faqs.question: Can an unregistered organization be a person under IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes. Whether incorporated or not, it may be treated as a person.

title: Public
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'public' includes any class of the public or any community.
simpleExplanation: The term 'public' is broad and includes the whole population, a section of people, or a specific community.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Residents of a village may constitute a class of the public.
A religious community may be regarded as part of the public.

faqs.question: Does public mean every citizen of India?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. It may also refer to a specific class or community.

faqs.question: Can a community be considered public?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any community may fall within the meaning of public.

title: Queen
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'Queen' denotes the Sovereign for the time being of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.
simpleExplanation: This section reflected the colonial-era reference to the British Sovereign and was relevant during British rule.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: The term was used in laws enacted during British administration.
References to the Queen related to the British Crown.

faqs.question: Is this section relevant today?
faqs.answer: It remains part of the original IPC text but has little practical significance after India's independence.

faqs.question: Why was this definition included?
faqs.answer: The IPC was enacted during British rule and therefore referred to the British Sovereign.

title: Servant of Government
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The words 'servant of Government' denote any officer or servant continued, appointed or employed in India by or under the authority of Government.
simpleExplanation: This section defines who is considered a government servant for purposes of the IPC.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A government clerk is a servant of Government.
A government-appointed officer falls within this definition.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to senior officials?
faqs.answer: No. It applies to officers and servants employed under Government authority.

faqs.question: Why is this definition important?
faqs.answer: Several IPC offences specifically involve government servants.

title: British India
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The words 'British India' denote all territories and places within Her Majesty's dominions which are for the time being governed by Her Majesty through the Governor-General of India or through any Governor or other officer subordinate to the Governor-General of India.
simpleExplanation: This section defined British India during the colonial period and identified territories administered under British authority.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: The term was used to identify territories directly administered by the British Government in India.
Colonial laws often relied on this definition for territorial application.

faqs.question: Does British India still exist?
faqs.answer: No. The term is of historical significance following India's independence.

faqs.question: Why is this section retained in historical IPC texts?
faqs.answer: It formed part of the original IPC enacted during British rule.

title: Government of India
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The words 'Government of India' denote the Governor-General of India acting on behalf of the Crown.
simpleExplanation: This section historically defined the Government of India during British rule as the Governor-General acting for the British Crown.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Orders issued by the Governor-General were considered acts of the Government of India.
Colonial statutes frequently referred to the Government of India using this definition.

faqs.question: Is this definition still applicable today?
faqs.answer: No. It is a historical provision from the colonial era.

faqs.question: Who represented the Government of India under this section?
faqs.answer: The Governor-General acting on behalf of the British Crown.

title: Government
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'Government' denotes the Central Government or the Government of a State.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that references to Government include both the Central Government and State Governments.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A direction issued by a State Government falls within this definition.
A notification issued by the Central Government is also covered.

faqs.question: Does Government include State Governments?
faqs.answer: Yes. It includes both Central and State Governments.

faqs.question: Why is this definition important?
faqs.answer: Many offences and legal powers under criminal law involve government authorities.

title: India
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: India means the territory of India.
simpleExplanation: This section defines India for the purposes of the IPC and determines the territorial scope of the law.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An offence committed within the territory of India may be governed by the IPC.
The definition includes all territories that legally form part of India.

faqs.question: Why is the definition of India necessary?
faqs.answer: It helps determine where the IPC applies.

faqs.question: Does this definition affect jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Yes. Territorial jurisdiction depends on whether the act occurred within India.

title: Judge
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'Judge' denotes not only every person who is officially designated as a Judge, but also every person who is empowered by law to give, in any legal proceeding, civil or criminal, a definitive judgment, or a judgment which, if not appealed against, would be definitive, or a judgment which, if confirmed by some other authority, would be definitive, or who is one of a body of persons empowered by law to give such a judgment.
simpleExplanation: A Judge includes not only judicial officers formally called judges but also any person legally authorized to decide legal proceedings and deliver binding decisions.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A Sessions Judge is a Judge under this section.
Members of a legally constituted tribunal exercising judicial powers may be treated as Judges.

faqs.question: Does Judge only mean a court judge?
faqs.answer: No. It can include others legally empowered to give binding judgments.

faqs.question: Can tribunal members be Judges under IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they are legally empowered to deliver definitive judgments.

title: Court of Justice
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The words 'Court of Justice' denote a Judge who is empowered by law to act judicially alone, or a body of Judges which is empowered by law to act judicially as a body, when such Judge or body of Judges is acting judicially.
simpleExplanation: A Court of Justice means a legally constituted judicial authority exercising judicial functions either through a single judge or a panel of judges.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A High Court acting in a judicial capacity is a Court of Justice.
A Magistrate hearing a case acts as a Court of Justice.

faqs.question: When does a Judge become a Court of Justice?
faqs.answer: When exercising judicial powers under law.

faqs.question: Can multiple judges together form a Court of Justice?
faqs.answer: Yes. A body of judges acting judicially is also a Court of Justice.

title: Public Servant
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The words 'public servant' denote a person falling under any of the categories specified in this section, including commissioned officers in the military, judges, court officers, arbitrators, government officers, local authority officers, persons authorized to perform public duties, and others entrusted with public functions.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a broad definition of who is considered a public servant. It includes not only government employees but also various persons entrusted with public duties and authority.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A police officer is a public servant.
A judge performing judicial functions is a public servant.
An officer of a municipal corporation may be a public servant.

faqs.question: Are all government employees public servants?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, if they fall within the categories listed in this section.

faqs.question: Can a person outside government service be a public servant?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain persons entrusted with public duties may also be public servants.

title: Movable Property
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The words 'movable property' are intended to include corporeal property of every description, except land and things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything attached to the earth.
simpleExplanation: Movable property means physical property that can be moved from one place to another. Land and permanently attached objects are excluded.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A car is movable property.
Jewellery is movable property.
A building attached to land is not movable property.

faqs.question: Is land movable property?
faqs.answer: No. Land is specifically excluded.

faqs.question: Can electronic devices be movable property?
faqs.answer: Yes. They are physical objects capable of being moved.

title: Wrongful Gain and Wrongful Loss
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Wrongful gain is gain by unlawful means of property to which the person gaining is not legally entitled. Wrongful loss is loss by unlawful means of property to which the person losing it is legally entitled.
simpleExplanation: A person obtains wrongful gain when they unlawfully acquire property they are not entitled to. Wrongful loss occurs when someone is unlawfully deprived of property they are entitled to possess.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Keeping another person's wallet knowing it belongs to them may amount to wrongful gain.
Depriving a lawful owner of their property may result in wrongful loss.

faqs.question: Can wrongful gain exist without actual profit?
faqs.answer: Yes. Obtaining possession unlawfully may constitute wrongful gain.

faqs.question: Must wrongful loss involve money?
faqs.answer: No. It can involve any property to which a person is legally entitled.

title: Dishonestly
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Whoever does anything with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another person is said to do that thing dishonestly.
simpleExplanation: An act is done dishonestly when it is performed with the intention of causing wrongful gain or wrongful loss.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Taking someone's property intending to keep it is dishonest conduct.
Altering records to benefit oneself financially may be dishonest.

faqs.question: What is the key element of dishonesty?
faqs.answer: The intention to cause wrongful gain or wrongful loss.

faqs.question: Is actual loss necessary to prove dishonesty?
faqs.answer: No. The intention itself is important.

title: Fraudulently
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: A person is said to do a thing fraudulently if he does that thing with intent to defraud but not otherwise.
simpleExplanation: An act is fraudulent when it is done with the intention to deceive another person in order to obtain an unlawful advantage or cause loss.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Submitting forged documents to obtain a benefit may be fraudulent.
Creating fake records to deceive authorities is a fraudulent act.

faqs.question: What is required for an act to be fraudulent?
faqs.answer: There must be an intention to defraud.

faqs.question: Is deception an important element of fraud?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraud generally involves deception or dishonest concealment.

title: Reason to Believe
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: A person is said to have 'reason to believe' a thing, if he has sufficient cause to believe that thing but not otherwise.
simpleExplanation: A person has reason to believe something when the available facts and circumstances provide sufficient grounds for that belief. Mere suspicion is not enough.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person buying goods at an unusually low price from a suspicious source may have reason to believe they are stolen.
Receiving a forged document with obvious signs of alteration may give reason to believe it is fake.

faqs.question: Is suspicion the same as reason to believe?
faqs.answer: No. There must be sufficient cause to support the belief.

faqs.question: Does actual knowledge need to be proven?
faqs.answer: No. Reason to believe is a lower threshold than actual knowledge.

title: Property in Possession of Wife, Clerk or Servant
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: When property is in the possession of a person's wife, clerk or servant on account of that person, it is in that person's possession within the meaning of this Code.
simpleExplanation: Property held by a wife, clerk, or servant on behalf of another person is legally treated as being in the possession of that person.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Money held by a cashier for a company is considered in the company's possession.
Goods stored by a servant for the owner remain in the owner's possession.

faqs.question: Can a servant's possession be treated as the owner's possession?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the servant holds the property on behalf of the owner.

faqs.question: Why is this provision important?
faqs.answer: It helps determine possession in offences such as theft and criminal breach of trust.

title: Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: A person is said to 'counterfeit' who causes one thing to resemble another thing, intending by means of that resemblance to practise deception, or knowing it to be likely that deception will thereby be practised.
simpleExplanation: Counterfeiting means making one thing look like another with the intention of deceiving people or knowing that deception is likely to occur.
punishment: Punishment depends on the specific offence involving the counterfeit item.
examples: Printing fake currency notes is counterfeiting.
Manufacturing imitation government seals to deceive authorities is counterfeiting.

faqs.question: Must the copy be identical to the original?
faqs.answer: No. It only needs to resemble the original enough to cause deception.

faqs.question: Is intention to deceive important?
faqs.answer: Yes. Deception or the likelihood of deception is an essential element.

title: Document
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'document' denotes any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, as evidence of that matter.
simpleExplanation: A document includes any written, printed, marked, or recorded material that can be used as evidence of facts or information.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A signed contract is a document.
A handwritten note containing important information may be a document.
A map or chart may also be considered a document.

faqs.question: Does a document have to be on paper?
faqs.answer: No. It can be on any substance capable of recording information.

faqs.question: Why is this definition important?
faqs.answer: Many offences such as forgery depend on the existence of a document.

title: Valuable Security
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The words 'valuable security' denote a document which creates, extends, transfers, restricts, extinguishes or releases any legal right, or whereby any person acknowledges liability or certifies that he lies under legal liability, or has not a certain legal right.
simpleExplanation: A valuable security is a document that creates, transfers, modifies, or records legal rights, obligations, or liabilities.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A promissory note is a valuable security.
A bond acknowledging a debt is a valuable security.
A deed transferring ownership rights may be a valuable security.

faqs.question: Why is a valuable security important in criminal law?
faqs.answer: Forgery or misuse of valuable securities is treated as a serious offence.

faqs.question: Can a document acknowledging debt be a valuable security?
faqs.answer: Yes. Such documents are specifically covered.

title: A Will
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The words 'a will' denote any testamentary document.
simpleExplanation: A will is a legal document through which a person expresses how their property or estate should be distributed after their death.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A signed testament specifying heirs is a will.
A document directing distribution of assets after death is a will.

faqs.question: What is a testamentary document?
faqs.answer: A document expressing a person's wishes regarding the distribution of property after death.

faqs.question: Why is a will important under IPC?
faqs.answer: Forgery or fraudulent alteration of a will can constitute serious criminal offences.

title: Words Referring to Acts Include Illegal Omissions
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: In every part of this Code, except where a contrary intention appears from the context, words which refer to acts done extend also to illegal omissions.
simpleExplanation: Whenever the IPC refers to doing an act, it may also include failing to do something that a person is legally required to do.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A public servant who deliberately fails to perform a legal duty may be liable for an illegal omission.
A parent legally bound to provide care may commit an offence through omission.

faqs.question: Can failing to act be treated as an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes, if there is a legal duty to act and the failure is unlawful.

faqs.question: Does every omission become a crime?
faqs.answer: No. Only omissions that are legally wrongful are covered.

title: Act, Omission
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'act' denotes as well a series of acts as a single act; the word 'omission' denotes as well a series of omissions as a single omission.
simpleExplanation: An act or omission under IPC can consist of a single event or a series of connected events.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Repeated fraudulent transactions may constitute a series of acts.
Continuous neglect of a legal duty may amount to a series of omissions.

faqs.question: Can multiple actions be treated as one act?
faqs.answer: Yes. A series of acts may be treated as an act under this section.

faqs.question: Does omission include repeated failures?
faqs.answer: Yes. It includes both single and repeated omissions.

title: Acts Done by Several Persons in Furtherance of Common Intention
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: When a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.
simpleExplanation: When two or more persons commit a crime together with a shared intention, each participant can be held responsible for the entire act.
punishment: Punishment is the same as for the principal offence committed.
examples: Three people jointly plan and execute a robbery; all may be liable for the robbery.
A group acting together to assault a victim may all be held responsible.

faqs.question: Is physical participation necessary?
faqs.answer: Not always. Participation with common intention may be sufficient.

faqs.question: What is common intention?
faqs.answer: A shared plan or meeting of minds to commit a criminal act.

title: When Such an Act is Criminal by Reason of Its Being Done with a Criminal Knowledge or Intention
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Whenever an act, which is criminal only by reason of its being done with a criminal knowledge or intention, is done by several persons, each of such persons who joins in the act with such knowledge or intention is liable for the act in the same manner as if the act were done by him alone.
simpleExplanation: If an offence requires criminal knowledge or intention and several persons participate, each person who possesses that knowledge or intention is equally liable.
punishment: Punishment depends on the underlying offence committed.
examples: Several people knowingly participate in a fraudulent scheme; each may be liable.
Persons acting together with criminal intent may all be punished for the offence.

faqs.question: Does every participant become liable?
faqs.answer: Only those who possess the required criminal knowledge or intention.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 34?
faqs.answer: Section 35 focuses on shared criminal knowledge or intention where such mental elements are essential to the offence.

title: Effect Caused Partly by Act and Partly by Omission
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Wherever the causing of a certain effect, or an attempt to cause that effect, by an act or by an omission, is an offence, it is to be understood that the causing of that effect partly by an act and partly by an omission is the same offence.
simpleExplanation: A person can commit an offence through a combination of actions and failures to act. The law treats the combined conduct the same as if the result had been caused entirely by an act or entirely by an omission.
punishment: Punishment depends on the underlying offence.
examples: A caretaker intentionally withholds food and also administers harmful substances, causing injury.
A person causes damage through both wrongful actions and neglect of a legal duty.

faqs.question: Can a crime be committed through both action and inaction?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section specifically recognizes offences caused partly by acts and partly by omissions.

faqs.question: Does the punishment change because both are involved?
faqs.answer: No. Liability is determined according to the underlying offence.

title: Co-operation by Doing One of Several Acts Constituting an Offence
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: When an offence is committed by means of several acts, whoever intentionally co-operates in the commission of that offence by doing any one of those acts, either singly or jointly with any other person, commits that offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly participates in even one part of a crime may be held responsible for the offence if that act contributes to its commission.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence committed.
examples: A person who knowingly provides forged documents used in a fraud may be liable for the fraud.
Someone assisting in one stage of a robbery may be held responsible for the offence.

faqs.question: Must a person perform every part of the crime?
faqs.answer: No. Participation in even one essential act may be sufficient.

faqs.question: Is intention required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The cooperation must be intentional.

title: Persons Concerned in Criminal Act May Be Guilty of Different Offences
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Where several persons are engaged or concerned in the commission of a criminal act, they may be guilty of different offences by means of that act.
simpleExplanation: People involved in the same incident may be guilty of different offences depending on their intentions, knowledge, and participation.
punishment: Punishment varies according to the offence committed by each person.
examples: One participant may be guilty of murder while another may be guilty of a lesser offence.
Different members of a group may face different charges based on their conduct.

faqs.question: Can participants in the same crime face different charges?
faqs.answer: Yes. Liability depends on each person's role and mental state.

faqs.question: Why are offences sometimes different?
faqs.answer: Because criminal intention and participation may differ among individuals.

title: "Voluntarily"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: A person is said to cause an effect 'voluntarily' when he causes it by means whereby he intended to cause it, or by means which, at the time of employing those means, he knew or had reason to believe to be likely to cause it.
simpleExplanation: An act is voluntary when a person either intends the result or knows that the result is likely to occur because of their actions.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person striking another knowing injury is likely acts voluntarily.
A person intentionally setting fire to property acts voluntarily.

faqs.question: Does voluntarily always mean intentional?
faqs.answer: It includes both intentional acts and acts done with knowledge of likely consequences.

faqs.question: Why is this definition important?
faqs.answer: Many IPC offences require proof that an act was done voluntarily.

title: "Offence"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Except in the Chapters and Sections mentioned in this section, the word 'offence' denotes a thing made punishable by this Code. In certain contexts it also includes acts punishable under special or local laws.
simpleExplanation: An offence generally means any act or omission punishable under the IPC. In some situations, it also includes offences under other laws.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Theft under the IPC is an offence.
Certain acts punishable under special laws may also be treated as offences depending on the context.

faqs.question: Does offence only mean crimes under IPC?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, but in certain contexts it can include offences under special or local laws.

faqs.question: Why is this definition important?
faqs.answer: Many IPC provisions depend on whether a particular act qualifies as an offence.

title: "Special Law"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: A 'special law' is a law applicable to a particular subject.
simpleExplanation: A special law is legislation enacted to deal with a specific subject matter, category of persons, or type of activity rather than the general public at large.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: The Prevention of Corruption Act is a special law dealing with corruption offences.
The Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act (POCSO) is a special law relating to child sexual offences.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a special law and a general law?
faqs.answer: A special law applies to a specific subject or class of cases, while a general law applies broadly.

faqs.question: Can a special law override a general law?
faqs.answer: In many situations, a special law prevails over a general law concerning the same subject.

title: "Local Law"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: A 'local law' is a law applicable only to a particular part of India.
simpleExplanation: A local law is legislation that applies only within a specific geographical area rather than throughout the entire country.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A law applicable only to a particular State may be considered a local law.
A municipal regulation applicable only within a city may fall under this category.

faqs.question: Does a local law apply throughout India?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only within a specified geographical area.

faqs.question: Can local laws create offences?
faqs.answer: Yes. Local laws may create offences enforceable within their jurisdiction.

title: "Illegal", "Legally Bound to Do"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'illegal' is applicable to everything which is an offence, which is prohibited by law, or which furnishes ground for a civil action; and a person is said to be 'legally bound to do' whatever it is illegal in him to omit.
simpleExplanation: An act is illegal if it is prohibited by law, constitutes an offence, or gives rise to civil liability. A person is legally bound to do something when failure to do it would itself be unlawful.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Failing to file a legally required report may be an illegal omission.
A public servant may be legally bound to perform duties assigned by law.

faqs.question: Does illegal only mean criminal?
faqs.answer: No. It can also include acts giving rise to civil liability.

faqs.question: What does legally bound mean?
faqs.answer: A person is legally bound when the law requires them to perform a particular act.

title: "Injury"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'injury' denotes any harm whatever illegally caused to any person, in body, mind, reputation or property.
simpleExplanation: Injury under IPC is not limited to physical harm. It includes harm to a person's body, mental well-being, reputation, or property.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Causing bodily harm is an injury.
Defaming a person may cause injury to reputation.
Damaging someone's property may amount to injury.

faqs.question: Does injury only mean physical harm?
faqs.answer: No. It also includes harm to mind, reputation, and property.

faqs.question: Why is this definition broad?
faqs.answer: To ensure legal protection against different forms of unlawful harm.

title: "Life"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'life' denotes the life of a human being, unless the contrary appears from the context.
simpleExplanation: The term 'life' in the IPC refers to the life of a human being unless a different meaning is clearly intended by the context.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Offences affecting human life are interpreted using this definition.
The expression 'imprisonment for life' relates to the life of a human being.

faqs.question: Does life include animal life?
faqs.answer: Generally no. It refers to the life of a human being unless context indicates otherwise.

faqs.question: Why is this definition necessary?
faqs.answer: Many serious offences under the IPC involve danger to or deprivation of human life.

title: "Death"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'death' denotes the death of a human being unless the contrary appears from the context.
simpleExplanation: The term 'death' under the IPC refers to the death of a human being unless a different meaning is clearly intended by the context.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Causing the death of a person may attract offences such as culpable homicide or murder.
References to death in criminal law generally concern human beings.

faqs.question: Does death include the death of animals?
faqs.answer: No. Unless the context requires otherwise, it refers to the death of a human being.

faqs.question: Why is this definition important?
faqs.answer: Many serious offences are based on causing or attempting to cause death.

title: "Animal"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'animal' denotes any living creature other than a human being.
simpleExplanation: Under the IPC, an animal means any living creature that is not a human being.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A dog is an animal under this section.
A horse, cow, bird, or fish may be treated as an animal for IPC purposes.

faqs.question: Are humans considered animals under this section?
faqs.answer: No. Humans are specifically excluded.

faqs.question: Does the definition include birds and fish?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any living creature other than a human being is included.

title: "Vessel"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'vessel' denotes anything made for the conveyance by water of human beings or of property.
simpleExplanation: A vessel is any boat, ship, or watercraft designed to transport people or goods on water.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A cargo ship is a vessel.
A passenger ferry is a vessel under this definition.

faqs.question: Does a vessel have to be large?
faqs.answer: No. Any craft used for conveyance by water may qualify.

faqs.question: Are fishing boats vessels?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they are used for transportation on water.

title: "Year" and "Month"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Wherever the word 'year' or the word 'month' is used, it is to be understood that the year or month is to be reckoned according to the British calendar.
simpleExplanation: References to years and months in the IPC are calculated according to the Gregorian (British) calendar.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A sentence of one year is calculated according to the calendar year.
A limitation period of one month is measured according to the calendar month.

faqs.question: Which calendar is used under this section?
faqs.answer: The Gregorian calendar, historically referred to as the British calendar.

faqs.question: Why is this clarification needed?
faqs.answer: To ensure uniform calculation of time periods under the law.

title: "Section"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'section' denotes one of those portions of a Chapter of this Code which are distinguished by prefixed numeral figures.
simpleExplanation: A section refers to a numbered provision within a chapter of the IPC.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Section 302 is a section of the IPC.
Each numbered provision in the Code is treated as a separate section.

faqs.question: What is a section in the IPC?
faqs.answer: A numbered legal provision within the Code.

faqs.question: Why is this definition included?
faqs.answer: To clarify references made throughout the IPC.

title: "Oath"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: The word 'oath' includes a solemn affirmation substituted by law for an oath, and any declaration required or authorized by law to be made before a public servant or to be used for the purpose of proof, whether in a Court of Justice or not.
simpleExplanation: An oath includes not only a religious oath but also a legal affirmation or declaration made before an authorized authority.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A witness giving evidence under affirmation is considered to have taken an oath.
A declaration submitted before a public authority may fall within this definition.

faqs.question: Is a religious oath necessary?
faqs.answer: No. A legal affirmation is treated the same as an oath.

faqs.question: Does this apply outside courts?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain declarations before public servants are also included.

title: "Good Faith"
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Nothing is said to be done or believed in good faith which is done or believed without due care and attention.
simpleExplanation: An act is done in good faith only when it is performed honestly and with proper care and attention. Mere honesty without reasonable care is insufficient.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A doctor who carefully examines a patient before treatment may act in good faith.
A person making allegations without verifying facts may not be acting in good faith.

faqs.question: Is honesty alone enough for good faith?
faqs.answer: No. Due care and attention are also required.

faqs.question: Why is good faith important?
faqs.answer: Several IPC exceptions and protections depend on acts being done in good faith.

title: Harbour
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
officialText: Except in section 157, and in section 130 in the case in which the harbour is given by the wife or husband of the person harboured, the word 'harbour' includes the supplying of a person with shelter, food, drink, money, clothes, arms, ammunition, or means of conveyance, or the assisting of a person by any means, whether of the same kind as those enumerated in this section or not, to evade apprehension.
simpleExplanation: Harbouring a person means helping them avoid arrest or capture by providing shelter, resources, transportation, money, or any other assistance.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence relating to harbouring under the relevant IPC section.
examples: Providing a fugitive with a place to hide may amount to harbouring.
Giving money or transport to help a wanted person escape may constitute harbouring.

faqs.question: Does harbouring only mean giving shelter?
faqs.answer: No. It includes many forms of assistance that help a person evade arrest.

faqs.question: Can financial help amount to harbouring?
faqs.answer: Yes. Supplying money to assist evasion may constitute harbouring.

title: Punishments
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: The punishments to which offenders are liable under the provisions of this Code are: First—Death; Secondly—Imprisonment for life; Fourthly—Imprisonment, which is of two descriptions, namely: (1) Rigorous, that is, with hard labour; (2) Simple; Fifthly—Forfeiture of property; Sixthly—Fine.
simpleExplanation: This section lists the different types of punishments that may be imposed under the IPC.
punishment: Defines categories of punishments rather than prescribing punishment for a specific offence.
examples: Murder may attract death penalty or life imprisonment.
Certain offences may be punishable with fine only.

faqs.question: What are the main punishments under IPC?
faqs.answer: Death, life imprisonment, imprisonment, forfeiture of property, and fine.

faqs.question: Does every offence carry all these punishments?
faqs.answer: No. Each offence specifies the applicable punishment.

title: Commutation of Sentence of Death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: In every case in which sentence of death shall have been passed, the appropriate Government may, without the consent of the offender, commute the punishment for any other punishment provided by this Code.
simpleExplanation: The Government has the power to replace a death sentence with another punishment allowed under law, even without the offender's consent.
punishment: Provides for commutation of a death sentence.
examples: A death sentence may be commuted to life imprisonment by the Government.
Executive clemency powers may be exercised under this provision.

faqs.question: Can a death sentence be reduced?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Government may commute it to another lawful punishment.

faqs.question: Is the offender's consent necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Consent is not required.

title: Commutation of Sentence of Imprisonment for Life
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: In every case in which a sentence of imprisonment for life shall have been passed, the appropriate Government may, without the consent of the offender, commute the punishment for imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding fourteen years.
simpleExplanation: The Government may reduce a life imprisonment sentence to a fixed term of imprisonment, subject to legal limits.
punishment: Provides for commutation of life imprisonment.
examples: A life sentence may be commuted to a term of years by the Government.
Executive powers may be exercised to modify the punishment.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be reduced?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Government may commute it according to law.

faqs.question: Is the prisoner's approval required?
faqs.answer: No. Consent is not necessary.

title: Omitted
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: Section 56 has been repealed and is no longer in force.
simpleExplanation: This section was removed from the Indian Penal Code through legislative amendment and has no current legal effect.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Section 56 does not create any offence or punishment.
Courts do not apply repealed provisions.

faqs.question: Is Section 56 still enforceable?
faqs.answer: No. It has been repealed.

faqs.question: Should Section 56 be considered while studying IPC?
faqs.answer: Only for historical reference.

title: Fractions of Terms of Punishment
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: In calculating fractions of terms of punishment, imprisonment for life shall be reckoned as equivalent to imprisonment for twenty years.
simpleExplanation: For the purpose of calculating fractions of a sentence under the IPC, life imprisonment is treated as equivalent to twenty years.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: One-half of a life sentence is calculated as ten years for statutory computation purposes.
This provision is used when a law refers to a fraction of life imprisonment.

faqs.question: Does life imprisonment actually mean twenty years?
faqs.answer: No. Life imprisonment ordinarily means imprisonment for the remainder of a person's natural life unless remitted or commuted.

faqs.question: Why is twenty years mentioned?
faqs.answer: Only for calculating fractions of punishment under the IPC.

title: Omitted
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: Section 58 has been repealed and is no longer in force.
simpleExplanation: This provision was omitted from the IPC through legislative amendment and has no legal effect today.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: No offence or punishment is defined under this section.
The section is retained only in historical records of the IPC.

faqs.question: Can a person be prosecuted under Section 58?
faqs.answer: No. The section has been repealed.

faqs.question: Why do some IPC section numbers appear blank?
faqs.answer: Because certain provisions were repealed but their numbering was retained.

title: Omitted
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: Section 59 has been repealed and is no longer in force.
simpleExplanation: This section was removed from the IPC and no longer has any operative legal effect.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Courts do not apply repealed provisions.
Section 59 remains only for historical reference.

faqs.question: Does Section 59 contain any current law?
faqs.answer: No. It has been omitted.

faqs.question: Why is the section number still present?
faqs.answer: Legislative drafting often retains numbering after repeal.

title: Sentence May Be Wholly or Partly Rigorous or Simple
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: In every case in which an offender is punishable with imprisonment which may be of either description, it shall be competent to the Court which sentences such offender to direct in the sentence that such imprisonment shall be wholly rigorous, or wholly simple, or that any part thereof shall be rigorous and the rest simple.
simpleExplanation: Where a law allows either rigorous or simple imprisonment, the court may decide whether the entire sentence will be rigorous, entirely simple, or partly of each type.
punishment: Provides guidance on the nature of imprisonment to be imposed.
examples: A court may order six months of rigorous imprisonment followed by six months of simple imprisonment.
A court may direct that the entire sentence be rigorous imprisonment.

faqs.question: What is rigorous imprisonment?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment that involves hard labour as prescribed by prison rules.

faqs.question: Can a sentence contain both rigorous and simple imprisonment?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court may divide the sentence between the two forms.

title: Omitted
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: Section 61 has been repealed and is no longer in force.
simpleExplanation: This section was removed from the Indian Penal Code through subsequent amendments and has no legal effect today.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: No prosecution can be initiated under a repealed provision.
Section 61 is retained only for historical reference in the IPC.

faqs.question: Is Section 61 currently enforceable?
faqs.answer: No. It has been repealed.

faqs.question: Why do repealed sections remain numbered in the IPC?
faqs.answer: To maintain consistency in legal references and citations.

title: Omitted
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: Section 62 has been repealed and is no longer in force.
simpleExplanation: This provision was omitted from the IPC and no longer creates any legal rights, duties, offences, or punishments.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Courts do not apply repealed sections when deciding cases.
Section 62 exists only in historical versions of the IPC.

faqs.question: Does Section 62 contain any active law?
faqs.answer: No. It has been repealed.

faqs.question: Can a repealed section be enforced?
faqs.answer: No. Repealed provisions cease to have legal effect.

title: Amount of Fine
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: Where no sum is expressed to which a fine may extend, the amount of fine to which the offender is liable is unlimited, but shall not be excessive.
simpleExplanation: If the IPC does not specify a maximum fine for an offence, the court may impose any reasonable amount of fine, provided it is not excessive or disproportionate.
punishment: Fine of an amount determined by the court, subject to reasonableness.
examples: A court may impose a substantial fine where the offence caused significant harm.
The fine must remain proportionate to the facts of the case.

faqs.question: Can courts impose any amount of fine?
faqs.answer: Yes, where no maximum is prescribed, but the amount must not be excessive.

faqs.question: What factors determine the fine amount?
faqs.answer: Courts consider the nature of the offence, circumstances of the case, and the offender's financial condition.

title: Sentence of Imprisonment for Non-Payment of Fine
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: In every case of an offence punishable with imprisonment as well as fine, in which the offender is sentenced to a fine, whether with or without imprisonment, and in every case of an offence punishable with fine only, in which the offender is sentenced to a fine, it shall be competent to the Court which sentences such offender to direct by the sentence that, in default of payment of the fine, the offender shall suffer imprisonment for a certain term.
simpleExplanation: A court may order imprisonment if a convicted person fails to pay a fine imposed as part of the sentence.
punishment: Imprisonment in default of payment of fine, as determined by the court.
examples: A person fined ₹10,000 may be ordered to undergo imprisonment if the fine is not paid.
The default imprisonment begins only when the fine remains unpaid.

faqs.question: Is imprisonment automatic when a fine is imposed?
faqs.answer: No. It occurs only if the fine is not paid and the court has provided for default imprisonment.

faqs.question: Does paying the fine avoid default imprisonment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Payment of the fine generally removes the default imprisonment liability.

title: Limit to Imprisonment for Non-Payment of Fine, When Imprisonment and Fine Awardable
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: The term for which the Court directs the offender to be imprisoned in default of payment of a fine shall not exceed one-fourth of the term of imprisonment which is the maximum fixed for the offence, if the offence is punishable with imprisonment as well as fine.
simpleExplanation: When an offence carries both imprisonment and fine, the default imprisonment for non-payment of the fine cannot exceed one-fourth of the maximum imprisonment prescribed for that offence.
punishment: Limits the duration of default imprisonment.
examples: If an offence carries a maximum imprisonment of four years, the default imprisonment cannot exceed one year.
The court must remain within the statutory limit when fixing default imprisonment.

faqs.question: How is default imprisonment calculated?
faqs.answer: It cannot exceed one-fourth of the maximum imprisonment prescribed for the offence.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to offences punishable only with fine?
faqs.answer: No. It applies where both imprisonment and fine are prescribed.

title: Description of Imprisonment for Non-Payment of Fine
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: The imprisonment which the Court imposes in default of payment of a fine may be of any description to which the offender might have been sentenced for the offence.
simpleExplanation: When a person fails to pay a fine, the court may order either rigorous imprisonment or simple imprisonment, provided that type of imprisonment is authorized for the offence.
punishment: Provides for rigorous or simple imprisonment in default of payment of fine.
examples: If an offence permits rigorous imprisonment, the default imprisonment may also be rigorous.
A court may order simple imprisonment for non-payment of a fine where appropriate.

faqs.question: Can default imprisonment be rigorous?
faqs.answer: Yes, if rigorous imprisonment is an authorized punishment for the offence.

faqs.question: Who decides the nature of the imprisonment?
faqs.answer: The court determines whether the imprisonment will be rigorous or simple.

title: Imprisonment for Non-Payment of Fine, When Offence Punishable with Fine Only
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: If the offence is punishable with fine only, the imprisonment which the Court imposes in default of payment of the fine shall be simple, and the term shall not exceed the limits specified in this section according to the amount of the fine.
simpleExplanation: Where an offence is punishable only with a fine, any imprisonment imposed for failure to pay that fine must be simple imprisonment and must remain within statutory limits.
punishment: Simple imprisonment in default of payment of fine, subject to statutory limits.
examples: A person convicted of an offence punishable only with a fine may be ordered to undergo simple imprisonment if the fine is not paid.
The duration of imprisonment depends on the amount of the unpaid fine.

faqs.question: Can rigorous imprisonment be imposed under this section?
faqs.answer: No. Only simple imprisonment is permitted.

faqs.question: Does the term of imprisonment vary with the fine amount?
faqs.answer: Yes. The IPC prescribes limits based on the amount of the fine.

title: Imprisonment to Terminate on Payment of Fine
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: The imprisonment imposed in default of payment of a fine shall terminate whenever that fine is either paid or levied by process of law.
simpleExplanation: A person imprisoned for not paying a fine must be released once the fine is paid or recovered through legal procedures.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A prisoner serving default imprisonment is released after the fine is paid.
Recovery of the fine through attachment of property ends the default imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can default imprisonment continue after the fine is paid?
faqs.answer: No. It must end immediately upon payment or lawful recovery of the fine.

faqs.question: What if the fine is recovered through legal proceedings?
faqs.answer: The imprisonment also terminates in that case.

title: Termination of Imprisonment on Payment of Proportional Part of Fine
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: If, before the expiration of the term of imprisonment fixed in default of payment of the fine, such proportion of the fine be paid or levied that the term of imprisonment suffered in default is not less than proportional to the part of the fine still unpaid, the imprisonment shall terminate.
simpleExplanation: A person may secure early release from default imprisonment by paying part of the fine, provided the imprisonment already served is proportionate to the remaining unpaid amount.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An offender may be released after making partial payment of a fine and serving a proportionate part of the default sentence.
Courts calculate whether the imprisonment already undergone corresponds to the unpaid balance.

faqs.question: Must the entire fine be paid for release?
faqs.answer: Not always. Partial payment may be sufficient under the conditions specified in this section.

faqs.question: How is release determined?
faqs.answer: By comparing the unpaid portion of the fine with the imprisonment already served.

title: Fine Leviable Within Six Years, or During Imprisonment; Death Not to Discharge Property from Liability
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: The fine, or any part thereof which remains unpaid, may be levied at any time within six years after the passing of the sentence, and if under the sentence the offender is liable to imprisonment for a longer period than six years, then at any time previous to the expiration of that period; and the death of the offender does not discharge from liability any property which would, after his death, be legally liable for his debts.
simpleExplanation: An unpaid fine can be recovered for up to six years after sentencing, or longer if the offender remains imprisoned. The offender's death does not automatically prevent recovery of the fine from property legally liable for debts.
punishment: Provides rules for recovery of fines.
examples: A court may recover an unpaid fine several years after conviction.
Property belonging to a deceased offender's estate may still be used to satisfy an unpaid fine where legally permissible.

faqs.question: How long can an unpaid fine be recovered?
faqs.answer: Generally up to six years after sentencing, subject to the conditions in the section.

faqs.question: Does death cancel an unpaid fine?
faqs.answer: No. Property legally liable for debts may still be proceeded against.

title: Limit of Punishment of Offence Made Up of Several Offences
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Where anything which is an offence is made up of parts, any of which parts is itself an offence, the offender shall not be punished with the punishment of more than one of such offences, unless it be so expressly provided.
simpleExplanation: If a single act constitutes multiple offences because it is made up of several components, a person should not be punished separately for each component unless the law specifically allows it.
punishment: Limits multiple punishments for the same criminal act.
examples: A person committing one act that technically satisfies several overlapping offences may receive only one punishment.
Courts avoid double punishment unless expressly authorized by law.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished twice for the same act?
faqs.answer: Generally no, unless the law specifically permits separate punishments.

faqs.question: Why does this section exist?
faqs.answer: To prevent excessive punishment for substantially the same criminal conduct.

title: Punishment of Person Guilty of One of Several Offences, the Judgment Stating That It Is Doubtful of Which
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: In all cases in which judgment is given that a person is guilty of one of several offences specified in the judgment, but that it is doubtful of which of those offences he is guilty, the offender shall be punished for the offence for which the lowest punishment is provided if the same punishment is not provided for all.
simpleExplanation: When a court is certain that a person committed an offence but cannot determine exactly which one of several offences applies, the person receives the punishment for the least severe offence.
punishment: Punishment for the offence carrying the lowest penalty among the possible offences.
examples: A court finds that either offence A or offence B was committed but cannot determine which one; the lower punishment applies.
The benefit of doubt operates in sentencing where uncertainty remains.

faqs.question: What happens when the exact offence cannot be identified?
faqs.answer: The accused is punished for the offence carrying the lowest punishment.

faqs.question: Does this mean the accused is acquitted?
faqs.answer: No. The court must still be satisfied that one of the specified offences was committed.

title: Solitary Confinement
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: Whenever any person is convicted of an offence for which, under this Code, the Court has power to sentence him to rigorous imprisonment, the Court may order that the offender shall be kept in solitary confinement for any portion or portions of the imprisonment to which he is sentenced, not exceeding three months in the whole, according to the scale prescribed in this section.
simpleExplanation: Courts may order solitary confinement as part of a sentence of rigorous imprisonment, but only within strict statutory limits.
punishment: Solitary confinement for up to three months, subject to legal limits.
examples: A court sentencing a person to rigorous imprisonment may include a limited period of solitary confinement.
The duration of solitary confinement depends on the length of the imprisonment sentence.

faqs.question: Can solitary confinement be imposed in every case?
faqs.answer: No. It can only be imposed where rigorous imprisonment is authorized.

faqs.question: Is there a maximum limit?
faqs.answer: Yes. The total period cannot exceed three months.

title: Limit of Solitary Confinement
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: In executing a sentence of solitary confinement, such confinement shall in no case exceed fourteen days at a time, with intervals between periods of confinement as prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: Even when solitary confinement is ordered, it cannot be imposed continuously beyond the limits fixed by law. Mandatory breaks must be provided.
punishment: Regulates the execution of solitary confinement.
examples: A prisoner cannot be kept in solitary confinement continuously for an unlawful duration.
Intervals between periods of solitary confinement are mandatory.

faqs.question: Can solitary confinement continue indefinitely?
faqs.answer: No. Strict statutory limits apply.

faqs.question: Why are intervals required?
faqs.answer: To protect prisoners from excessive hardship and abuse.

title: Enhanced Punishment for Certain Offences Under Chapter XII or Chapter XVII After Previous Conviction
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
officialText: Whoever, having been convicted by a Court in India of an offence punishable under Chapter XII or Chapter XVII of this Code with imprisonment of either description for a term of three years or upwards, shall afterwards be guilty of any offence punishable under either of those Chapters with imprisonment for a term of three years or upwards, shall be subject to enhanced punishment as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: A person who commits certain property-related offences again after a previous conviction may receive a harsher punishment as a repeat offender.
punishment: Enhanced punishment for repeat offenders, subject to the limits prescribed by law.
examples: A person previously convicted of theft who commits another qualifying property offence may receive a higher sentence.
Repeat offenders may face stricter punishment than first-time offenders.

faqs.question: Does this apply to first-time offenders?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only when there is a qualifying previous conviction.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: To deter habitual offenders and protect society from repeated criminal conduct.

title: Act Done by a Person Bound, or by Mistake of Fact Believing Himself Bound, by Law
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law, in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for an act done because they are legally required to do it, or because they honestly and reasonably believe, due to a mistake of fact, that the law requires them to do it.
punishment: Not applicable. This is a legal defense.
examples: A police officer arrests a person under a valid warrant believing he is legally bound to do so.
A person acts on a court order that later turns out to contain a factual error.

faqs.question: Does a mistake of law provide protection?
faqs.answer: No. The protection applies only to a mistake of fact, not a mistake of law.

faqs.question: Is good faith required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must honestly believe they are legally bound to act.

title: Act of Judge When Acting Judicially
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a Judge when acting judicially in the exercise of any power which is, or which in good faith he believes to be, given to him by law.
simpleExplanation: A judge is protected from criminal liability for acts performed while exercising judicial powers, provided the judge acts lawfully or honestly believes that the law grants such authority.
punishment: Not applicable. This is a legal defense.
examples: A judge issues a lawful arrest warrant while performing judicial duties.
A judge passes an order believing in good faith that the law authorizes it.

faqs.question: Can a judge be prosecuted for judicial decisions?
faqs.answer: Generally not, when acting judicially within or in good-faith belief of legal authority.

faqs.question: Does this protection apply to acts outside judicial functions?
faqs.answer: No. The protection applies only to judicial acts.

title: Act Done Pursuant to the Judgment or Order of Court
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing which is done in pursuance of, or which is warranted by, the judgment or order of a Court of Justice, if done while such judgment or order remains in force, is an offence, notwithstanding the Court may have had no jurisdiction to pass such judgment or order, provided the person doing the act in good faith believes that the Court had such jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: A person acting under a court order is protected from criminal liability if the order is still in force and the person honestly believes the court had authority to issue it.
punishment: Not applicable. This is a legal defense.
examples: A police officer executes a court-issued warrant in good faith.
Court officials enforce a judicial order that is later found defective.

faqs.question: What if the court lacked jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: The protection still applies if the person acted in good faith believing the court had jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Must the order still be valid at the time?
faqs.answer: Yes. The order must remain in force when the act is done.

title: Act Done by a Person Justified, or by Mistake of Fact Believing Himself Justified, by Law
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by any person who is justified by law, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law, in good faith believes himself to be justified by law, in doing it.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for an act that the law permits or for an act they honestly and reasonably believe is permitted due to a mistake of fact.
punishment: Not applicable. This is a legal defense.
examples: A police officer uses lawful force while carrying out official duties.
A person assists in an arrest believing the suspect is legally wanted.

faqs.question: Does a mistake of law excuse criminal liability?
faqs.answer: No. Only a mistake of fact may provide protection.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sections 76 and 79?
faqs.answer: Section 76 applies when a person believes they are bound by law; Section 79 applies when they believe they are justified by law.

title: Accident in Doing a Lawful Act
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge, in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for harm caused by a genuine accident while performing a lawful act lawfully, carefully, and without criminal intent.
punishment: Not applicable. This is a legal defense.
examples: A person accidentally injures another during a lawful sporting activity despite taking proper precautions.
A driver following traffic rules causes harm due to an unforeseeable mechanical failure.

faqs.question: Does every accident qualify for this defense?
faqs.answer: No. The act must be lawful, and proper care and caution must have been exercised.

faqs.question: Can negligence be protected under Section 80?
faqs.answer: No. Negligence generally defeats the defense because proper care and caution are required.

title: Act Likely to Cause Harm, but Done Without Criminal Intent and to Prevent Other Harm
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for causing harm if the act was done in good faith, without criminal intent, and to prevent a greater harm.
punishment: Not applicable. This is a legal defense.
examples: A firefighter breaks down a door to rescue people trapped inside a burning building.
A person cuts a rope to prevent a collapsing structure from causing greater injury.

faqs.question: Can harm be justified under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it is done in good faith to prevent a greater harm.

faqs.question: Is criminal intention allowed?
faqs.answer: No. The act must be free from criminal intent.

title: Act of a Child Under Seven Years of Age
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under seven years of age.
simpleExplanation: A child below seven years of age cannot be held criminally responsible for any act under the IPC.
punishment: Not applicable. Complete immunity from criminal liability.
examples: A six-year-old child breaking a window cannot be prosecuted under the IPC.
A child under seven causing damage is not criminally liable.

faqs.question: Can a child under seven be convicted of a crime?
faqs.answer: No. The law grants complete immunity.

faqs.question: Why is this protection provided?
faqs.answer: Children below seven are presumed incapable of understanding criminal responsibility.

title: Act of a Child Above Seven and Under Twelve of Immature Understanding
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above seven years of age and under twelve, who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge the nature and consequences of his conduct on that occasion.
simpleExplanation: A child between seven and twelve years old is not criminally liable if the child lacked sufficient maturity to understand the nature and consequences of the act.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: An eight-year-old who cannot understand the consequences of an act may be protected under this section.
A ten-year-old lacking maturity may not be held criminally responsible.

faqs.question: Are all children between seven and twelve protected?
faqs.answer: No. Protection depends on whether the child had sufficient maturity of understanding.

faqs.question: Who determines maturity?
faqs.answer: The court examines the child's understanding based on the facts of the case.

title: Act of a Person of Unsound Mind
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable if, due to unsoundness of mind, they were unable to understand the nature of their act or that it was wrong or illegal.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person suffering from severe mental illness who cannot understand their actions may be protected.
An accused unable to distinguish right from wrong due to unsoundness of mind may invoke this defense.

faqs.question: Does every mental illness qualify?
faqs.answer: No. The illness must render the person incapable of understanding the nature or wrongfulness of the act.

faqs.question: When is mental condition assessed?
faqs.answer: At the time the act was committed.

title: Act of a Person Incapable of Judgment by Reason of Intoxication Caused Against His Will
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, is, by reason of intoxication, incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law, provided that the thing which intoxicated him was administered to him without his knowledge or against his will.
simpleExplanation: A person may avoid criminal liability if they became intoxicated without their consent or knowledge and, because of that intoxication, could not understand the nature or wrongfulness of their actions.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person's drink is secretly drugged, causing severe intoxication and loss of judgment.
An individual forced to consume an intoxicating substance may rely on this defense.

faqs.question: Does voluntary intoxication qualify for this defense?
faqs.answer: No. The intoxication must be involuntary.

faqs.question: What must be proven?
faqs.answer: That the intoxication occurred without knowledge or against the person's will and rendered them incapable of judgment.

title: Offence Requiring a Particular Intent or Knowledge Committed by One Who is Intoxicated
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
