officialText: In cases where an act done is not an offence unless done with a particular knowledge or intent, a person who does the act in a state of intoxication shall be liable to be dealt with as if he had the same knowledge as he would have had if he had not been intoxicated, unless the intoxicating substance was administered to him without his knowledge or against his will.
simpleExplanation: Voluntary intoxication is generally not a defense. A person who becomes intoxicated voluntarily is treated as having the same knowledge they would have had if sober.
punishment: Not applicable. This section limits the intoxication defense.
examples: A person who voluntarily drinks alcohol and commits an offence cannot usually avoid liability by claiming intoxication.
Knowledge required for an offence may still be attributed to an intoxicated person.

faqs.question: Can voluntary intoxication be used as a defense?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The law treats the person as having the same knowledge as if sober.

faqs.question: Is there any exception?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the intoxication was caused without the person's knowledge or against their will.

title: Act Not Intended and Not Known to Be Likely to Cause Death or Grievous Hurt, Done by Consent
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing which is not intended to cause death or grievous hurt, and which is not known by the doer to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt, is an offence by reason of any harm which it may cause to a person above eighteen years of age who has given consent, whether express or implied, to suffer that harm or to take the risk of that harm.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for causing harm to an adult who voluntarily consented to the risk, provided there was no intention or likelihood of causing death or grievous hurt.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: Participants in a lawful contact sport consent to ordinary risks of injury.
A person consents to a harmless physical challenge and suffers a minor injury.

faqs.question: Can consent justify serious injury?
faqs.answer: No. The section does not apply if death or grievous hurt is intended or likely.

faqs.question: What age must the consenting person be?
faqs.answer: The person must be above eighteen years of age.

title: Act Not Intended to Cause Death, Done by Consent in Good Faith for Person's Benefit
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing, which is not intended to cause death, is an offence by reason of any harm which it may cause, or be intended by the doer to cause, or be known by the doer to be likely to cause, to any person for whose benefit it is done in good faith, and who has given a consent, whether express or implied, to suffer that harm, or to take the risk of that harm.
simpleExplanation: A person acting in good faith for another person's benefit is protected if the other person consents to the risk and there is no intention to cause death.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A surgeon performs a risky operation with the patient's consent.
A medical procedure causing pain but intended to improve health is protected.

faqs.question: Why are doctors often protected under this section?
faqs.answer: Because medical treatment is generally performed in good faith for the patient's benefit with consent.

faqs.question: Can the act be intended to cause death?
faqs.answer: No. The protection does not apply if death is intended.

title: Act Done in Good Faith for Benefit of Child or Person of Unsound Mind, by or by Consent of Guardian
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing which is done in good faith for the benefit of a person under twelve years of age, or of a person of unsound mind, by or with the consent of the guardian or other person having lawful charge of that person, is an offence by reason of any harm which it may cause, or be intended by the doer to cause, or be known by the doer to be likely to cause, to that person.
simpleExplanation: Acts done in good faith for the benefit of a child under twelve or a person of unsound mind are protected when performed with the consent of the lawful guardian, subject to statutory limitations.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A doctor treats a child with the consent of the parents.
Medical care is provided to a person of unsound mind with guardian approval.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to all acts involving children?
faqs.answer: No. The act must be done in good faith for the child's benefit.

faqs.question: Is guardian consent required?
faqs.answer: Yes, except where another legal exception applies.

title: Consent Known to Be Given Under Fear or Misconception
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: A consent is not such a consent as is intended by any section of this Code if the consent is given by a person under fear of injury, or under a misconception of fact, and if the person doing the act knows, or has reason to believe, that the consent was given in consequence of such fear or misconception; or if the consent is given by a person who, from unsoundness of mind or intoxication, is unable to understand the nature and consequence of that to which he gives consent; or unless the contrary appears from the context, if the consent is given by a person who is under twelve years of age.
simpleExplanation: Consent is invalid if it is obtained through fear, deception, misconception of facts, intoxication, unsoundness of mind, or from a child under twelve years of age.
punishment: Not applicable. This section defines valid consent.
examples: Consent obtained by threatening a person is not valid consent.
Consent based on false facts may be legally invalid.
A child under twelve generally cannot provide legally valid consent under the IPC.

faqs.question: Is consent obtained through fear valid?
faqs.answer: No. Consent given under fear of injury is invalid.

faqs.question: Can an intoxicated person give valid consent?
faqs.answer: Not if they are unable to understand the nature and consequences of the act.

title: Exclusion of Acts Which Are Offences Independently of Harm Caused
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: The exceptions in sections 87, 88 and 89 do not extend to acts which are offences independently of any harm which they may cause, or be intended to cause, or be known to be likely to cause, to the person giving the consent, or on whose behalf the consent is given.
simpleExplanation: Even if a person consents, certain acts remain offences because the law prohibits them regardless of whether harm occurs or consent is given.
punishment: Not applicable. This section limits the scope of consent-based defenses.
examples: Consent cannot legalize an act that is independently prohibited by law.
A person cannot avoid liability for an offence merely because the victim consented.

faqs.question: Can consent make every act legal?
faqs.answer: No. Some acts remain offences regardless of consent.

faqs.question: Why does this section exist?
faqs.answer: To prevent consent from being used to justify inherently unlawful acts.

title: Act Done in Good Faith for Benefit of a Person Without Consent
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence by reason of any harm which it may cause to a person for whose benefit it is done in good faith, even without that person's consent, if the circumstances are such that it is impossible for that person to signify consent, or if that person is incapable of giving consent and has no guardian or other person in lawful charge from whom consent can be obtained in time.
simpleExplanation: A person acting in good faith for another's benefit may be protected even without consent when obtaining consent is impossible or impractical, such as during emergencies.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A doctor performs emergency surgery on an unconscious accident victim.
Life-saving treatment is provided when no guardian can be contacted in time.

faqs.question: Can treatment be given without consent in emergencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it is done in good faith for the person's benefit.

faqs.question: Does this apply when consent can easily be obtained?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only when obtaining consent is impossible or impractical.

title: Communication Made in Good Faith
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: No communication made in good faith is an offence by reason of any harm to the person to whom it is made, if it is made for the benefit of that person.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for communicating information in good faith for another person's benefit, even if the information causes emotional distress or other harm.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A doctor honestly informs a patient about a serious medical condition.
A friend warns another person about a potential danger in good faith.

faqs.question: Can unpleasant information be legally communicated?
faqs.answer: Yes, if communicated in good faith for the recipient's benefit.

faqs.question: What is required for protection under this section?
faqs.answer: The communication must be made in good faith and for the person's benefit.

title: Act to Which a Person Is Compelled by Threats
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Except murder and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is compelled to do it by threats which reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death would otherwise be the consequence.
simpleExplanation: A person may be excused from criminal liability if they commit an offence because of an immediate threat of death, except in cases of murder or certain offences against the State.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person forced at gunpoint to assist in a crime may invoke this defense.
An individual compelled by threats of immediate death may not be criminally liable.

faqs.question: Can this defense be used for murder?
faqs.answer: No. Murder is specifically excluded.

faqs.question: Must the threat be immediate?
faqs.answer: Yes. There must be a reasonable fear of instant death.

title: Act Causing Slight Harm
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.
simpleExplanation: The law does not concern itself with trivial or insignificant harm that a reasonable person would not consider worth complaining about.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A minor accidental push causing no meaningful injury may fall under this section.
A trivial inconvenience that causes negligible harm may not amount to an offence.

faqs.question: Does every minor harm amount to a crime?
faqs.answer: No. Trivial harms may be excluded under this section.

faqs.question: How is slight harm determined?
faqs.answer: By considering whether a reasonable person would regard the harm as significant.

title: Things Done in Private Defence
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for acts done while lawfully exercising the right of private defence of body or property.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person uses reasonable force to protect themselves from an attacker.
A homeowner prevents a trespasser from unlawfully entering the house.

faqs.question: Is every act of self-defense protected?
faqs.answer: Only acts that fall within the lawful limits of private defence.

faqs.question: Does this section itself define the limits of private defence?
faqs.answer: No. The limits are explained in the following sections.

title: Right of Private Defence of the Body and of Property
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: Every person has a right, subject to the restrictions contained in section 99, to defend: First—His own body, and the body of any other person, against any offence affecting the human body; Secondly—The property, whether movable or immovable, of himself or of any other person, against certain offences including theft, robbery, mischief and criminal trespass.
simpleExplanation: A person may defend themselves, others, or property against unlawful attacks and certain offences affecting property.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person intervenes to stop an assault on another individual.
A shop owner prevents a thief from stealing goods.

faqs.question: Can I defend another person?
faqs.answer: Yes. The right extends to defending the body of any other person.

faqs.question: Does the right apply only to my own property?
faqs.answer: No. It may also apply to the property of another person.

title: Right of Private Defence Against the Act of a Person of Unsound Mind, etc.
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: When an act, which would otherwise be a certain offence, is not that offence by reason of the youth, want of maturity of understanding, unsoundness of mind, intoxication of the person doing that act, or by reason of any misconception on the part of that person, every person has the same right of private defence against that act which he would have if the act were that offence.
simpleExplanation: A person may exercise private defence even against someone who is legally incapable of committing an offence due to age, intoxication, unsoundness of mind, or similar reasons.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person may defend themselves against an attack by an intoxicated individual.
Private defence may be exercised against a violent act committed by a person of unsound mind.

faqs.question: Can private defence be used against a child?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the child's act creates a threat that would otherwise amount to an offence.

faqs.question: Does the attacker's legal responsibility matter?
faqs.answer: No. The right focuses on the threat faced by the defender.

title: Acts Against Which There Is No Right of Private Defence
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: There is no right of private defence against an act which does not reasonably cause the apprehension of death or grievous hurt if done, or attempted to be done, by a public servant acting in good faith under colour of his office. There is also no right where there is sufficient time to seek protection from public authorities. The right does not extend to inflicting more harm than is necessary.
simpleExplanation: The right of private defence is subject to limitations. It cannot be exercised against lawful acts of public servants in certain situations, when legal protection is available, or beyond what is reasonably necessary.
punishment: Not applicable. This section restricts the right of private defence.
examples: A person cannot use excessive force when defending themselves.
A lawful police action generally cannot be resisted through private defence.

faqs.question: Can I use unlimited force in self-defense?
faqs.answer: No. Only reasonable and necessary force may be used.

faqs.question: Can private defence be claimed against lawful police action?
faqs.answer: Generally no, where the officer acts in good faith under colour of office.

title: When the Right of Private Defence of the Body Extends to Causing Death
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: The right of private defence of the body extends, under the restrictions mentioned in section 99, to the voluntary causing of death or any other harm to the assailant if the offence occasions the reasonable apprehension of death, grievous hurt, rape, unnatural lust, kidnapping, abduction, wrongful confinement under certain circumstances, or acid attack.
simpleExplanation: A person may, in exceptional circumstances, cause the death of an attacker when facing grave threats such as death, grievous hurt, rape, kidnapping, acid attack, or similar serious offences.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person facing an armed attack that creates a reasonable fear of death may use deadly force in self-defence.
A victim resisting a rape attempt may exercise the right to the extent permitted by law.

faqs.question: Can self-defense justify causing death?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only in the serious circumstances specified in this section.

faqs.question: Must the threat be reasonable?
faqs.answer: Yes. There must be a reasonable apprehension of the specified harm.

title: When Such Right Extends to Causing Any Harm Other Than Death
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: If the offence does not fall within any of the descriptions enumerated in Section 100, the right of private defence of the body does not extend to the voluntary causing of death to the assailant, but does extend, under the restrictions mentioned in Section 99, to the voluntary causing to the assailant of any harm other than death.
simpleExplanation: When the threat is not serious enough to justify causing death under Section 100, a person may still use reasonable force and cause non-fatal harm to protect themselves or others.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person pushes away and injures an attacker during a minor assault.
Reasonable force is used to stop a person from causing bodily harm.

faqs.question: Can I kill an attacker in every case of self-defense?
faqs.answer: No. Death can be caused only in the situations listed in Section 100.

faqs.question: What force is allowed under this section?
faqs.answer: Only such force as is reasonably necessary, excluding causing death.

title: Commencement and Continuance of the Right of Private Defence of the Body
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: The right of private defence of the body commences as soon as a reasonable apprehension of danger to the body arises from an attempt or threat to commit an offence, though the offence may not have been committed, and continues as long as such apprehension of danger continues.
simpleExplanation: The right of private defence begins when a person reasonably fears an attack and continues until that danger has passed.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person sees an attacker approaching with a weapon and acts in self-defense before being struck.
The right continues while the threat remains active.

faqs.question: Must I wait until I am actually attacked?
faqs.answer: No. The right begins when there is a reasonable apprehension of danger.

faqs.question: When does the right end?
faqs.answer: When the danger or reasonable apprehension of danger ceases.

title: When the Right of Private Defence of Property Extends to Causing Death
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: The right of private defence of property extends, under the restrictions mentioned in Section 99, to the voluntary causing of death or any other harm to the wrongdoer if the offence is robbery, house-breaking by night, mischief by fire to a dwelling house or place used for custody of property, or theft, mischief, or house-trespass under circumstances causing reasonable apprehension of death or grievous hurt.
simpleExplanation: In certain serious offences against property, such as robbery or house-breaking by night, the law permits the use of deadly force in defence of property.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A homeowner faces armed robbers and uses deadly force in self-defence of property.
A person resists a night-time house-breaking that poses a serious threat.

faqs.question: Can deadly force be used to protect property?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only in the exceptional situations specified in this section.

faqs.question: Does ordinary theft justify causing death?
faqs.answer: Not unless the circumstances fall within the situations described in the section.

title: When Such Right Extends to Causing Any Harm Other Than Death
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: If the offence, the committing of which, or the attempting to commit which, occasions the exercise of the right of private defence of property, is not of any of the descriptions enumerated in Section 103, that right does not extend to the voluntary causing of death, but does extend, under the restrictions mentioned in Section 99, to the voluntary causing to the wrongdoer of any harm other than death.
simpleExplanation: When an offence against property is not serious enough to justify causing death, a person may still use reasonable force and cause non-fatal harm to protect property.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A shopkeeper uses reasonable force to stop a thief from escaping.
A property owner restrains a trespasser without causing death.

faqs.question: Can death be caused for every property offence?
faqs.answer: No. Only the offences listed in Section 103 permit that level of force.

faqs.question: What force is allowed here?
faqs.answer: Reasonable force causing harm short of death.

title: Commencement and Continuance of the Right of Private Defence of Property
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: The right of private defence of property commences when a reasonable apprehension of danger to the property begins and continues for different durations depending on the nature of the offence, such as theft, robbery, criminal trespass, or house-breaking.
simpleExplanation: The right to defend property starts when a reasonable threat to property arises and continues until the threat or consequences of the offence have ended, as specified by law.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: The right against theft continues until the property is recovered or public authorities intervene.
The right against robbery continues while the offender causes fear, restraint, or attempts to escape with the property.

faqs.question: When does the right to defend property begin?
faqs.answer: When there is a reasonable apprehension of danger to the property.

faqs.question: Does the duration depend on the offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The IPC specifies different periods for theft, robbery, trespass, and house-breaking.

title: Right of Private Defence Against Deadly Assault When There Is Risk of Harm to Innocent Person
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
officialText: If in the exercise of the right of private defence against an assault which reasonably causes the apprehension of death, the defender cannot effectively exercise that right without risk of harm to an innocent person, the right of private defence extends to running that risk.
simpleExplanation: A person defending themselves against a deadly attack may lawfully act even if there is an unavoidable risk of harm to an innocent person, provided the action is necessary to protect against the deadly assault.
punishment: Not applicable where the exception applies.
examples: A person being attacked by an armed assailant fires in self-defence even though there is an unavoidable risk to a nearby innocent person.
A hostage victim resists a deadly attack despite the possibility of collateral harm.

faqs.question: Does this section allow intentional harm to innocent people?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only where the risk is unavoidable while defending against a deadly assault.

faqs.question: What type of threat must exist?
faqs.answer: There must be a reasonable apprehension of death from the assault.

title: Abetment of a Thing
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: A person abets the doing of a thing who: First—Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly—Engages with one or more persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy; or Thirdly—Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.
simpleExplanation: A person commits abetment by encouraging, conspiring, or intentionally helping another person to commit an act or offence.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence abetted and the relevant IPC provisions.
examples: A person persuades another to commit theft.
Several people plan a crime together and one of them takes steps to carry out the plan.
A person knowingly provides tools to help commit an offence.

faqs.question: What are the three forms of abetment?
faqs.answer: Instigation, conspiracy, and intentional aid.

faqs.question: Can a person be liable even if they do not commit the offence themselves?
faqs.answer: Yes. Abettors may be criminally liable.

title: Abettor
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: A person abets an offence who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.
simpleExplanation: An abettor is a person who encourages, assists, or otherwise abets the commission of an offence or an act that would be an offence if committed by a legally responsible person.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence abetted and applicable provisions.
examples: A person who encourages a child to commit theft may be an abettor.
Someone who assists another in carrying out a criminal plan may be an abettor.

faqs.question: Must the abettor commit the offence personally?
faqs.answer: No. Liability arises from the act of abetment.

faqs.question: Can a person abet someone legally incapable of committing an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The abettor may still be liable.

title: Punishment of Abetment if the Act Abetted Is Committed in Consequence and Where No Express Provision Is Made
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence.
simpleExplanation: If a person abets an offence and the offence is actually committed because of that abetment, the abettor may receive the same punishment as the principal offender unless the IPC provides otherwise.
punishment: Same punishment as the offence abetted, unless otherwise provided.
examples: A person instigates a theft and the theft is committed.
An individual aids the commission of an offence that is subsequently carried out.

faqs.question: Can an abettor receive the same punishment as the offender?
faqs.answer: Yes. Where no special provision exists, the abettor may be punished as if they committed the offence.

faqs.question: Must the offence actually occur?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section applies when the act abetted is committed.

title: Punishment of Abetment if Person Abetted Does Act with Different Intention from That of Abettor
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whoever abets the commission of an offence shall, if the person abetted commits the act with a different intention or knowledge from that of the abettor, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence which would have been committed if the act had been done with the intention or knowledge of the abettor and with no other.
simpleExplanation: An abettor's liability is generally determined by the intention or knowledge that the abettor possessed, even if the person committing the act had a different mental state.
punishment: Punishment based on the offence corresponding to the abettor's intention or knowledge.
examples: A person encourages conduct expecting minor harm, but the principal offender acts with a more serious intention.
The abettor is punished according to the offence they intended to facilitate.

faqs.question: Is an abettor always liable for the exact offence committed?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Liability may depend on the abettor's own intention or knowledge.

faqs.question: Why is intention important here?
faqs.answer: Because criminal liability often depends on the mental state of the accused.

title: Liability of Abettor When One Act Abetted and Different Act Done
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had directly abetted it, provided the act done was a probable consequence of the abetment and was committed under the influence of the instigation, conspiracy, or aid.
simpleExplanation: An abettor may be held responsible for a different act than the one originally intended if that act was a likely consequence of the abetment.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence actually committed.
examples: A person instigates an assault, and the victim suffers more serious injuries than expected.
An abettor may be liable where the resulting act was a foreseeable consequence of the abetment.

faqs.question: Can an abettor be liable for a different offence than the one intended?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the offence committed was a probable consequence of the abetment.

faqs.question: Must the act be connected to the abetment?
faqs.answer: Yes. It must result from the instigation, conspiracy, or aid.

title: Abettor When Liable to Cumulative Punishment for Act Abetted and for Act Done
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: If the act for which the abettor is liable under Section 111 is committed in addition to the act abetted, and constitutes a distinct offence, the abettor may be punished for both offences.
simpleExplanation: An abettor may be punished for both the offence originally abetted and the additional offence actually committed if they are separate offences.
punishment: Punishment for both offences, subject to applicable legal provisions.
examples: A person abets causing hurt, but a separate offence is also committed as a consequence.
The abettor may face liability for multiple offences arising from the same conduct.

faqs.question: Can an abettor be punished for more than one offence?
faqs.answer: Yes, when separate offences arise from the abetment.

faqs.question: Must the offences be distinct?
faqs.answer: Yes. The additional act must constitute a separate offence.

title: Liability of Abettor for an Effect Caused by the Act Abetted Different from That Intended by the Abettor
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: When an act is abetted with the intention of causing a particular effect, and a different effect is caused by the act, the abettor is liable for the effect actually caused if he knew that the act was likely to cause that effect.
simpleExplanation: An abettor can be held liable for consequences different from those intended if those consequences were reasonably foreseeable.
punishment: Punishment depends on the effect and offence actually caused.
examples: A person abets an assault expecting injury, but the victim dies from the attack.
Liability may arise if the more serious consequence was foreseeable.

faqs.question: Must the abettor intend the exact result?
faqs.answer: No. Foreseeable consequences may also create liability.

faqs.question: What is the key factor?
faqs.answer: Whether the abettor knew the consequence was likely.

title: Abettor Present When Offence Is Committed
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whenever any person who, if absent, would be liable to be punished as an abettor, is present when the act or offence for which he would be punishable in consequence of the abetment is committed, he shall be deemed to have committed such act or offence.
simpleExplanation: An abettor who is present when the offence is committed may be treated as if they personally committed the offence.
punishment: Same punishment as for the offence committed.
examples: A person who planned a theft and is present during its execution may be treated as a principal offender.
An instigator attending the commission of the offence may incur direct liability.

faqs.question: Does mere presence create liability?
faqs.answer: No. The person must already qualify as an abettor.

faqs.question: Why is presence important?
faqs.answer: Because the law treats a present abettor more like a direct participant.

title: Abetment of Offence Punishable with Death or Imprisonment for Life if Offence Not Committed
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whoever abets the commission of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life shall, if the offence is not committed in consequence of the abetment and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. If any act for which the abettor is liable causes hurt to any person, the punishment may extend to fourteen years and fine.
simpleExplanation: A person may be punished for abetting a very serious offence even if the offence is never actually committed.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; up to 14 years and fine if hurt is caused.
examples: A person encourages another to commit murder, but the murder never takes place.
An attempted conspiracy to commit a capital offence may attract punishment under this section.

faqs.question: Can abetment be punished even if the offence never occurs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Serious offences may attract punishment even when not carried out.

faqs.question: What if someone is injured because of the abetment?
faqs.answer: The punishment may be increased up to fourteen years.

title: Abetment of Offence Punishable with Imprisonment if Offence Not Committed
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whoever abets an offence punishable with imprisonment shall, if that offence is not committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for that offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth of the longest term provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both; and if the abettor or the person abetted is a public servant whose duty it is to prevent the offence, the punishment may extend to one-half of the longest term provided for the offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who abets an offence punishable with imprisonment can still be punished even if the offence is never committed. Higher punishment applies when a public servant is involved.
punishment: Up to one-fourth of the maximum imprisonment for the offence, or fine, or both; up to one-half in certain cases involving public servants.
examples: A person encourages another to commit theft, but the theft never occurs.
A public servant fails in a duty and abets an offence that is not ultimately committed.

faqs.question: Can abetment be punished even if the offence does not happen?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section specifically punishes such abetment.

faqs.question: Is punishment higher for public servants?
faqs.answer: Yes. Enhanced punishment may apply where a public servant is involved.

title: Abetting Commission of Offence by the Public or by More Than Ten Persons
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whoever abets the commission of an offence by the public generally or by any number or class of persons exceeding ten shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who encourages a large group of people or the general public to commit an offence may be punished under this section.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person publicly urges a crowd to commit violence.
A leader encourages a group of more than ten persons to engage in unlawful acts.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to a small group?
faqs.answer: It specifically applies to the public or a group exceeding ten persons.

faqs.question: Is actual commission of the offence necessary?
faqs.answer: The focus is on the act of abetment itself.

title: Concealing Design to Commit Offence Punishable with Death or Imprisonment for Life
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whoever intending to facilitate or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby facilitate the commission of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, voluntarily conceals by any act or illegal omission the existence of a design to commit such offence, shall, if the offence be committed, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offence be not committed, with imprisonment up to three years and fine.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly hides a plan to commit a very serious offence such as murder may be punished, even if the offence is never actually carried out.
punishment: Up to 7 years and fine if the offence is committed; up to 3 years and fine if not committed.
examples: A person learns of a planned murder and deliberately conceals the information.
Someone hides evidence of a conspiracy to commit a capital offence.

faqs.question: Can silence amount to concealment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Illegal omission may constitute concealment.

faqs.question: Does punishment apply even if the offence never happens?
faqs.answer: Yes, though the punishment is lower.

title: Public Servant Concealing Design to Commit Offence Which It Is His Duty to Prevent
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, intending to facilitate or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby facilitate the commission of an offence which it is his duty as such public servant to prevent, voluntarily conceals by any act or illegal omission the existence of a design to commit such offence, shall be punished according to the provisions of this section.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who hides information about an offence that they are duty-bound to prevent may face criminal punishment.
punishment: Punishment varies depending on the seriousness of the offence concealed.
examples: A police officer conceals information about a planned robbery.
A public authority intentionally ignores a known criminal plan.

faqs.question: Why are public servants treated differently?
faqs.answer: Because they have a legal duty to prevent certain offences.

faqs.question: Can omission alone lead to liability?
faqs.answer: Yes. Deliberate failure to disclose may be punishable.

title: Concealing Design to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
officialText: Whoever intending to facilitate or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby facilitate the commission of an offence punishable with imprisonment, voluntarily conceals by any act or illegal omission the existence of a design to commit such offence, shall, if the offence be committed, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one-fourth of the longest term provided for the offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both; and if the offence be not committed, with imprisonment up to one-eighth of the longest term, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly hides a plan to commit an imprisonable offence may be punished, whether or not the offence is ultimately committed.
punishment: Up to one-fourth of the maximum punishment if the offence is committed; up to one-eighth if not committed.
examples: A person conceals a planned burglary from authorities.
Someone intentionally withholds information about a criminal conspiracy.

faqs.question: Must the offence actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Concealment itself may be punishable.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of the plan necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The concealment must be intentional or knowing.

title: Definition of Criminal Conspiracy
chapter: Chapter VA - Criminal Conspiracy
officialText: When two or more persons agree to do, or cause to be done, an illegal act, or an act which is not illegal by illegal means, such an agreement is designated a criminal conspiracy. Provided that no agreement except an agreement to commit an offence shall amount to a criminal conspiracy unless some act besides the agreement is done by one or more parties in pursuance thereof.
simpleExplanation: Criminal conspiracy occurs when two or more persons agree to commit an illegal act or to achieve a lawful objective through illegal means. In most cases, some action must be taken to carry out the agreement.
punishment: Punishment is provided under Section 120B.
examples: Two persons agree to commit a robbery and begin planning its execution.
Several people agree to obtain government contracts through forged documents.

faqs.question: Is mere agreement enough for criminal conspiracy?
faqs.answer: For conspiracy to commit an offence, yes. In other cases, an overt act is generally required.

faqs.question: How many persons are needed for a conspiracy?
faqs.answer: At least two persons must agree.

title: Punishment of Criminal Conspiracy
chapter: Chapter VA - Criminal Conspiracy
officialText: Whoever is a party to a criminal conspiracy to commit an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment for a term of two years or more shall, where no express provision is made elsewhere in this Code for the punishment of such conspiracy, be punished in the same manner as if he had abetted such offence. In other cases, the punishment may extend to six months, or fine, or both.
simpleExplanation: Persons participating in a criminal conspiracy may be punished even if the planned offence is not completed. Serious conspiracies attract severe punishment.
punishment: For serious offences, punishment similar to abetment; otherwise imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A conspiracy to commit murder may attract punishment even if the murder never occurs.
A conspiracy to commit fraud may be punished under this section.

faqs.question: Can conspiracy be punished even if the offence is not committed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The conspiracy itself is punishable.

faqs.question: Does punishment depend on the planned offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. More serious planned offences attract harsher punishment.

title: Waging, or Attempting to Wage War, or Abetting Waging of War, Against the Government of India
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever wages war against the Government of India, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes acts of war, attempted war, or assistance in waging war against the Government of India.
punishment: Death, or imprisonment for life, and fine.
examples: Organizing an armed rebellion against the Government of India.
Providing support to a group engaged in waging war against the State.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to actual war?
faqs.answer: No. Attempts and abetment are also punishable.

faqs.question: Is this one of the most serious offences under IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes. It carries the possibility of death penalty.

title: Conspiracy to Commit Offences Punishable by Section 121
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever conspires to commit any of the offences punishable by Section 121, or conspires to overawe by means of criminal force or the show of criminal force the Central Government or any State Government, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Planning or conspiring to wage war against the Government, or to intimidate it through criminal force, is itself a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A group secretly plans an armed uprising against the Government.
Conspiring to use force to compel the Government to act in a certain way.

faqs.question: Must war actually occur for this offence?
faqs.answer: No. The conspiracy itself is punishable.

faqs.question: What does overawe mean?
faqs.answer: To intimidate or compel through force or threat of force.

title: Collecting Arms, etc., with Intention of Waging War Against the Government of India
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever collects men, arms or ammunition or otherwise prepares to wage war with the intention of either waging or being prepared to wage war against the Government of India shall be punished with imprisonment for life or imprisonment which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Preparing for war against the Government by gathering people, weapons, or resources is a serious criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Recruiting armed groups for an uprising against the Government.
Stockpiling weapons with the intention of waging war.

faqs.question: Is actual war necessary for punishment?
faqs.answer: No. Preparatory acts alone may constitute the offence.

faqs.question: What kind of preparation is covered?
faqs.answer: Collecting men, arms, ammunition, or other preparations for war.

title: Concealing with Intent to Facilitate Design to Wage War
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever, by any act or illegal omission, conceals the existence of a design to wage war against the Government of India, intending by such concealment to facilitate, or knowing it to be likely that such concealment will facilitate, the waging of war, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly hiding information about a plan to wage war against the Government is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A person learns of a plan to wage war and deliberately conceals the information.
An individual hides evidence relating to an armed rebellion.

faqs.question: Can omission amount to concealment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Deliberately failing to disclose information may be punishable.

faqs.question: Must the person intend to help the plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent to facilitate, or knowledge that facilitation is likely, is required.

title: Assaulting President, Governor, etc., with Intent to Compel or Restrain Exercise of Lawful Power
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever, with the intention of inducing or compelling the President of India, or the Governor of any State, to exercise or refrain from exercising any lawful power, assaults or wrongfully restrains, or attempts to wrongfully restrain, the President or such Governor, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: It is a serious offence to assault or wrongfully restrain the President or a Governor in an attempt to influence the exercise of their official powers.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person physically restrains a Governor to prevent an official action.
An individual assaults the President to compel a particular governmental decision.

faqs.question: Is actual restraint necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Even an attempt to wrongfully restrain is punishable.

faqs.question: Why does this section exist?
faqs.answer: To protect constitutional authorities from coercion and interference.

title: Sedition
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever by words, either spoken or written, or by signs, or by visible representation, or otherwise, brings or attempts to bring into hatred or contempt, or excites or attempts to excite disaffection towards the Government established by law in India shall be punished as provided in this section. Explanation: 'Disaffection' includes disloyalty and all feelings of enmity. Comments expressing disapproval of Government measures with a view to lawful change, without exciting hatred, contempt or disaffection, do not constitute an offence.
simpleExplanation: Historically, this section dealt with acts intended to create hatred, contempt, or disaffection against the Government established by law. Its interpretation has been significantly limited by judicial decisions, and it has effectively been replaced under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita framework.
punishment: Historically: Imprisonment for life with fine, or imprisonment up to 3 years with fine, or fine.
examples: Advocating violent rebellion against the Government was historically prosecuted under this section.
Mere criticism of government policies without incitement to violence was generally not sufficient.

faqs.question: Is criticism of the Government sedition?
faqs.answer: No. Lawful criticism or disapproval of government actions is not by itself sedition.

faqs.question: Is this section still in active use?
faqs.answer: The legal position has changed significantly, and the BNS has introduced different provisions relating to acts against the sovereignty and integrity of India.

title: Waging War Against Any Asiatic Power in Alliance with the Government of India
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever wages war against the Government of any Asiatic Power in alliance or at peace with the Government of India, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, to which fine may be added, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits waging war against friendly Asiatic nations that are allied with or at peace with India.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 7 years, and fine.
examples: Organizing armed attacks against a friendly allied nation.
Assisting a military campaign against a nation at peace with India.

faqs.question: Does this section apply only to India?
faqs.answer: No. It protects certain foreign governments allied with or at peace with India.

faqs.question: Is abetment also punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempting or abetting such war is punishable.

title: Committing Depredation on Territories of Power at Peace with the Government of India
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever commits depredation, or makes preparations to commit depredation, on the territories of any Power at peace with the Government of India shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine, and any property used or intended to be used in committing such depredation may be forfeited.
simpleExplanation: It is an offence to engage in plundering, raiding, or similar hostile acts against territories belonging to nations that are at peace with India.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, fine, and possible forfeiture of property.
examples: Organizing raids into the territory of a friendly foreign nation.
Preparing resources for cross-border plundering activities.

faqs.question: What is depredation?
faqs.answer: It generally means plundering, raiding, or violent taking of property.

faqs.question: Can preparation alone be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preparations to commit depredation are also covered.

title: Receiving Property Taken by War or Depredation Mentioned in Sections 125 and 126
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever receives any property knowing it to have been taken in the commission of any of the offences mentioned in Sections 125 and 126 shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine, and the property so received shall be liable to forfeiture.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly receives property obtained through warlike acts or depredation against friendly foreign states commits an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, fine, and forfeiture of property.
examples: A person knowingly purchases goods looted during an unlawful raid on a friendly nation.
Receiving stolen property from cross-border depredation activities.

faqs.question: Is knowledge important under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know that the property was obtained through such offences.

faqs.question: Can the property be confiscated?
faqs.answer: Yes. The property is liable to forfeiture.

title: Public Servant Voluntarily Allowing Prisoner of State or War to Escape
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant and having the custody of any State prisoner or prisoner of war, voluntarily suffers such prisoner to escape from any place in which such prisoner is confined, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who intentionally allows a State prisoner or prisoner of war to escape commits a serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A prison officer deliberately leaves a secure gate open to facilitate escape.
A public servant secretly assists a prisoner of war in escaping custody.

faqs.question: Does accidental escape fall under this section?
faqs.answer: No. The escape must be voluntarily permitted.

faqs.question: Why is the punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because it involves a breach of public trust and national security concerns.

title: Public Servant Negligently Suffering Such Prisoner to Escape
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant and having the custody of any State prisoner or prisoner of war, negligently suffers such prisoner to escape from any place in which such prisoner is confined, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: A public servant responsible for a State prisoner or prisoner of war commits an offence if negligence results in the prisoner's escape.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A prison guard leaves a secure gate unlocked, allowing a prisoner of war to escape.
A public servant fails to follow required security procedures, resulting in escape.

faqs.question: Is intention required under this section?
faqs.answer: No. Negligence alone is sufficient.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 128?
faqs.answer: Section 128 involves intentional conduct, while Section 129 deals with negligence.

title: Aiding Escape of, Rescuing or Harbouring Such Prisoner
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever aids or assists in the escape of a State prisoner or prisoner of war, rescues or attempts to rescue such prisoner, or harbours or conceals such prisoner after escape, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Helping a State prisoner or prisoner of war escape, rescuing them, or hiding them after escape is a serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A person provides transport to help a prisoner of war escape.
Someone shelters a State prisoner knowing that the prisoner has escaped.

faqs.question: Does harbouring an escaped prisoner amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Concealing or sheltering such a prisoner is punishable.

faqs.question: Is an attempt to rescue also punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempts are expressly covered.

title: Abetting Mutiny, or Attempting to Seduce a Soldier, Sailor or Airman from His Duty
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets the committing of mutiny by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India, or attempts to seduce any such officer, soldier, sailor or airman from his allegiance or duty, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Encouraging military personnel to rebel against authority or abandon their duties is a serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A person encourages soldiers to disobey lawful military commands.
An individual attempts to persuade military personnel to join a rebellion.

faqs.question: What is mutiny?
faqs.answer: Mutiny generally refers to rebellion or insubordination by military personnel against lawful authority.

faqs.question: Is merely attempting to influence a soldier punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the purpose is to seduce the soldier from duty or allegiance.

title: Abetment of Mutiny, if Mutiny Is Committed in Consequence Thereof
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets the committing of mutiny by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman, if mutiny is committed in consequence of that abetment, shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, or imprisonment which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: A person who encourages mutiny and whose encouragement results in actual mutiny faces extremely severe punishment.
punishment: Death, or life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A civilian instigates military personnel to revolt and the mutiny occurs.
An organizer's actions directly lead to a military rebellion.

faqs.question: Why is the punishment more severe than Section 131?
faqs.answer: Because actual mutiny has occurred as a result of the abetment.

faqs.question: Can the death penalty be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section permits the death penalty.

title: Abetment of Assault by Soldier, Sailor or Airman on His Superior Officer, When in Execution of His Office
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman on any superior officer being in the execution of his office shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Encouraging military personnel to assault a superior officer performing official duties is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A person urges a soldier to attack a commanding officer during official duties.
Someone encourages violence against a superior officer carrying out military functions.

faqs.question: Must the superior officer be on duty?
faqs.answer: Yes. The officer must be acting in the execution of official duties.

faqs.question: Does the abettor need to physically participate in the assault?
faqs.answer: No. Encouraging or instigating the assault is sufficient.

title: Abetment of Such Assault if the Assault Is Committed
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman on any superior officer being in the execution of his office, if such assault is committed in consequence of that abetment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: If a person encourages a military member to assault a superior officer and the assault actually occurs, the abettor faces enhanced punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person persuades a soldier to attack a commanding officer and the attack takes place.
An instigator is held liable when the encouraged assault is carried out.

faqs.question: Is punishment higher than under Section 133?
faqs.answer: Yes. Because the assault was actually committed.

faqs.question: Must the assault result from the abetment?
faqs.answer: Yes. The assault must occur in consequence of the abetment.

title: Abetment of Desertion of Soldier, Sailor or Airman
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets the desertion of any officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Helping or encouraging military personnel to abandon their service duties amounts to an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person persuades a soldier to leave military service without authorization.
An individual assists a sailor in avoiding military obligations.

faqs.question: What is desertion?
faqs.answer: Desertion is the unauthorized abandonment of military service or duty.

faqs.question: Can civilians commit this offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any person who abets desertion may be liable.

title: Harbouring Deserter
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an officer, soldier, sailor or airman has deserted, harbours such person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly sheltering or concealing a military deserter is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person hides a soldier knowing that he has deserted military service.
A friend shelters a deserter to prevent military authorities from locating him.

faqs.question: Must the person know that the individual is a deserter?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Does temporary shelter count as harbouring?
faqs.answer: It may, depending on the circumstances and intent.

title: Deserter Concealed on Board Merchant Vessel Through Negligence of Master
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: The master or person in charge of a merchant vessel who, through negligence, allows a deserter from the Army, Navy or Air Force to be concealed on board the vessel shall be punished with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees.
simpleExplanation: The captain or person responsible for a merchant vessel may be fined if negligence allows a military deserter to hide on board.
punishment: Fine up to ₹500.
examples: A ship captain fails to conduct proper checks, allowing a deserter to remain hidden.
A vessel operator negligently overlooks the presence of a military deserter.

faqs.question: Is intention required?
faqs.answer: No. Negligence is sufficient under this section.

faqs.question: Who can be punished?
faqs.answer: The master or person in charge of the merchant vessel.

title: Abetment of Act of Insubordination by Soldier, Sailor or Airman
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets what he knows to be an act of insubordination by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India shall, if the act of insubordination is committed in consequence of the abetment, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Encouraging military personnel to disobey lawful orders or act insubordinately is punishable if the insubordination actually occurs.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A person encourages soldiers to ignore lawful orders from their commander.
An individual persuades military personnel to refuse assigned duties.

faqs.question: Must the insubordination actually occur?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be committed as a consequence of the abetment.

faqs.question: What is insubordination?
faqs.answer: Disobedience or defiance of lawful authority within the military.

title: Persons Subject to Certain Acts
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Section 139 has been repealed by subsequent legislation and is no longer in force.
simpleExplanation: This provision was repealed and no longer forms part of the operative provisions of the Indian Penal Code.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Courts do not apply repealed provisions.
Section 139 remains only for historical reference.

faqs.question: Is Section 139 currently enforceable?
faqs.answer: No. It has been repealed.

faqs.question: Why is the section number retained?
faqs.answer: To preserve the numbering structure of the IPC.

title: Wearing Garb or Carrying Token Used by Soldier, Sailor or Airman
chapter: Chapter VII - Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever, not being a soldier, sailor or airman in the Military, Naval or Air Service of the Government of India, wears any garb or carries any token resembling any garb or token used by such a soldier, sailor or airman, with the intention that it may be believed, or with knowledge that it is likely to be believed, that he is such a soldier, sailor or airman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Pretending to be a member of the armed forces by wearing military uniforms or insignia with intent to deceive is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: A civilian wears an Army uniform to falsely claim military status.
A person uses military insignia to gain public trust or benefits.

faqs.question: Is wearing military-style clothing always illegal?
faqs.answer: No. The offence requires an intention to deceive others into believing the person is military personnel.

faqs.question: What kinds of items are covered?
faqs.answer: Military uniforms, badges, insignia, and similar identifying tokens.

title: Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: An assembly of five or more persons is designated an unlawful assembly if the common object of the persons composing that assembly is to overawe the Government or public servant by criminal force, resist execution of law, commit an offence, take possession of property by criminal force, deprive a person of rights by criminal force, or compel a person to do what he is not legally bound to do or omit what he is legally entitled to do.
simpleExplanation: A group of five or more persons becomes an unlawful assembly when they gather with certain illegal common objectives.
punishment: Punishment is provided under subsequent sections.
examples: A group gathers to forcibly seize land from another person.
Five or more persons assemble to commit violence against public authorities.

faqs.question: How many people are required for an unlawful assembly?
faqs.answer: At least five persons.

faqs.question: Does every gathering of five people become unlawful?
faqs.answer: No. The group must share one of the unlawful common objects specified in the section.

title: Being Member of Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever, being aware of facts which render any assembly an unlawful assembly, intentionally joins that assembly, or continues in it, is said to be a member of an unlawful assembly.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly joins or remains in an unlawful assembly becomes a member of that unlawful assembly.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 143.
examples: A person joins a violent mob knowing its unlawful objective.
An individual remains with a crowd after becoming aware of its illegal purpose.

faqs.question: Is knowledge important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know facts that make the assembly unlawful.

faqs.question: Can remaining in the group create liability?
faqs.answer: Yes. Continuing in the assembly after learning its purpose is sufficient.

title: Punishment for Being Member of Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever is a member of an unlawful assembly shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who is a member of an unlawful assembly commits an offence and may be punished accordingly.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A person knowingly participates in a mob formed for an unlawful purpose.
An individual remains part of an unlawful assembly despite understanding its objectives.

faqs.question: Can mere membership be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Membership itself is an offence under this section.

faqs.question: Must violence occur for this section to apply?
faqs.answer: No. Membership in the unlawful assembly is sufficient.

title: Joining Unlawful Assembly Armed with Deadly Weapon
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever, being armed with any deadly weapon, or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, is a member of an unlawful assembly, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A member of an unlawful assembly who carries a deadly weapon commits a more serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person joins a violent mob carrying a sword.
An individual participates in an unlawful assembly while armed with a firearm.

faqs.question: Must the weapon actually be used?
faqs.answer: No. Mere membership in the unlawful assembly while armed is sufficient.

faqs.question: What is considered a deadly weapon?
faqs.answer: Any weapon likely to cause death when used offensively.

title: Joining or Continuing in Unlawful Assembly Knowing It Has Been Commanded to Disperse
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever joins or continues in an unlawful assembly, knowing that such unlawful assembly has been lawfully commanded to disperse, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by remaining in or joining an unlawful assembly after authorities have lawfully ordered it to disperse.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person ignores a police order directing an unlawful crowd to disperse.
An individual joins a mob after a lawful dispersal order has already been issued.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of the dispersal order required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know that the assembly has been ordered to disperse.

faqs.question: Who may issue the order?
faqs.answer: A competent authority acting under law.

title: Rioting
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, every member of such assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting.
simpleExplanation: Rioting occurs when force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly in pursuit of its common objective.
punishment: Punishment is provided under Section 147.
examples: A mob damages public property while pursuing its unlawful objective.
Members of an unlawful assembly attack individuals using force.

faqs.question: Must every member personally use violence?
faqs.answer: No. All members may be liable if violence is used in pursuit of the common object.

faqs.question: What is required for rioting?
faqs.answer: An unlawful assembly and the use of force or violence.

title: Punishment for Rioting
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever is guilty of rioting shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person guilty of rioting is liable for criminal punishment under this section.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Members of a violent mob damaging vehicles may be prosecuted for rioting.
A group using force during an unlawful assembly may be punished under this section.

faqs.question: Does rioting require actual violence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Force or violence must be used.

faqs.question: Can all members be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Members of the unlawful assembly may be liable.

title: Rioting, Armed with Deadly Weapon
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever is guilty of rioting, being armed with a deadly weapon or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Rioting becomes a more serious offence when the offender is armed with a deadly weapon.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A rioter carrying a firearm during a violent mob incident.
A person participates in a riot while armed with a deadly weapon.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher under this section?
faqs.answer: Because carrying a deadly weapon increases the danger posed by the riot.

faqs.question: Must the weapon be used?
faqs.answer: No. Being armed while rioting is sufficient.

title: Every Member of Unlawful Assembly Guilty of Offence Committed in Prosecution of Common Object
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly, is guilty of that offence.
simpleExplanation: Every member of an unlawful assembly may be held responsible for offences committed by any member if those offences further the assembly's common object or were likely consequences of it.
punishment: Same punishment as the offence committed.
examples: Members of a mob may all be liable for an assault committed by one member if it furthers the group's objective.
An unlawful assembly engaged in violence may make all members responsible for foreseeable offences.

faqs.question: Must every member personally commit the offence?
faqs.answer: No. Membership in the unlawful assembly may be sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is this section important?
faqs.answer: It creates vicarious liability for members of unlawful assemblies.

title: Hiring, Engaging or Employing Persons to Join Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever hires, engages or employs, or promotes or connives at the hiring, engagement or employment of any person to join or become a member of an unlawful assembly shall be punishable as a member of such unlawful assembly, and for any offence committed by such assembly in the same manner as if he had been a member thereof.
simpleExplanation: A person who recruits, hires, or pays others to join an unlawful assembly is treated as if they were a member of that assembly.
punishment: Same punishment as a member of the unlawful assembly and related offences.
examples: A person pays individuals to participate in a violent mob.
An organizer recruits people for an unlawful gathering intended to commit offences.

faqs.question: Can someone be liable without attending the assembly?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hiring or encouraging others to join is sufficient.

faqs.question: Is payment necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Promoting or facilitating such recruitment may also attract liability.

title: Knowingly Joining or Continuing in Assembly of Five or More Persons After It Has Been Commanded to Disperse
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever knowingly joins or continues in any assembly of five or more persons likely to cause disturbance of the public peace after it has been lawfully commanded to disperse shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who remains in a crowd likely to disturb public peace after a lawful dispersal order commits an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A crowd refuses to disperse despite repeated lawful police warnings.
A person joins a gathering after knowing it has already been ordered to disperse.

faqs.question: Must the assembly already be unlawful?
faqs.answer: No. It is enough that it is likely to disturb public peace and has been lawfully ordered to disperse.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of the order necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must knowingly join or remain.

title: Assaulting or Obstructing Public Servant When Suppressing Riot, etc.
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever assaults or threatens to assault, or obstructs or attempts to obstruct, any public servant engaged in suppressing a riot or unlawful assembly, or dispersing an assembly lawfully commanded to disperse, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: It is an offence to assault, threaten, or obstruct public servants while they are lawfully controlling riots or dispersing assemblies.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person attacks a police officer attempting to control a riot.
An individual blocks authorities from dispersing an unlawful assembly.

faqs.question: Does verbal threat count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Threatening assault is covered.

faqs.question: Who is protected by this section?
faqs.answer: Public servants engaged in lawful riot-control or dispersal duties.

title: Wantonly Giving Provocation with Intent to Cause Riot—If Rioting Be Committed; If Not Committed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
