officialText: Whoever malignantly, or wantonly, by doing anything which is illegal, gives provocation to any person intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause the offence of rioting to be committed, shall, if the offence of rioting is committed in consequence of such provocation, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both; and if the offence of rioting is not committed, with imprisonment which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who intentionally provokes others to riot may be punished whether or not the riot actually occurs.
punishment: If rioting occurs: imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both. If not: imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A person deliberately incites a crowd to attack another group.
An individual spreads inflammatory material likely to trigger a riot.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished even if no riot occurs?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section provides punishment even when the riot does not take place.

faqs.question: Is intention important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The provocation must be intentional or likely to cause rioting.

title: Promoting Enmity Between Different Groups on Grounds of Religion, Race, Place of Birth, Residence, Language, etc., and Doing Acts Prejudicial to Maintenance of Harmony
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever by words, either spoken or written, or by signs, or by visible representations, or otherwise, promotes or attempts to promote feelings of enmity, hatred or ill-will between different religious, racial, language or regional groups or castes or communities, or commits any act prejudicial to the maintenance of harmony, shall be punished as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes acts or speech that promote hatred, hostility, or disharmony between different communities or groups.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both; in certain circumstances, imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
examples: Publishing material intended to create hatred between religious communities.
Delivering speeches that encourage hostility against a particular linguistic group.

faqs.question: Does this section apply only to religious groups?
faqs.answer: No. It also covers racial, linguistic, regional, caste and community groups.

faqs.question: Can written publications be covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both spoken and written communications are included.

title: Imputations, Assertions Prejudicial to National Integration
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever by words, either spoken or written, or by signs, or by visible representations, or otherwise, makes or publishes any imputation or assertion that any class of persons cannot, by reason of their religion, race, language, caste or community, bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India or uphold the sovereignty and integrity of India, shall be punished as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: This section penalizes statements that undermine national integration by falsely alleging that certain groups cannot be loyal citizens of India.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both; in aggravated cases up to 5 years and fine.
examples: Publishing claims that a particular community cannot be loyal to India.
Making public statements questioning the allegiance of an entire group based solely on religion.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of this section?
faqs.answer: To protect national unity and prevent divisive assertions against groups.

faqs.question: Does it apply to public speeches?
faqs.answer: Yes. Spoken, written, and other forms of communication are covered.

title: Owner or Occupier of Land on Which an Unlawful Assembly Is Held
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever any unlawful assembly or riot takes place upon land or in any building, and the owner or occupier of such land or building, or any person having an interest therein or in the rents or profits thereof, knowingly omits to give the earliest notice thereof to the nearest police station or magistrate, he shall be punishable as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Property owners or occupiers who knowingly fail to report unlawful assemblies or riots occurring on their premises may be held criminally liable.
punishment: Fine or imprisonment as prescribed under the IPC.
examples: A landowner knowingly allows a riot to continue without informing authorities.
A building manager fails to report an unlawful assembly taking place on the premises.

faqs.question: Is knowledge necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The owner or occupier must knowingly omit to report the assembly.

faqs.question: Who must be informed?
faqs.answer: The nearest police station or magistrate.

title: Liability of Person for Whose Benefit Riot Is Committed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever a riot is committed for the benefit or on behalf of any person, that person shall be liable to punishment if he or his agent knew or had reason to believe that the riot was likely to be committed and failed to take lawful measures to prevent it.
simpleExplanation: A person who benefits from a riot may be held responsible if they knew or should have known about it and failed to try to stop it.
punishment: Fine and other penalties as prescribed by law.
examples: A property dispute beneficiary allows supporters to riot on his behalf.
A person gains from a riot and knowingly does nothing to prevent it.

faqs.question: Must the person directly participate in the riot?
faqs.answer: No. Liability may arise from benefiting from the riot and failing to prevent it.

faqs.question: What if the person had no knowledge?
faqs.answer: Knowledge or reason to believe is generally required.

title: Liability of Agent of Owner or Occupier for Whose Benefit Riot Is Committed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever a riot is committed for the benefit or on behalf of any owner or occupier of property, or any person interested therein, the agent or manager of such person shall be liable if, having reason to believe that a riot was likely to occur, he failed to use all lawful means within his power to prevent it.
simpleExplanation: Agents or managers who fail to take reasonable steps to prevent a riot committed for their employer's benefit may be punished.
punishment: Fine and other penalties as provided by law.
examples: A property manager knows supporters plan a riot and takes no preventive action.
An estate agent ignores warnings about a planned violent disturbance.

faqs.question: Who can be liable under this section?
faqs.answer: Agents, managers, or persons acting on behalf of the beneficiary.

faqs.question: What is required for liability?
faqs.answer: Knowledge or reason to believe and failure to take lawful preventive measures.

title: Harbouring Persons Hired for an Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever harbours, receives, or assembles persons knowing that they have been hired, engaged, or employed to join or become members of an unlawful assembly shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Providing shelter or assistance to people hired to participate in an unlawful assembly is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A person knowingly provides accommodation to hired members of a violent mob.
An organizer arranges lodging for individuals recruited for an unlawful assembly.

faqs.question: Must the person know the purpose of the group?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge that they were hired for an unlawful assembly is required.

faqs.question: Is temporary shelter sufficient?
faqs.answer: Yes. Harbouring in any form may attract liability.

title: Being Hired to Take Part in an Unlawful Assembly or Riot
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever is engaged or hired, or offers or attempts to be engaged or hired, to join or become a member of an unlawful assembly, or to commit or assist in committing any act of riot, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both; and whoever, being so engaged or hired, joins such assembly or commits such act shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: It is an offence both to accept payment to join an unlawful assembly and to actually participate in it after being hired.
punishment: Up to 6 months, or fine, or both; up to 2 years, or fine, or both, if participation actually occurs.
examples: A person accepts money to participate in a violent protest.
An individual joins a riot after being paid by organizers.

faqs.question: Is merely agreeing to be hired punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even offering or attempting to be hired is covered.

faqs.question: Is punishment higher if the person actually joins?
faqs.answer: Yes. Participation attracts enhanced punishment.

title: Affray
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: When two or more persons, by fighting in a public place, disturb the public peace, they are said to commit an affray.
simpleExplanation: An affray occurs when two or more people fight in a public place and their conduct disturbs public peace.
punishment: Punishment is provided under Section 160.
examples: Two groups engage in a street fight in a marketplace.
Individuals fighting in a public park cause panic among bystanders.

faqs.question: Does a private fight amount to affray?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The fight must occur in a public place.

faqs.question: How many persons are required?
faqs.answer: At least two persons.

title: Punishment for Committing Affray
chapter: Chapter VIII - Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever commits an affray shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to one hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who participates in a public fight that disturbs public peace may be punished under this section.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹100, or both.
examples: Two individuals engage in a public fight on a busy street.
Participants in a public brawl are prosecuted for affray.

faqs.question: Must public peace be disturbed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Disturbance of public peace is an essential element.

faqs.question: Is affray a serious offence?
faqs.answer: It is generally considered less serious than rioting.

title: Public Servant Taking Gratification Other Than Legal Remuneration in Respect of an Official Act
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Section 161 has been repealed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
simpleExplanation: This provision formerly dealt with bribery by public servants but has been replaced by provisions under the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
punishment: Not applicable under IPC; governed by the Prevention of Corruption Act.
examples: Bribery offences by public servants are now prosecuted under the Prevention of Corruption Act.
Courts no longer apply IPC Section 161.

faqs.question: Is Section 161 still in force?
faqs.answer: No. It has been repealed.

faqs.question: Which law now governs such offences?
faqs.answer: The Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.

correspondingSection: Covered under Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988

title: Taking Gratification in Order, by Corrupt or Illegal Means, to Influence Public Servant
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Section 162 has been repealed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
simpleExplanation: This section previously punished persons accepting gratification to improperly influence public servants. The subject is now governed by anti-corruption legislation.
punishment: Not applicable under IPC; governed by the Prevention of Corruption Act.
examples: Accepting money to influence a government official is now prosecuted under anti-corruption laws.
Section 162 is retained only for historical reference.

faqs.question: Why was this section repealed?
faqs.answer: Its subject matter was consolidated into the Prevention of Corruption Act.

faqs.question: Can a person still be prosecuted for such conduct?
faqs.answer: Yes, under the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.

correspondingSection: Covered under Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988

title: Taking Gratification for Exercise of Personal Influence with Public Servant
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Section 163 has been repealed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
simpleExplanation: This section formerly punished persons who accepted gratification in return for using personal influence over public servants. Such matters are now governed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
punishment: Not applicable under IPC; governed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
examples: Accepting money to influence a government official is now prosecuted under anti-corruption laws.
Section 163 remains only as a historical reference.

faqs.question: Is Section 163 still enforceable?
faqs.answer: No. It has been repealed.

faqs.question: Which law now covers such conduct?
faqs.answer: The Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.

correspondingSection: Covered under Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988

title: Abetment by Public Servant of Offences Defined in Sections 162 or 163
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Section 164 has been repealed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
simpleExplanation: This provision previously dealt with public servants abetting corruption-related offences. It is now covered by anti-corruption legislation.
punishment: Not applicable under IPC; governed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
examples: A public servant assisting bribery-related conduct is now prosecuted under the Prevention of Corruption Act.
The IPC no longer contains this offence.

faqs.question: Why was this section repealed?
faqs.answer: The Prevention of Corruption Act consolidated corruption-related offences.

faqs.question: Can such conduct still be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes, under the applicable anti-corruption laws.

correspondingSection: Covered under Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988

title: Public Servant Obtaining Valuable Thing Without Consideration from Person Concerned in Proceeding or Business Transacted by Such Public Servant
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Section 165 has been repealed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
simpleExplanation: This section formerly prohibited public servants from obtaining valuable items without adequate consideration from persons connected with official matters handled by them.
punishment: Not applicable under IPC; governed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
examples: Receiving gifts from persons with pending official matters is now addressed under anti-corruption laws.
Section 165 is no longer operative.

faqs.question: Does this section still apply?
faqs.answer: No. It was repealed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.

faqs.question: What law now governs such misconduct?
faqs.answer: The Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.

correspondingSection: Covered under Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988

title: Punishment for Abetment of Offences Defined in Sections 161 and 165
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Section 165A has been repealed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
simpleExplanation: This section previously punished the abetment of certain corruption offences. It has been superseded by the Prevention of Corruption Act.
punishment: Not applicable under IPC; governed by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
examples: Offering a bribe is now punishable under anti-corruption laws rather than IPC Section 165A.
The section remains only for historical reference.

faqs.question: Is bribery abetment still punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes, under the Prevention of Corruption Act.

faqs.question: Why is Section 165A no longer used?
faqs.answer: Because it was repealed when anti-corruption laws were consolidated.

correspondingSection: Covered under Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988

title: Public Servant Disobeying Law, with Intent to Cause Injury to Any Person
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, knowingly disobeys any direction of the law as to the way in which he is to conduct himself as such public servant, intending thereby, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby, cause injury to any person, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A public servant commits an offence by deliberately violating legal duties in a way that causes or is likely to cause harm to another person.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: A government official intentionally ignores mandatory legal procedures to harm a citizen.
A public servant abuses authority by knowingly acting contrary to legal requirements.

faqs.question: Is mere mistake sufficient for liability?
faqs.answer: No. The disobedience must be knowing and intentional.

faqs.question: Must injury occur?
faqs.answer: The public servant must intend injury or know that injury is likely.

title: Public Servant Framing Incorrect Document with Intent to Cause Injury
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, and being charged with the preparation or translation of any document or electronic record, frames, prepares or translates that document or electronic record in a manner which he knows or believes to be incorrect, intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he may thereby cause, injury to any person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A public servant commits an offence by intentionally creating or altering official records incorrectly to cause harm to someone.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A government officer deliberately records false information in an official document.
A public servant intentionally mistranslates a record to harm a citizen.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes. It expressly covers electronic records.

faqs.question: Is intentional wrongdoing required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The public servant must know the document is incorrect.

title: Public Servant Unlawfully Engaging in Trade
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, and being legally bound as such public servant not to engage in trade, engages in trade, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who is prohibited by law from carrying on business or trade commits an offence if they engage in such activities.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: A government officer operates a private business despite rules prohibiting it.
A public servant secretly runs a commercial enterprise while in office.

faqs.question: Does this apply to all public servants?
faqs.answer: It applies only where the law or service rules prohibit engaging in trade.

faqs.question: Why is such conduct restricted?
faqs.answer: To prevent conflicts of interest and maintain public trust.

title: Public Servant Unlawfully Buying or Bidding for Property
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, and being legally bound as such public servant not to purchase or bid for certain property, purchases or bids for that property, either in his own name or in the name of another, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both; and the property, if purchased, shall be liable to confiscation.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who unlawfully purchases or bids for property that they are prohibited from dealing with commits an offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both; property may be confiscated.
examples: A revenue officer secretly purchases property involved in official proceedings.
A public servant uses another person's name to bid for restricted property.

faqs.question: Can the property be confiscated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Property purchased in violation of this section may be confiscated.

faqs.question: Does using another person's name avoid liability?
faqs.answer: No. The section expressly covers purchases through another person.

title: Personating a Public Servant
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever pretends to hold any particular office as a public servant, knowing that he does not hold such office, or falsely personates any other person holding such office, and in such assumed character does or attempts to do any act under colour of such office, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Pretending to be a public servant and acting under that false identity is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person falsely claims to be a police officer and conducts checks.
An individual impersonates a government official to obtain information.

faqs.question: Is merely claiming to be a public servant enough?
faqs.answer: The person must also perform or attempt to perform an act under the assumed office.

faqs.question: Why is this offence punishable?
faqs.answer: Because it undermines public trust and can facilitate fraud.

title: Wearing Garb or Carrying Token Used by Public Servant with Fraudulent Intent
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences by or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, not belonging to a certain class of public servants, wears any garb or carries any token resembling that used by such class of public servants, with the intention that it may be believed, or with knowledge that it is likely to be believed, that he belongs to that class of public servants, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Using official uniforms, badges, or symbols to falsely appear as a public servant is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹200, or both.
examples: A civilian wears a police uniform to gain access to restricted areas.
A person displays official insignia to create a false impression of authority.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to deceive?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must intend or know that others are likely to be misled.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to uniforms?
faqs.answer: No. It also covers badges, tokens, and other official identifiers.

title: Definitions Relating to Elections
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: In this Chapter, 'candidate' means a person who has been nominated as a candidate at any election; and 'electoral right' means the right of a person to stand or not to stand as, or to withdraw or not withdraw from being, a candidate, or to vote or refrain from voting at an election.
simpleExplanation: This section defines key terms used in election-related offences, including 'candidate' and 'electoral right'.
punishment: Not applicable. This is a definition section.
examples: A person nominated for an election becomes a candidate under this chapter.
The right to vote or abstain from voting is an electoral right.

faqs.question: What is an electoral right?
faqs.answer: It includes rights relating to contesting, withdrawing from, or voting in an election.

faqs.question: Does this section create an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It only provides definitions.

title: Bribery
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever gives a gratification to any person with the object of inducing him or any other person to exercise any electoral right, or rewarding any person for having exercised such right, commits the offence of bribery. A person who accepts such gratification also commits bribery.
simpleExplanation: Offering, giving, receiving, or accepting money, gifts, or benefits to influence voting or election-related decisions is bribery.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 171E.
examples: A candidate distributes cash to voters in exchange for votes.
A voter accepts money to vote for a particular candidate.

faqs.question: Does bribery include non-cash benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any gratification intended to influence electoral rights may qualify.

faqs.question: Can a voter be punished for accepting a bribe?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both giving and receiving electoral bribes are offences.

title: Undue Influence at Elections
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever voluntarily interferes or attempts to interfere with the free exercise of any electoral right commits the offence of undue influence at an election.
simpleExplanation: A person commits undue influence by improperly pressuring, threatening, or interfering with someone’s freedom to vote or participate in an election.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 171F.
examples: Threatening a voter to support a particular candidate.
Using intimidation to prevent someone from voting.

faqs.question: What is undue influence in elections?
faqs.answer: Improper interference with a person's freedom to exercise electoral rights.

faqs.question: Does intimidation qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. Threats and coercion are common forms of undue influence.

title: Personation at Elections
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever applies for a voting paper or votes in the name of another person, whether living, dead or fictitious, or votes more than once in the same election, commits the offence of personation at an election.
simpleExplanation: Voting in someone else's name, using a fake identity, or voting multiple times in the same election is an election offence.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 171F.
examples: A person casts a vote using another voter's identity.
An individual votes twice in the same election.

faqs.question: Is voting in a deceased person's name an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. It amounts to personation.

faqs.question: Can voting more than once be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Multiple voting in the same election is covered.

title: Punishment for Bribery
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever commits the offence of bribery shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both. However, bribery by treating shall be punishable with fine only.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes punishment for election bribery, including offering or accepting benefits to influence voting.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both; in cases of treating, fine only.
examples: A candidate pays voters for electoral support.
A voter accepts money in exchange for voting for a candidate.

faqs.question: What is the punishment for election bribery?
faqs.answer: Up to one year imprisonment, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: What is treating?
faqs.answer: Providing food, drink, entertainment, or similar benefits to influence voters.

title: Punishment for Undue Influence or Personation at an Election
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever commits the offence of undue influence or personation at an election shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who intimidates voters or commits voter impersonation may be punished under this section.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening voters to influence election outcomes.
Casting a vote using another person's identity.

faqs.question: Does this section cover both undue influence and personation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both offences are punishable under this section.

faqs.question: Can imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court may impose imprisonment, fine, or both.

title: False Statement in Connection with an Election
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever, with intent to affect the result of an election, makes or publishes any statement purporting to be a statement of fact which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, in relation to the personal character or conduct of any candidate, shall be punished with fine.
simpleExplanation: Publishing or spreading false statements about a candidate's character or conduct to influence an election is an offence.
punishment: Fine.
examples: A person circulates a knowingly false allegation about a candidate before an election.
Publishing fabricated misconduct claims to damage a candidate's reputation.

faqs.question: Must the statement be false?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know it is false or not believe it to be true.

faqs.question: Does the statement need to affect the election?
faqs.answer: It must be made with the intent to affect the election result.

title: Illegal Payments in Connection with an Election
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever without the general or special authority in writing of a candidate incurs or authorizes expenses on account of the holding of any public meeting, or upon any advertisement, circular or publication, or in any other way whatsoever for the purpose of promoting or procuring the election of such candidate, shall be punished with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees.
simpleExplanation: Spending money to support a candidate's election campaign without proper written authorization from the candidate is an offence.
punishment: Fine up to ₹500.
examples: A supporter independently funds campaign advertisements without authorization.
A person organizes election rallies and incurs expenses without the candidate's approval.

faqs.question: Can supporters spend money freely for a candidate?
faqs.answer: Not without the candidate's written authorization where required by law.

faqs.question: What kinds of expenses are covered?
faqs.answer: Meetings, advertisements, publications, and other election-related expenses.

title: Failure to Keep Election Accounts
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever, being required by any law for the time being in force or any rule having the force of law to keep accounts of expenses incurred at or in connection with an election, fails to keep such accounts, shall be punished with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees.
simpleExplanation: Candidates or other persons required by law to maintain election expense records commit an offence if they fail to keep those accounts.
punishment: Fine up to ₹500.
examples: A candidate fails to maintain records of campaign expenditures.
Election-related expenses are incurred but not properly documented.

faqs.question: Why are election accounts important?
faqs.answer: They help ensure transparency and accountability in elections.

faqs.question: What happens if accounts are not maintained?
faqs.answer: The person may be fined under this section.

title: Absconding to Avoid Service of Summons or Other Proceeding
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever absconds in order to avoid being served with a summons, notice, order or proclamation proceeding from any public servant legally competent to issue the same shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both; and where the summons, notice, order or proclamation is to attend in person or by agent, or to produce a document, the punishment may extend to six months, or fine up to one thousand rupees, or both.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by hiding or fleeing to avoid receiving official legal notices, summons, or orders.
punishment: Up to 1 month imprisonment or ₹500 fine, or both; in aggravated cases up to 6 months imprisonment or ₹1,000 fine, or both.
examples: A witness deliberately leaves town to avoid receiving a court summons.
A person hides to avoid being served with an official order.

faqs.question: Does intentionally avoiding a summons amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Absconding to avoid service is punishable.

faqs.question: Is punishment higher when personal attendance is required?
faqs.answer: Yes. Enhanced punishment applies in such cases.

title: Preventing Service of Summons or Other Proceeding, or Preventing Publication Thereof
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever in any manner intentionally prevents the serving on himself or any other person of any summons, notice, order or proclamation proceeding from any public servant legally competent to issue the same, or intentionally prevents the lawful affixing or publication thereof, shall be punished as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally interfering with the service or publication of legal notices, summons, or orders is an offence.
punishment: Up to 1 month imprisonment or ₹500 fine, or both; in aggravated cases up to 6 months imprisonment or ₹1,000 fine, or both.
examples: A person tears down a legally affixed court notice.
Someone prevents a process server from delivering a summons.

faqs.question: Can interfering with service on another person be an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies to interference affecting any person.

faqs.question: Does removing a public notice attract liability?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preventing lawful publication is punishable.

title: Non-Attendance in Obedience to an Order from Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to attend in person or by an agent at a certain place and time in obedience to a summons, notice, order or proclamation issued by a public servant legally competent to issue the same, intentionally omits to attend, shall be punished as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: Failing to appear when legally required by a lawful order, notice, or summons from a public servant is an offence.
punishment: Up to 1 month imprisonment or ₹500 fine, or both; up to 6 months imprisonment or ₹1,000 fine, or both where attendance before a court is required.
examples: A witness ignores a lawful summons to appear before a court.
A person deliberately fails to attend an official inquiry.

faqs.question: Must the absence be intentional?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intentional omission is required.

faqs.question: Does this apply to court summons?
faqs.answer: Yes. Court attendance orders are covered.

title: Omission to Produce Document or Electronic Record to Public Servant by Person Legally Bound to Produce It
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to produce or deliver up any document or electronic record to any public servant, intentionally omits to do so, shall be punished as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to provide documents or electronic records to a public servant commits an offence by intentionally failing to do so.
punishment: Up to 1 month imprisonment or ₹500 fine, or both; up to 6 months imprisonment or ₹1,000 fine, or both in certain cases.
examples: A person refuses to provide financial records required by law.
An organization ignores a lawful order to produce electronic records.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes. It expressly includes electronic records.

faqs.question: Must the omission be intentional?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intentional failure is required.

title: Omission to Give Notice or Information to Public Servant by Person Legally Bound to Give It
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to give any notice or furnish information on any subject to any public servant, intentionally omits to give such notice or furnish such information in the manner and at the time required by law, shall be punished as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to provide information to a public servant commits an offence by intentionally failing to do so.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 month or fine up to ₹500, or both; in certain cases imprisonment up to 6 months or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A person fails to report information that the law requires them to disclose.
An official custodian intentionally withholds mandatory information from authorities.

faqs.question: Does accidental failure amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: No. The omission must be intentional.

faqs.question: Why is this section important?
faqs.answer: It helps public authorities obtain information necessary for lawful functions.

title: Furnishing False Information
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to furnish information on any subject to any public servant, furnishes, as true, information on the subject which he knows or has reason to believe to be false, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both. If the information relates to the commission of an offence or is required for preventing an offence, the punishment may extend to two years imprisonment, or fine, or both.
simpleExplanation: Giving false information to a public servant when legally required to provide truthful information is an offence.
punishment: Up to 6 months imprisonment or ₹1,000 fine, or both; up to 2 years imprisonment in serious cases.
examples: A person knowingly gives false details during an official investigation.
Someone provides incorrect information to prevent police from detecting a crime.

faqs.question: Must the person know the information is false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe the information is false is required.

faqs.question: Is punishment higher when criminal investigations are involved?
faqs.answer: Yes. Enhanced punishment applies.

title: Refusing Oath or Affirmation When Duly Required by Public Servant to Make It
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever refuses to bind himself by an oath or affirmation to state the truth when required by a public servant legally competent to require such oath or affirmation shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by refusing to take a lawful oath or affirmation when required to do so by a competent public servant.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A witness refuses to take an oath before giving evidence.
A person declines to affirm the truth of a statement when lawfully required.

faqs.question: Can a person refuse an oath without consequence?
faqs.answer: No. Refusal may amount to an offence under this section.

faqs.question: Does affirmation count as an alternative to oath?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section covers both.

title: Refusing to Answer Public Servant Authorized to Question
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to state the truth on any subject to any public servant, refuses to answer any question demanded of him by such public servant in the exercise of his lawful powers, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Refusing to answer lawful questions from a public servant when legally obligated to do so is an offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A witness refuses to answer relevant questions during an official inquiry.
A person declines to provide legally required information to an investigating authority.

faqs.question: Must the public servant be authorized to ask the question?
faqs.answer: Yes. The questioning must be within lawful authority.

faqs.question: Can refusal alone be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Refusal to answer is itself an offence.

title: Refusing to Sign Statement
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever refuses to sign any statement made by him when required by a public servant legally competent to require that signature shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who refuses to sign a statement after lawfully making it before a competent public servant commits an offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: A witness refuses to sign a statement recorded by an authorized officer.
A person denies signing a declaration they have already provided.

faqs.question: Must the statement have been made by the same person?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies to statements made by that person.

faqs.question: Who can require the signature?
faqs.answer: A legally competent public servant.

title: False Statement on Oath or Affirmation to Public Servant or Person Authorized to Administer Oath or Affirmation
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound by an oath or affirmation to state the truth on any subject to any public servant or other person authorized by law to administer such oath or affirmation, makes a statement which is false and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Making a false statement after taking a lawful oath or affirmation is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A witness knowingly gives false evidence under oath.
A person intentionally makes a false sworn declaration.

faqs.question: Must the statement be knowingly false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or belief in its falsity is required.

faqs.question: Is this related to perjury?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is closely related to offences involving false sworn statements.

title: False Information, with Intent to Cause Public Servant to Use His Lawful Power to the Injury of Another Person
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever gives to any public servant any information which he knows or believes to be false, intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, such public servant to do or omit anything which such public servant ought not to do or omit if the true state of facts were known, or to use the lawful power of such public servant to the injury or annoyance of any person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Providing false information to a public servant in order to cause harm, trouble, or wrongful action against another person is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Filing a knowingly false complaint to get someone arrested.
Giving false information to authorities to harass a rival.

faqs.question: Is a mistaken statement punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The information must be knowingly false.

faqs.question: Must the public servant actually act on the information?
faqs.answer: The offence focuses on the intention or likelihood of causing such action.

title: Resistance to the Taking of Property by the Lawful Authority of a Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever offers any resistance to the taking of any property by the lawful authority of any public servant, knowing or having reason to believe that he is such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by unlawfully resisting a public servant who is legally authorized to seize or take property.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A person forcibly prevents an authorized seizure of property.
An individual obstructs officials executing a lawful attachment order.

faqs.question: Must the seizure be lawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. The public servant must be acting under lawful authority.

faqs.question: Does the accused need to know the person's status?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

title: Obstructing Sale of Property Offered for Sale by Authority of Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever intentionally obstructs any sale of property held by the lawful authority of any public servant, as such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally interfering with a lawful auction or sale conducted by a public servant is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: Disrupting a court-ordered auction of property.
Preventing bidders from participating in a lawful government sale.

faqs.question: Must the sale be conducted lawfully?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies only to lawful sales.

faqs.question: Can verbal interference amount to obstruction?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it intentionally obstructs the sale.

title: Illegal Purchase or Bidding for Property Offered for Sale by Authority of Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, at any sale referred to in Section 184, purchases or bids for any property on behalf of a person whom he knows to be under a legal incapacity to purchase that property at that sale, or purchases such property himself when under such incapacity, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees, or with both; and the property purchased may be confiscated.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by bidding or purchasing property at a public sale despite being legally prohibited from doing so.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹200, or both; property may be confiscated.
examples: A person bids on auctioned property despite being legally disqualified.
An individual purchases property on behalf of a prohibited bidder.

faqs.question: Can the purchased property be confiscated?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law permits confiscation.

faqs.question: Must the buyer know about the disqualification?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of the incapacity is required.

title: Obstructing Public Servant in Discharge of Public Functions
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by intentionally interfering with a public servant performing official duties.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: A person blocks a government officer conducting an official inspection.
An individual interferes with police officers carrying out lawful duties.

faqs.question: Must the obstruction be intentional?
faqs.answer: Yes. Voluntary obstruction is required.

faqs.question: Does physical force need to be used?
faqs.answer: No. Any voluntary obstruction may be sufficient.

title: Omission to Assist Public Servant When Bound by Law to Give Assistance
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being bound by law to render or furnish assistance to any public servant in the execution of his public duty, intentionally omits to give such assistance, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees, or with both; and where such assistance is required for preventing or suppressing a breach of the peace, or for apprehending an offender, the punishment may extend to six months, or fine up to five hundred rupees, or both.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to assist a public servant and intentionally refuses to do so commits an offence.
punishment: Up to 1 month imprisonment or ₹200 fine, or both; up to 6 months imprisonment or ₹500 fine, or both in serious cases.
examples: A person refuses to assist police in apprehending an offender when legally required.
A citizen intentionally ignores a lawful request for assistance during a public disturbance.

faqs.question: Must there be a legal duty to assist?
faqs.answer: Yes. The obligation must arise under law.

faqs.question: Is punishment higher in public safety situations?
faqs.answer: Yes. Enhanced punishment applies in such cases.

title: Disobedience to Order Duly Promulgated by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, knowing that an order promulgated by a public servant lawfully empowered to promulgate such order, directs him to abstain from a certain act or to take certain order with certain property in his possession or under his management, disobeys such direction, shall be punished as provided in this section. If such disobedience causes or tends to cause obstruction, annoyance, injury, or risk thereof, punishment may extend to one month imprisonment or fine up to two hundred rupees. If it causes danger to human life, health, safety, riot, or affray, punishment may extend to six months imprisonment or fine up to one thousand rupees, or both.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly violating a lawful order issued by a public servant is an offence, especially when it endangers public safety or order.
punishment: Up to 1 month imprisonment or ₹200 fine; up to 6 months imprisonment or ₹1,000 fine, or both, in aggravated cases.
examples: Ignoring a lawful prohibitory order issued during an emergency.
Violating restrictions imposed to prevent a public disturbance.

faqs.question: Must the person know about the order?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of the order is required.

faqs.question: Does actual harm need to occur?
faqs.answer: No. It is sufficient if the disobedience tends to cause harm or danger.

title: Threat of Injury to Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever holds out any threat of injury to any public servant, or to any person in whom that public servant is interested, for the purpose of inducing that public servant to do any act connected with the exercise of his public functions, or to forbear or delay to do any act connected with such functions, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Threatening a public servant or someone connected to them in order to influence official actions is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening a government officer to obtain a favorable decision.
Intimidating a public servant's family member to influence official conduct.

faqs.question: Must the threat relate to official duties?
faqs.answer: Yes. The threat must be intended to influence public functions.

faqs.question: Can threats against family members be covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section includes persons in whom the public servant is interested.

title: Threat of Injury to Induce Person to Refrain from Applying for Protection to Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever holds out any threat of injury to any person for the purpose of inducing that person to refrain from making a lawful application for protection to any public servant, or from instituting or causing to be instituted any proceeding against any person, or from giving evidence in any matter, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Threatening someone to stop them from seeking legal protection, filing a complaint, or giving evidence is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening a victim to prevent them from reporting a crime.
Intimidating a witness to stop them from testifying.

faqs.question: Does this section protect witnesses?
faqs.answer: Yes. It covers threats intended to prevent giving evidence.

faqs.question: Can threats to stop complaints be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Such conduct is expressly covered.

title: Giving False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound by an oath, affirmation, or by law to state the truth, or being bound by law to make a declaration upon any subject, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, is said to give false evidence.
simpleExplanation: A person gives false evidence when they knowingly make a false statement while under a legal duty to tell the truth.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 193.
examples: A witness knowingly lies under oath in court.
A person files a false sworn declaration.

faqs.question: Must the statement be knowingly false?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know or believe it to be false.

faqs.question: Does this section itself prescribe punishment?
faqs.answer: No. Punishment is provided in Section 193.

title: Fabricating False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever causes any circumstance to exist, or makes any false entry in any book or record, or electronic record, or makes any document or electronic record containing a false statement, intending that such circumstance, entry, or statement may appear in evidence in a judicial proceeding or before a public servant and cause an erroneous opinion on a material point, is said to fabricate false evidence.
simpleExplanation: A person fabricates false evidence by creating false records, documents, electronic records, or circumstances intended to mislead a court or public authority.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 193.
examples: Creating fake invoices to influence a court case.
Altering electronic records to support a false claim.

faqs.question: Does this section cover electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic records are expressly included.

faqs.question: Must the evidence be intended for use in proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. The fabrication must be intended to influence a proceeding or official decision.

title: Punishment for False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever intentionally gives false evidence in any stage of a judicial proceeding, or fabricates false evidence for the purpose of being used in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. In other cases, the punishment may extend to three years and fine.
simpleExplanation: Giving false evidence or creating false evidence for use in legal proceedings is a serious offence.
punishment: Up to 7 years imprisonment and fine in judicial proceedings; otherwise up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A witness lies under oath during a trial.
A person fabricates documents to mislead a court.

faqs.question: Is punishment higher for court proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. Judicial proceedings attract more severe punishment.

faqs.question: Can fabricating evidence be punished even if not used?
faqs.answer: Yes, if created for use in a judicial proceeding.

title: Giving or Fabricating False Evidence with Intent to Procure Conviction of Capital Offence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, any person to be convicted of an offence punishable with death shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to ten years, and fine. If an innocent person is executed as a result, the offender may be punished with death or the punishment prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: Creating or giving false evidence to secure the conviction of someone for a capital offence is an extremely serious crime.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; in certain cases, death penalty may apply.
examples: Fabricating evidence to falsely implicate a person in a murder case.
Giving false testimony to secure a death sentence against an innocent person.

faqs.question: Why is punishment so severe?
faqs.answer: Because the offence may lead to wrongful conviction and execution.

faqs.question: Can the death penalty apply?
faqs.answer: Yes, in circumstances specified by law.

title: Giving or Fabricating False Evidence with Intent to Procure Conviction of Offence Punishable with Imprisonment for Life or Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, any person to be convicted of an offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment shall be punished as if he had committed that offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who fabricates or gives false evidence to secure another person's conviction may be punished as if they themselves committed the offence falsely alleged.
punishment: Punishment equivalent to the offence for which the false conviction was sought.
examples: Fabricating evidence to falsely convict someone of robbery.
Giving false testimony to secure imprisonment of an innocent person.

faqs.question: How is punishment determined?
faqs.answer: The offender may receive the same punishment as the offence involved.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to cause conviction?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent or knowledge is essential.

title: Using Evidence Known to Be False
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true or genuine evidence any evidence which he knows to be false or fabricated shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave or fabricated false evidence.
simpleExplanation: Using evidence that a person knows to be false is treated as seriously as creating or giving false evidence.
punishment: Same punishment as for giving or fabricating false evidence.
examples: Submitting forged documents in court while knowing they are fake.
Presenting fabricated electronic records as genuine evidence.

faqs.question: Must the person know the evidence is false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is a key requirement.

faqs.question: Is using false evidence as serious as creating it?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law treats both similarly.

title: Issuing or Signing False Certificate
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever issues or signs any certificate required by law to be given or signed, relating to any fact of which such certificate is by law admissible in evidence, knowing or believing that such certificate is false in any material point, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by knowingly issuing or signing a false certificate that may be used as legal evidence.
punishment: Same punishment as giving false evidence under Section 193.
examples: A doctor knowingly issues a false medical certificate.
An authority signs a certificate containing false information.

faqs.question: Must the certificate be legally admissible as evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies to certificates recognized by law as evidence.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of falsity required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know or believe the certificate is false.

title: Using as True a Certificate Known to Be False
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true any certificate knowing the same to be false in any material point shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
simpleExplanation: Using a certificate that a person knows is false is treated as seriously as giving false evidence.
punishment: Same punishment as giving false evidence under Section 193.
examples: Submitting a fake medical certificate in court.
Using a forged educational certificate to obtain benefits.

faqs.question: Must the person know the certificate is false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of the falsity is essential.

faqs.question: Does attempted use also count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempting to use the false certificate is covered.

title: False Statement Made in Declaration Which Is by Law Receivable as Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, in any declaration made or subscribed by him, which declaration any court of justice or public servant is bound or authorized by law to receive as evidence, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, touching any point material to the object for which the declaration is made or used, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
simpleExplanation: Making a knowingly false statement in a declaration that may be accepted as evidence is an offence.
punishment: Same punishment as giving false evidence under Section 193.
examples: Submitting a false affidavit to a government authority.
Making a false declaration in support of a legal application.

faqs.question: Does the false statement need to be material?
faqs.answer: Yes. It must relate to a material point.

faqs.question: Are affidavits covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Affidavits are common examples of such declarations.

title: Using as True Such Declaration Knowing It to Be False
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true any such declaration knowing the same to be false in any material point shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
simpleExplanation: Using a declaration that is known to be false is treated as seriously as making the false declaration itself.
punishment: Same punishment as giving false evidence under Section 193.
examples: Submitting a false affidavit while knowing it contains false information.
Using a fabricated declaration in support of a legal claim.

faqs.question: Must the person know the declaration is false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is a necessary element.

faqs.question: Can attempted use be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempts are also covered.

title: Causing Disappearance of Evidence of Offence, or Giving False Information to Screen Offender
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, causes any evidence of the commission of that offence to disappear, or gives any information respecting the offence which he knows or believes to be false, with the intention of screening the offender from legal punishment, shall be punished according to the gravity of the offence involved.
simpleExplanation: Destroying evidence or giving false information to help an offender avoid punishment is a criminal offence.
punishment: Varies according to the seriousness of the underlying offence; may extend to life imprisonment-related offences in severe cases.
examples: Destroying CCTV footage to protect a criminal.
Providing false information to police to hide an offender's involvement.

faqs.question: Must the person know an offence was committed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Does destroying evidence amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Causing evidence to disappear is expressly covered.

title: Intentional Omission to Give Information of Offence by Person Bound to Inform
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, intentionally omits to give any information respecting that offence which he is legally bound to give, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to report an offence commits an offence by intentionally failing to provide that information.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A person legally required to report a crime deliberately withholds information.
An official fails to notify authorities about a known offence despite a legal duty.

faqs.question: Must there be a legal duty to report?
faqs.answer: Yes. Liability arises only when the law requires disclosure.

faqs.question: Is mere ignorance punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The omission must be intentional and based on knowledge or reason to believe.

title: Giving False Information Respecting an Offence Committed
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, gives any information respecting that offence which he knows or believes to be false, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Giving false information about a crime to authorities is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person knowingly provides a false account of a robbery.
An individual falsely identifies an innocent person as the offender.

faqs.question: Must the information relate to a crime?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section concerns information about an offence.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of falsity required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know or believe the information is false.

title: Destruction of Document or Electronic Record to Prevent Its Production as Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever secretes or destroys any document or electronic record which he may be lawfully compelled to produce as evidence in a court of justice, or intentionally renders it illegible or unavailable, with the intention of preventing its production or use as evidence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Destroying, hiding, or altering documents or electronic records to stop them from being used as evidence is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Deleting emails relevant to a court case.
Destroying contracts to prevent their use in litigation.

faqs.question: Does this section cover electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic records are expressly included.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to prevent use as evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent is an essential requirement.

title: False Personation for Purpose of Act or Proceeding in Suit or Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever falsely personates another, and in such assumed character makes any admission or statement, or causes any process to be issued, or becomes bail or security, or does any other act in any suit or criminal prosecution, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Pretending to be another person in a legal proceeding is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person appears in court pretending to be someone else.
An individual falsely signs legal documents in another person's name.

faqs.question: Does this apply to both civil and criminal cases?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies to suits and criminal prosecutions.

faqs.question: Must the impersonation occur in connection with legal proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically relates to judicial proceedings.

title: Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property to Prevent Its Seizure as Forfeited or in Execution
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently removes, conceals, transfers or delivers any property, intending thereby to prevent that property from being taken as forfeited, or in execution of a decree or order which has been made or which he knows is likely to be made by a court or other competent authority, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Hiding or transferring property to avoid court seizure, forfeiture, or execution of a legal order is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A debtor secretly transfers assets before a court attachment order.
A person hides property to prevent lawful seizure.

faqs.question: Must there be fraudulent intent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulent intent is required.

faqs.question: Can transferring property to relatives be covered?
faqs.answer: Yes, if done to defeat lawful seizure or forfeiture.

title: Fraudulent Claim to Property to Prevent Its Seizure as Forfeited or in Execution
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently accepts, receives, or claims any property or any interest therein, intending thereby to prevent that property from being taken as forfeited or in execution of a decree, order, or sentence which has been made or which he knows is likely to be made by a competent authority, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Falsely claiming ownership or interest in property to prevent its lawful seizure or forfeiture is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A friend falsely claims ownership of property facing court attachment.
A person accepts a sham transfer of assets to prevent seizure.

faqs.question: Must the claim be fraudulent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Honest ownership disputes are not covered.

faqs.question: Does the section apply before an order is passed?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies where the person knows an order is likely.

title: Fraudulently Suffering Decree for Sum Not Due
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently causes or suffers a decree or order to be passed against him for a sum not due, or for a larger sum than is due, intending thereby to prevent property from being taken in execution or forfeiture, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Allowing a false court decree to be passed against oneself to shield assets from creditors or authorities is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A debtor colludes with another person to obtain a fake decree.
A person permits a false claim to succeed to protect property from attachment.

faqs.question: Why would someone allow a false decree?
faqs.answer: Often to hide assets from lawful recovery proceedings.

faqs.question: Must there be fraudulent intent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulent intent is essential.

title: Dishonestly Making False Claim in Court
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently, dishonestly, or with intent to injure or annoy any person, makes in a court of justice any claim which he knows to be false, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly filing a false claim in court is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
examples: A person files a lawsuit based on a claim known to be false.
An individual falsely claims ownership of property in court.

faqs.question: Must the person know the claim is false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of falsity is required.

faqs.question: Can civil claims be covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies to claims made in courts of justice.

title: Fraudulently Obtaining Decree for Sum Not Due
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently obtains a decree or order against any person for a sum not due, or for a larger sum than is due, or for property or interest to which he is not entitled, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Obtaining a court decree through fraud for money or property not actually owed is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person uses fabricated evidence to obtain a money decree.
Someone secures a court order for property they are not entitled to.

faqs.question: Does the decree have to be obtained fraudulently?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraud is a key element of the offence.

faqs.question: Can property claims be covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies to money, property, and interests.

title: False Charge of Offence Made with Intent to Injure
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, with intent to cause injury to any person, institutes or causes to be instituted any criminal proceeding against that person, or falsely charges any person with having committed an offence, knowing that there is no just or lawful ground for such proceeding or charge, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. If the false charge relates to an offence punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment for seven years or more, the punishment may extend to seven years and fine.
simpleExplanation: Falsely accusing someone of a crime or initiating a criminal case without lawful grounds is a serious offence.
punishment: Up to 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both; up to 7 years and fine for serious offences.
examples: A person knowingly files a false criminal complaint out of revenge.
Someone falsely accuses another of murder despite knowing the accusation is untrue.

faqs.question: Must the accusation be knowingly false?
faqs.answer: Yes. The accuser must know there is no lawful basis.

faqs.question: Is punishment higher for serious accusations?
faqs.answer: Yes. False accusations involving grave offences attract enhanced punishment.

title: Harbouring Offender
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever harbours or conceals a person whom he knows or has reason to believe to be an offender, with the intention of screening him from legal punishment, shall be punished according to the gravity of the offence committed by the offender.
simpleExplanation: Helping a criminal hide from authorities or avoid arrest is an offence.
punishment: Varies based on the seriousness of the offence committed by the offender being harboured.
examples: Providing shelter to a wanted criminal.
Helping an offender evade police arrest.

faqs.question: Must the person know the individual is an offender?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Does punishment depend on the underlying offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. More serious offences attract higher punishment.

title: Taking Gift, etc., to Screen an Offender from Punishment
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever accepts or obtains, or agrees to accept or attempts to obtain, any gratification or consideration for concealing an offence, screening an offender from punishment, or abstaining from legal proceedings against an offender, shall be punished as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: Accepting money or benefits in exchange for helping a criminal avoid punishment is an offence.
punishment: Varies according to the seriousness of the underlying offence.
examples: Accepting money to avoid reporting a crime.
Taking payment to withdraw support for a criminal prosecution.

faqs.question: Must the payment actually be received?
faqs.answer: No. Agreeing to accept or attempting to obtain it is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is this offence punishable?
faqs.answer: Because it interferes with the administration of justice.

title: Offering Gift or Restoration of Property in Consideration of Screening Offender
