chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever gives, offers, or promises any gratification, or restores or causes the restoration of any property, in consideration of any person's concealing an offence or screening an offender from punishment, shall be punished according to the gravity of the offence concealed.
simpleExplanation: Offering money, gifts, or property to help an offender escape punishment is an offence.
punishment: Varies depending on the seriousness of the offence involved.
examples: Offering money to a witness to suppress evidence.
Returning stolen property in exchange for dropping a complaint.

faqs.question: Is merely offering a benefit punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. An offer or promise is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does punishment depend on the offence concealed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The severity depends on the underlying offence.

title: Taking Gift to Help Recover Stolen Property, etc.
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever takes or agrees to take any gratification under pretence or on account of helping any person recover movable property which has been lost or of which the owner has been deprived by an offence, without using all means in his power to cause the offender to be brought to justice, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Accepting money to help recover stolen or lost property without attempting to bring the offender to justice is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Demanding payment to return stolen goods while protecting the thief.
Acting as an intermediary to recover property without reporting the offender.

faqs.question: Must the person attempt to bring the offender to justice?
faqs.answer: Yes. Failure to do so may attract liability.

faqs.question: Does the offence apply to lost property?
faqs.answer: Yes. It covers property lost or obtained through an offence.

title: Harbouring Offender Who Has Escaped from Custody or Whose Apprehension Has Been Ordered
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that any person has escaped from lawful custody or that an order has been issued for his apprehension, harbours or conceals that person with the intention of preventing him from being apprehended, shall be punished as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: Sheltering a person who has escaped from custody or is wanted by authorities is an offence.
punishment: Varies according to the gravity of the offence for which the person is sought.
examples: Hiding a prisoner who escaped from jail.
Providing shelter to a fugitive knowing that police are searching for them.

faqs.question: Must the person know the offender is wanted?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Does the offence apply to escaped prisoners?
faqs.answer: Yes. Escaped prisoners are specifically covered.

title: Penalty for Harbouring Robbers or Dacoits
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever harbours any person whom he knows or has reason to believe to be about to commit or to have recently committed robbery or dacoity, and thereby facilitates the commission of such offence, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Providing shelter or assistance to robbers or dacoits is a serious offence.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person knowingly shelters members of a dacoity gang.
Someone provides accommodation to robbers before a planned robbery.

faqs.question: Is knowledge required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know or have reason to believe the individual is involved in robbery or dacoity.

faqs.question: Why is the punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because robbery and dacoity are grave offences that threaten public safety.

title: Public Servant Disobeying Direction of Law with Intent to Save Person from Punishment or Property from Forfeiture
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, knowingly disobeys any direction of law regulating the manner in which he is to conduct himself, intending thereby to save, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby save, any person from legal punishment or subject any property to forfeiture less than that to which it is legally liable, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A public servant commits an offence by deliberately violating legal duties to protect an offender or property from lawful consequences.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: An officer intentionally ignores evidence to protect an accused person.
A public servant deliberately avoids enforcing a forfeiture order.

faqs.question: Must the public servant act knowingly?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge and intent are essential.

faqs.question: Can this apply to protecting property?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section covers both offenders and property.

title: Public Servant Framing Incorrect Record or Writing with Intent to Save Person from Punishment or Property from Forfeiture
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant charged with the preparation of any record or writing, knowingly frames that record or writing in a manner which he knows to be incorrect, intending thereby to save any person from legal punishment or to save property from forfeiture, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who deliberately prepares false records to help an offender or protect property commits an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A police officer falsifies investigation records to help an accused person.
An official alters records to avoid lawful confiscation of property.

faqs.question: Must the record be intentionally incorrect?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intentional falsification is required.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to written records?
faqs.answer: It applies to any official record or writing prepared by the public servant.

title: Public Servant in Judicial Proceeding Corruptly Making Report, etc., Contrary to Law
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant and legally bound in a judicial proceeding to make or pronounce any report, order, verdict, or decision, corruptly or maliciously makes or pronounces it contrary to law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A public servant commits an offence by intentionally making unlawful judicial decisions or reports out of corruption or malice.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A judicial officer knowingly issues an unlawful order for personal gain.
An official gives a decision contrary to law to harm a party.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to judicial proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. It specifically concerns judicial functions.

faqs.question: Must there be corruption or malice?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mere error of judgment is not sufficient.

title: Commitment for Trial or Confinement by Person Having Authority Who Knows That He Is Acting Contrary to Law
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being in any office which gives him legal authority to commit persons for trial or to confinement, corruptly or maliciously commits any person for trial or confinement, knowing that in so doing he is acting contrary to law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A public official who knowingly and unlawfully orders someone's trial or detention commits a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: An official knowingly orders the detention of an innocent person without legal basis.
A magistrate maliciously commits a person to trial contrary to law.

faqs.question: Must the official know the action is unlawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of illegality is essential.

faqs.question: Is wrongful detention covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Illegal confinement is specifically addressed.

title: Intentional Omission to Apprehend on the Part of Public Servant Bound to Apprehend
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement any person charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence, intentionally omits to apprehend such person or intentionally allows such person to escape, shall be punished according to the gravity of the offence for which the person was liable to be apprehended or confined.
simpleExplanation: A public servant commits an offence by deliberately failing to arrest or detain a person whom the law requires them to apprehend.
punishment: Varies depending on the seriousness of the underlying offence.
examples: A police officer intentionally allows a wanted criminal to escape.
A jail official deliberately fails to detain a person lawfully committed to custody.

faqs.question: Must the omission be intentional?
faqs.answer: Yes. Deliberate failure is required.

faqs.question: Does punishment depend on the underlying offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. More serious offences attract greater punishment.

title: Intentional Omission to Apprehend on the Part of Public Servant Bound to Apprehend Person Under Sentence or Lawfully Committed
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement any person under sentence of a court or lawfully committed to custody, intentionally omits to apprehend such person or intentionally suffers such person to escape, shall be punished according to the nature of the sentence or confinement involved.
simpleExplanation: A public servant commits an offence by deliberately allowing a convicted prisoner or detainee to escape.
punishment: Varies according to the sentence being served by the prisoner.
examples: A prison guard intentionally allows a convicted prisoner to escape.
A police officer deliberately ignores a prisoner fleeing from lawful custody.

faqs.question: Does this apply to convicted prisoners?
faqs.answer: Yes. It specifically covers persons under sentence or lawful confinement.

faqs.question: Must the escape be intentional?
faqs.answer: Yes. The public servant must intentionally allow it.

title: Escape from Confinement or Custody Negligently Suffered by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to keep in confinement any person charged with or convicted of an offence, negligently suffers such person to escape from confinement, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A public servant may be punished if negligence allows a prisoner or accused person to escape.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A jail guard leaves a cell unlocked, allowing a prisoner to escape.
A police officer negligently fails to supervise a detainee.

faqs.question: Is intention required?
faqs.answer: No. Negligence is sufficient under this section.

faqs.question: How does this differ from Sections 221 and 222?
faqs.answer: Those sections require intentional conduct, while this section deals with negligence.

title: Resistance or Obstruction by a Person to His Lawful Apprehension
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever intentionally resists or obstructs his own lawful apprehension for any offence, or escapes or attempts to escape from lawful custody, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both, in addition to any punishment for the offence for which he was apprehended.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by resisting arrest, obstructing lawful apprehension, or escaping from lawful custody.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both, in addition to punishment for the original offence.
examples: A suspect fights with police officers attempting a lawful arrest.
A detainee escapes from police custody.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished for both the original offence and escape?
faqs.answer: Yes. Punishment under this section is additional.

faqs.question: Must the apprehension be lawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. The arrest or custody must be lawful.

title: Resistance or Obstruction to Lawful Apprehension of Another Person
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever intentionally resists or obstructs the lawful apprehension of another person, or rescues or attempts to rescue another person from lawful custody, shall be punished according to the seriousness of the offence for which that person is liable to be apprehended or detained.
simpleExplanation: Helping another person avoid arrest or escape from lawful custody is a criminal offence.
punishment: Varies according to the seriousness of the underlying offence.
examples: A person interferes with police officers attempting to arrest a suspect.
Someone helps a prisoner escape from lawful custody.

faqs.question: Does the section apply to attempted rescues?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempts are also punishable.

faqs.question: Does punishment depend on the underlying offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The severity is linked to the offence involved.

title: Omission to Apprehend, or Suffering Escape on Part of Public Servant, in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement any person, intentionally omits to apprehend such person or intentionally suffers such person to escape in cases not otherwise provided for in this Code, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section covers situations where a public servant deliberately fails to arrest or detain a person and no other specific IPC provision applies.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A public servant intentionally ignores a duty to apprehend a person when no other specific provision applies.
An official knowingly allows a detainee to leave custody unlawfully.

faqs.question: Why does this section exist?
faqs.answer: It acts as a residual provision covering situations not specifically addressed elsewhere.

faqs.question: Is negligence enough?
faqs.answer: No. The omission or escape must be intentional.

title: Resistance or Obstruction to Lawful Apprehension, or Escape or Rescue in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever resists or obstructs the lawful apprehension of any person, or rescues or attempts to rescue any person from lawful custody, in cases not otherwise provided for in this Code, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes obstruction of lawful arrest or rescue from custody where no other specific IPC provision applies.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A person blocks officials from lawfully apprehending someone.
An individual helps another person escape custody in circumstances not covered by other sections.

faqs.question: Is this a general provision?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies where no more specific section covers the conduct.

faqs.question: Does attempted rescue count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempts are expressly covered.

title: Unlawful Return from Transportation
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: This section related to unlawful return from transportation and has become obsolete due to the abolition of the punishment of transportation under Indian law.
simpleExplanation: The provision historically applied to convicts sentenced to transportation but is now obsolete.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: The section has historical significance only.
Transportation is no longer a recognized punishment.

faqs.question: Is this section still relevant today?
faqs.answer: No. It is effectively obsolete.

faqs.question: Why is it retained in numbering?
faqs.answer: For continuity of the IPC structure.

title: Violation of Condition of Remission of Punishment
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, having accepted any conditional remission of punishment, knowingly violates any condition on which such remission was granted, shall be punished with the punishment to which he was originally sentenced if the condition had not been remitted.
simpleExplanation: A person who breaches the conditions attached to a remission of punishment may be required to undergo the original sentence.
punishment: Revival of the original sentence or remaining portion thereof.
examples: A prisoner released subject to conditions violates those conditions.
A convict breaches restrictions imposed as part of a remission order.

faqs.question: What is remission?
faqs.answer: It is a reduction or relaxation of punishment granted by competent authority.

faqs.question: What happens if conditions are violated?
faqs.answer: The original sentence may be enforced.

title: Intentional Insult or Interruption to Public Servant Sitting in Judicial Proceeding
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever intentionally offers any insult, or causes any interruption, to any public servant while such public servant is sitting in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Insulting or disrupting a judge or other public servant during judicial proceedings is an offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A person shouts abusive remarks at a judge during court proceedings.
An individual deliberately disrupts a judicial hearing.

faqs.question: Must the act be intentional?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intentional insult or interruption is required.

faqs.question: Does the section apply only to judges?
faqs.answer: It applies to any public servant conducting a judicial proceeding.

title: Personation of a Juror or Assessor
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever personates a juror or assessor, or knowingly suffers himself to be returned, empanelled, or sworn as a juror or assessor in the name of another person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Pretending to be a juror or assessor, or serving in that capacity under another person's identity, is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person falsely appears as a juror using another individual's identity.
Someone knowingly serves as an assessor under a false name.

faqs.question: What is personation?
faqs.answer: It means falsely pretending to be another person.

faqs.question: Why is this offence serious?
faqs.answer: Because it undermines the integrity of judicial proceedings.

title: Coin Defined
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Coin is metal used for the time being as money, and stamped and issued by the authority of some State or Sovereign Power in order to be so used.
simpleExplanation: This section defines what qualifies as a coin under the IPC. It refers to officially issued metal money recognized by a government or sovereign authority.
punishment: Not applicable. This is a definition section.
examples: An Indian rupee coin issued by the Government of India is a coin.
A foreign country's officially issued currency coin is also covered.

faqs.question: Does this section create an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It only defines the term 'coin'.

faqs.question: Are unofficial tokens considered coins?
faqs.answer: No. The coin must be officially issued by a sovereign authority.

title: Counterfeiting Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Making fake coins or participating in the process of creating counterfeit coins is a serious criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person manufactures fake currency coins.
An individual assists in producing counterfeit coins.

faqs.question: Is partial involvement punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even performing part of the counterfeiting process is covered.

faqs.question: Must the coin be successfully circulated?
faqs.answer: No. Participation in counterfeiting itself is sufficient.

title: Counterfeiting Indian Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Indian coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Counterfeiting Indian coins is treated more seriously than counterfeiting foreign coins because it directly affects the country's currency system.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A person manufactures fake Indian rupee coins.
An individual assists in the production of counterfeit Indian coins.

faqs.question: Why is punishment more severe for Indian coins?
faqs.answer: Because it directly affects India's monetary system.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section expressly permits life imprisonment.

title: Making or Selling Instrument for Counterfeiting Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever makes, buys, sells, disposes of, or has in his possession any machinery, instrument, or material for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for counterfeiting coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Possessing or dealing in equipment intended for making counterfeit coins is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A person sells coin-stamping tools to counterfeiters.
An individual possesses machinery intended for making fake coins.

faqs.question: Must the equipment actually be used?
faqs.answer: No. Possession with the required knowledge or intent is enough.

faqs.question: Does this apply to sellers?
faqs.answer: Yes. Selling such instruments is specifically covered.

title: Making or Selling Instrument for Counterfeiting Indian Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever makes, buys, sells, disposes of, or has in his possession any machinery, instrument, or material for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for counterfeiting Indian coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Possessing or supplying equipment intended for counterfeiting Indian coins is treated as a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person manufactures dies specifically designed for fake Indian coins.
An individual stores coin-making equipment knowing it will be used to counterfeit Indian currency.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher than Section 233?
faqs.answer: Because the offence relates specifically to Indian coinage.

faqs.question: Is possession alone sufficient?
faqs.answer: Yes, if accompanied by the required knowledge or intent.

title: Possession of Instrument or Material for the Purpose of Using the Same for Counterfeiting Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever is in possession of any instrument, tool, machinery, or material intended for use in counterfeiting coin, knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended for such use, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Keeping tools or materials intended for making counterfeit coins is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A person stores molds designed for making fake coins.
An individual possesses equipment intended for coin counterfeiting.

faqs.question: Must the tools actually be used?
faqs.answer: No. Possession with the required knowledge is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does suspicion alone establish guilt?
faqs.answer: No. Knowledge or reason to believe the intended use must be proved.

title: Abetting in India the Counterfeiting Out of India of Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, while in India, abets the counterfeiting of coin outside India shall be punished in the same manner as if the counterfeiting had been committed within India.
simpleExplanation: Helping or encouraging the counterfeiting of coins outside India is punishable as if the offence occurred in India.
punishment: Same punishment as the corresponding counterfeiting offence.
examples: A person in India finances a coin-counterfeiting operation abroad.
An individual supplies plans for making counterfeit coins in another country.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished for foreign counterfeiting activities?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they abet the offence from India.

faqs.question: How is punishment determined?
faqs.answer: It is the same as if the offence had occurred in India.

title: Import or Export of Counterfeit Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever imports into India, or exports from India, any counterfeit coin, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Bringing counterfeit coins into India or sending them out of India is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A person smuggles counterfeit coins into India.
An individual exports fake coins to another country.

faqs.question: Must the person know the coins are counterfeit?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Does this apply to both import and export?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both are covered.

title: Import or Export of Counterfeit Indian Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever imports into India, or exports from India, any counterfeit Indian coin, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Importing or exporting counterfeit Indian coins is treated as a more serious offence than dealing with counterfeit foreign coins.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A person imports counterfeit Indian rupee coins from abroad.
An individual exports fake Indian coins for illegal circulation.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher?
faqs.answer: Because the offence directly affects India's currency system.

faqs.question: Must the person know the coins are counterfeit?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

title: Delivery of Coin as Genuine Which, When First Possessed, the Deliverer Did Not Know to Be Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever delivers as genuine any coin which, when he became possessed of it, he did not know to be counterfeit, but which before delivering it he discovered to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by passing a counterfeit coin after discovering that it is fake.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A shopkeeper learns a coin is counterfeit but uses it in a later transaction.
A person knowingly passes a fake coin after discovering its true nature.

faqs.question: What if the person did not know the coin was fake when received?
faqs.answer: The offence arises only if the person later discovers the truth and still passes it as genuine.

faqs.question: Must the coin be delivered as genuine?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must represent it as real currency.

title: Delivery of Indian Coin as Genuine Which, When First Possessed, the Deliverer Did Not Know to Be Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever delivers as genuine any counterfeit Indian coin which, when he became possessed of it, he did not know to be counterfeit, but which before delivering it he discovered to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by knowingly passing a counterfeit Indian coin as genuine after discovering that it is fake.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
examples: A person receives a fake ₹10 coin unknowingly but later spends it after discovering it is counterfeit.
A shopkeeper knowingly uses a counterfeit Indian coin in a transaction.

faqs.question: Is initial ignorance a defense?
faqs.answer: Only until the person discovers the coin is counterfeit.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher than Section 239?
faqs.answer: Because the offence involves counterfeit Indian coin.

title: Possession of Counterfeit Coin by Person Who Knew It to Be Counterfeit When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever has in his possession any counterfeit coin, knowing at the time when he became possessed thereof that it was counterfeit, and intending to use the same as genuine or that it may be used as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly possessing counterfeit coins with the intention of using them as genuine currency is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A person stores counterfeit foreign coins intending to circulate them.
An individual knowingly acquires fake coins for use in transactions.

faqs.question: Must the person know the coin is counterfeit?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge at the time of possession is required.

faqs.question: Is intention to use the coin important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The counterfeit coin must be intended for use as genuine.

title: Possession of Counterfeit Indian Coin by Person Who Knew It to Be Counterfeit When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever has in his possession any counterfeit Indian coin, knowing at the time when he became possessed thereof that it was counterfeit, and intending to use the same as genuine or that it may be used as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Possessing counterfeit Indian coins while knowing they are fake and intending to use them as real currency is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person keeps counterfeit Indian coins for circulation in the market.
An individual knowingly acquires fake rupee coins for commercial use.

faqs.question: Why is punishment more severe?
faqs.answer: Because the offence directly affects Indian currency.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to use the coin?
faqs.answer: Yes. Possession alone is insufficient without the required intent.

title: Possession of Counterfeit Coin by Person Who Did Not Know It to Be Counterfeit When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, having become possessed of any counterfeit coin without knowing it to be counterfeit, afterwards discovers it to be counterfeit and nevertheless keeps it with intent that it may be used as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Keeping a counterfeit coin after discovering it is fake, intending to use it as genuine, is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: A person finds out a coin is counterfeit but keeps it for future use.
An individual retains fake currency intending to pass it to someone else.

faqs.question: What if the person initially received the coin innocently?
faqs.answer: The offence begins only after discovering the coin is counterfeit and retaining it with unlawful intent.

faqs.question: Is actual use required?
faqs.answer: No. Retention with intent is sufficient.

title: Person Having Possession of Counterfeit Indian Coin and Who Knew It to Be Counterfeit When He Became Possessed Thereof Delivering It to Another Person
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, having possessed counterfeit Indian coin knowing it to be counterfeit at the time of possession, delivers it to another person as genuine, or attempts to induce another person to receive it as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly passing counterfeit Indian coins to another person as genuine currency is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person knowingly uses counterfeit Indian coins to make purchases.
An individual persuades another person to accept fake Indian coins as genuine.

faqs.question: Must the coin be represented as genuine?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence involves presenting the coin as real currency.

faqs.question: Can an attempt be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempting to induce acceptance is also covered.

title: Person Having Possession of Counterfeit Indian Coin and Who Did Not Know It to Be Counterfeit When He Became Possessed Thereof Delivering It to Another Person
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, having become possessed of counterfeit Indian coin without knowing it to be counterfeit, afterwards discovers it to be counterfeit and nevertheless delivers it to another person as genuine, or attempts to induce another person to receive it as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who unknowingly receives a counterfeit Indian coin but later discovers it is fake and still passes it as genuine commits an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A shopkeeper discovers a coin is counterfeit but uses it in another transaction.
A person knowingly passes a fake Indian coin after learning its true nature.

faqs.question: Is initial ignorance a defence?
faqs.answer: Only until the person discovers the coin is counterfeit.

faqs.question: Must the coin be passed as genuine?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence requires representing it as genuine.

title: Fraudulently or Dishonestly Diminishing Weight or Altering Composition of Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly diminishes the weight or alters the composition of any coin, intending that when so altered it may pass as coin of a different description or value, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Altering a coin's weight or metal composition to deceive others about its value is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Removing precious metal from coins and circulating them as genuine.
Changing the composition of coins to imitate higher-value coins.

faqs.question: Must the alteration be fraudulent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulent or dishonest intent is essential.

faqs.question: Does the offence require circulation of the altered coin?
faqs.answer: No. The alteration itself with the required intent is sufficient.

title: Fraudulently or Dishonestly Diminishing Weight or Altering Composition of Indian Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly diminishes the weight or alters the composition of any Indian coin shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Altering the weight or composition of Indian coins is treated as a more serious offence because it directly affects the national currency.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Melting portions of Indian coins and returning them to circulation.
Changing the metallic composition of Indian currency coins.

faqs.question: Why is punishment more severe?
faqs.answer: Because the offence directly impacts India's currency system.

faqs.question: Must there be fraudulent intent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulent or dishonest conduct is required.

title: Altering Appearance of Coin with Intent That It Shall Pass as Coin of Different Description
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly alters the appearance of any coin with intent that it shall pass as coin of a different description shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Changing the appearance of a coin so that it appears to be a different coin is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Modifying a low-value coin to resemble a higher-value coin.
Altering markings on a coin to deceive users.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to deceive?
faqs.answer: Yes. The altered coin must be intended to pass as a different coin.

faqs.question: Does physical alteration matter?
faqs.answer: Yes. The appearance must be changed.

title: Altering Appearance of Indian Coin with Intent That It Shall Pass as Coin of Different Description
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly alters the appearance of any Indian coin with intent that it shall pass as coin of a different description shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Changing the appearance of Indian coins to misrepresent their value or type is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Altering an Indian coin to resemble a higher denomination coin.
Changing coin markings to deceive users regarding its value.

faqs.question: Why is punishment greater than Section 248?
faqs.answer: Because the offence specifically affects Indian currency.

faqs.question: Must the alteration be dishonest?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulent or dishonest intent is required.

title: Delivery of Coin, Possessed with Knowledge That It Is Altered
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, having possession of coin which he knows to have been altered in a manner described in Sections 246, 247, 248, or 249, fraudulently or dishonestly delivers it as genuine coin, or attempts to induce another person to receive it as genuine coin, shall be punished according to law.
simpleExplanation: A person commits an offence by knowingly passing an altered coin as genuine currency.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine (depending on the nature of the altered coin).
examples: A person knowingly uses an altered coin in a commercial transaction.
An individual persuades another person to accept a modified coin as genuine.

faqs.question: Must the person know the coin is altered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is an essential requirement.

faqs.question: Is an attempt punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempting to induce acceptance is covered.

title: Delivery of Indian Coin, Possessed with Knowledge That It Is Altered
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, having possession of Indian coin which he knows to have been altered in a manner described in Sections 247 or 249, fraudulently or dishonestly delivers it as genuine Indian coin, or attempts to induce another person to receive it as genuine Indian coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly passing an altered Indian coin as genuine is a serious criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person knowingly circulates an altered Indian coin.
An individual attempts to pass a modified Indian coin as genuine currency.

faqs.question: Why is punishment more severe?
faqs.answer: Because the offence concerns Indian currency.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of alteration required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know the coin has been altered.

title: Possession of Coin by Person Who Knew It to Be Altered When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever has in his possession any coin which he knows to have been altered and intends to use it as genuine, or that it may be used as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Possessing altered coins with the intention of using them as genuine currency is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A person knowingly stores altered coins for future circulation.
An individual acquires modified coins intending to pass them as genuine.

faqs.question: Must the person know the coin is altered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge at the time of possession is required.

faqs.question: Is intent important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The altered coin must be intended for use as genuine.

title: Possession of Indian Coin by Person Who Knew It to Be Altered When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever has in his possession any altered Indian coin, knowing it to be altered when he became possessed thereof, and intending to use it as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Possessing altered Indian coins with knowledge of the alteration and intent to use them as genuine is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person knowingly stores altered Indian coins for circulation.
An individual acquires modified Indian coins intending to use them in transactions.

faqs.question: Why is punishment greater than Section 252?
faqs.answer: Because the offence specifically concerns Indian currency.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to use the coin?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mere possession without intent is insufficient.

title: Delivery of Coin as Genuine Which, When First Possessed, the Deliverer Did Not Know to Be Altered
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, having become possessed of altered coin without knowing it to be altered, afterwards discovers the alteration and nevertheless delivers it as genuine coin, or attempts to induce another person to receive it as genuine coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Passing an altered coin as genuine after discovering the alteration is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person discovers a coin has been altered but still uses it in a transaction.
An individual knowingly passes a modified coin after learning of its alteration.

faqs.question: Does initial ignorance excuse liability?
faqs.answer: Only until the person learns the coin is altered.

faqs.question: Must the coin be presented as genuine?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence requires representing it as genuine.

title: Counterfeiting Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting, any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Making fake government revenue stamps or participating in their creation is a very serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A person prints counterfeit stamp papers.
An individual assists in manufacturing fake revenue stamps.

faqs.question: Does the offence apply to partial participation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even performing part of the counterfeiting process is punishable.

faqs.question: Why is the punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because counterfeit government stamps undermine public revenue and official documentation.

title: Having Possession of Instrument or Material for Counterfeiting Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever has in his possession any instrument, tool, machinery, or material for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for counterfeiting any Government stamp, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Possessing tools or materials intended for making fake government stamps is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person stores printing plates used for counterfeit stamp papers.
An individual possesses machinery intended for producing fake government stamps.

faqs.question: Must the tools actually be used?
faqs.answer: No. Possession with the required knowledge is sufficient.

faqs.question: What kind of materials are covered?
faqs.answer: Any tools, machinery, plates, dies, or materials intended for counterfeiting.

title: Making or Selling Instrument for Counterfeiting Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever makes, buys, sells, disposes of, or has in his possession any machinery, instrument, or material intended for counterfeiting Government stamps shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Manufacturing, selling, purchasing, or possessing equipment intended for making counterfeit government stamps is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person sells counterfeit stamp-printing equipment.
An individual manufactures plates for fake revenue stamps.

faqs.question: Does selling such equipment amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Selling, making, buying, or possessing it is punishable.

faqs.question: Must there be knowledge of the intended use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know or have reason to believe it is for counterfeiting.

title: Sale of Counterfeit Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever sells, offers for sale, uses, or has in his possession any counterfeit Government stamp, knowing or having reason to believe it to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Selling, using, or possessing counterfeit government stamps while knowing they are fake is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person sells fake stamp papers to the public.
An individual knowingly uses counterfeit revenue stamps in official documents.

faqs.question: Must the person know the stamp is counterfeit?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Is mere possession punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes, when accompanied by the required knowledge.

title: Having Possession of Counterfeit Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever has in his possession any counterfeit Government stamp, knowing it to be counterfeit and intending to use it as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Possessing counterfeit government stamps with the intention of using them as genuine is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person stores counterfeit stamp papers for future transactions.
An individual possesses fake revenue stamps intending to use them in legal documents.

faqs.question: Must the person intend to use the stamp?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent to use it as genuine is required.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of counterfeiting necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know the stamp is counterfeit.

title: Using as Genuine a Government Stamp Known to Be Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever uses as genuine any Government stamp which he knows or has reason to believe to be counterfeit shall be punished in the same manner as if he had counterfeited such stamp.
simpleExplanation: Using a counterfeit government stamp while knowing it is fake is treated as seriously as making the counterfeit stamp itself.
punishment: Same punishment as counterfeiting a Government stamp (life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine).
examples: Using a fake stamp paper in a property transaction.
Submitting a document bearing a counterfeit revenue stamp.

faqs.question: Must the person know the stamp is counterfeit?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Why is the punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because counterfeit government stamps affect public revenue and legal documentation.

title: Effacing Writing from Substance Bearing Government Stamp, or Removing from Document a Stamp Used for It, with Intent to Cause Loss to Government
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever removes, effaces, or alters any writing on a document bearing a Government stamp, or removes such stamp from a document, with intent to cause loss to Government, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Removing or altering government stamps to avoid paying stamp duties or to reuse stamps is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Removing a stamp from an old document for reuse.
Erasing cancellation marks from a revenue stamp.

faqs.question: Why is this prohibited?
faqs.answer: Because it can deprive the government of lawful revenue.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to cause loss to Government?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent is a necessary element.

title: Using Government Stamp Known to Have Been Before Used
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to Government uses for any purpose a Government stamp which he knows has been used before shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees.
simpleExplanation: Reusing a government stamp that has already been used is an offence.
punishment: Fine up to ₹200.
examples: Using a previously cancelled stamp on another document.
Reapplying an old revenue stamp to avoid paying stamp duty.

faqs.question: Must the person know the stamp was previously used?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is required.

faqs.question: Does the offence require intent to cause loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulent intent or intent to cause loss to Government is necessary.

title: Erasure of Mark Denoting That Stamp Has Been Used
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever erases or removes from a Government stamp any mark put or impressed upon it for the purpose of denoting that the stamp has been used, or knowingly possesses any such stamp from which such mark has been removed, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Removing cancellation marks from used government stamps to make them appear unused is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Erasing a cancellation mark from a revenue stamp.
Possessing a stamp from which an official usage mark has been removed.

faqs.question: Can possession alone be an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the person knowingly possesses such a stamp.

faqs.question: Why are cancellation marks important?
faqs.answer: They prevent reuse of government stamps.

title: Prohibition of Fictitious Stamps
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever makes, knowingly utters, deals in, sells, possesses, or uses any fictitious stamp, or any stamp so nearly resembling a Government stamp as to be likely to deceive, shall be punished with fine or imprisonment as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Creating, selling, possessing, or using fake stamps that resemble official government stamps is an offence.
punishment: Punishment as prescribed under the section, including imprisonment and/or fine.
examples: Printing fake postal or revenue stamps that resemble official ones.
Selling imitation government stamps to the public.

faqs.question: What is a fictitious stamp?
faqs.answer: A stamp that falsely resembles an official government-issued stamp.

faqs.question: Is possession alone punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes, if accompanied by the required knowledge.

title: Fraudulent Use of False Instrument for Weighing
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Offences Relating to Weights and Measures
officialText: Whoever fraudulently uses any instrument for weighing which he knows to be false, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Using a weighing instrument that is known to be inaccurate or manipulated in order to deceive others is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: A shopkeeper uses a tampered weighing scale to give customers less product than paid for.
A trader knowingly uses a defective weighing machine during commercial transactions.

faqs.question: Must the person know the instrument is false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of the falsity is required.

faqs.question: Is accidental use punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The use must be fraudulent and knowing.

title: Fraudulent Use of False Weight or Measure
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Offences Relating to Weights and Measures
officialText: Whoever fraudulently uses any false weight or false measure of length or capacity, or fraudulently uses any weight or measure as a different weight or measure from what it really is, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Using false weights or measures to deceive buyers or sellers is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Using a 900-gram weight while representing it as 1 kilogram.
Selling fuel using a manipulated measuring device.

faqs.question: Does this apply to both weights and measures?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section covers both.

faqs.question: Must the conduct be fraudulent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulent use is required.

title: Being in Possession of False Weight or Measure
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Offences Relating to Weights and Measures
officialText: Whoever is in possession of any false weight or false measure of length or capacity, knowing it to be false, and intending that it may be fraudulently used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Keeping false weights or measures with the intention that they be used for fraud is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: A merchant stores tampered weights for use in business transactions.
A trader keeps altered measuring containers to deceive customers.

faqs.question: Is mere possession enough?
faqs.answer: No. The person must know the weight or measure is false and intend fraudulent use.

faqs.question: Must actual fraud occur?
faqs.answer: No. Intent for fraudulent use is sufficient.

title: Making or Selling False Weight or Measure
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Offences Relating to Weights and Measures
officialText: Whoever makes, sells, or disposes of any false weight or false measure of length or capacity, knowing it to be false, in order that the same may be used as true, or knowing that it is likely to be used as true, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Manufacturing or selling false weights and measures that may be used to deceive others is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: A manufacturer produces tampered weighing weights for sale.
A supplier sells false measuring instruments to retailers.

faqs.question: Must the seller know the item is false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of falsity is required.

faqs.question: Does the section cover manufacturers?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies to making, selling, or disposing of false weights or measures.

title: Public Nuisance
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger, or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, danger, or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right.
simpleExplanation: A public nuisance occurs when a person's act or failure to act causes harm, danger, inconvenience, or annoyance to the public at large.
punishment: Defined offence; punishment generally provided under Section 290 unless otherwise specifically provided.
examples: Blocking a public road causing inconvenience to the public.
Discharging pollutants that affect an entire neighborhood.

faqs.question: Does the nuisance have to affect many people?
faqs.answer: Yes. It must affect the public or a section of the public, not merely a private individual.

faqs.question: Is actual injury required?
faqs.answer: No. Danger, obstruction, or annoyance may be sufficient.

title: Negligent Act Likely to Spread Infection of Disease Dangerous to Life
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Negligently acting in a way that may spread a life-threatening infectious disease is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: Ignoring quarantine requirements while carrying a contagious disease.
Negligently exposing others to a dangerous infectious illness.

faqs.question: Must the person know there is a risk of spreading disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe the risk exists is required.

faqs.question: Is negligence sufficient?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section specifically covers negligent acts.

title: Malignant Act Likely to Spread Infection of Disease Dangerous to Life
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever malignantly does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally or maliciously acting in a way that may spread a dangerous infectious disease is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Knowingly exposing others to a dangerous contagious disease.
Deliberately violating isolation orders with intent to spread infection.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 269?
faqs.answer: Section 270 involves malicious or intentional conduct, whereas Section 269 involves negligence.

faqs.question: Must there be actual infection?
faqs.answer: No. The act only needs to be likely to spread infection.

title: Disobedience to Quarantine Rule
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever knowingly disobeys any rule made and promulgated by the Government for putting any vessel into a state of quarantine, or for regulating the intercourse of vessels in a state of quarantine with the shore or with other vessels, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly violating government quarantine rules designed to prevent the spread of disease is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A ship operator violates quarantine restrictions imposed by authorities.
A person knowingly breaches quarantine regulations during an outbreak.

faqs.question: Does the section apply only to ships?
faqs.answer: Historically it focused on vessels, but quarantine-related violations remain relevant in public health contexts.

faqs.question: Must the violation be knowing?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must knowingly disobey the rule.

title: Adulteration of Food or Drink Intended for Sale
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever adulterates any article of food or drink so as to make such article noxious as food or drink, intending to sell such article as food or drink, or knowing it to be likely that the same will be sold as food or drink, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Mixing harmful substances into food or drink intended for sale is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment and/or fine as prescribed by law (subject to amendments and special food safety laws).
examples: Adding harmful chemicals to milk for commercial sale.
Mixing unsafe substances into beverages sold to consumers.

faqs.question: Must the food be intended for sale?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies to food or drink meant for sale.

faqs.question: Why is adulteration punishable?
faqs.answer: Because it endangers public health and consumer safety.

title: Sale of Noxious Food or Drink
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever sells, or offers or exposes for sale, as food or drink, any article which has been rendered or has become noxious, or is in a state unfit for food or drink, knowing or having reason to believe that the same is noxious or unfit, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Selling food or drinks that are harmful or unfit for consumption, while knowing their condition, is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment and/or fine as prescribed by law.
examples: Selling spoiled milk knowing it is unsafe for consumption.
Offering contaminated food products to customers.

faqs.question: Must the seller know the food is harmful?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Does the section apply to offering food for sale?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even offering or exposing it for sale is covered.

title: Adulteration of Drugs
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever adulterates any drug or medical preparation in such a manner as to lessen its efficacy, change its operation, or make it harmful, intending that it shall be sold or used as genuine, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Tampering with medicines so that they become ineffective or harmful is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment and/or fine as prescribed by law.
examples: Diluting a medicine before sale.
Mixing harmful substances into pharmaceutical products.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to medicines?
faqs.answer: It applies to drugs and medical preparations.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to sell or use it as genuine?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent is a necessary element.

title: Sale of Adulterated Drugs
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever knowingly sells, or offers for sale, any adulterated drug or medical preparation as genuine shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Selling medicines that have been adulterated while presenting them as genuine is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment and/or fine as prescribed by law.
examples: Selling diluted medicines as authentic products.
Offering adulterated drugs to patients.

faqs.question: Must the seller know the drug is adulterated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is required.

faqs.question: Does offering the drug for sale count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Offering for sale is covered.

title: Sale of Drug as a Different Drug or Preparation
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever knowingly sells, or offers or exposes for sale, any drug or medical preparation as a different drug or medical preparation shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Selling one medicine while falsely representing it as another medicine is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment and/or fine as prescribed by law.
examples: Selling a low-cost generic medicine as a premium branded drug.
Providing the wrong medicine while claiming it is the prescribed one.

faqs.question: Must there be knowledge of the misrepresentation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The seller must knowingly misrepresent the drug.

faqs.question: Why is this offence serious?
faqs.answer: Because incorrect medicines can endanger health and life.

title: Fouling Water of Public Spring or Reservoir
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever voluntarily corrupts or fouls the water of any public spring or reservoir so as to render it less fit for the purpose for which it is ordinarily used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Polluting a public water source and making it unsafe or unsuitable for use is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine, or both.
examples: Dumping waste into a public reservoir.
Contaminating a village water source with chemicals.

faqs.question: Does the section apply only to drinking water?
faqs.answer: No. It applies to public water sources used for ordinary public purposes.

faqs.question: Must the act be voluntary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The fouling or corruption must be voluntary.

title: Making Atmosphere Noxious to Health
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever voluntarily vitiates the atmosphere in any place so as to make it noxious to the health of persons in general dwelling or carrying on business in the neighborhood, or passing along a public way, shall be punished with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally polluting the air in a way that harms public health is an offence.
punishment: Fine up to ₹500.
examples: Releasing toxic fumes into a residential area.
Burning hazardous waste that pollutes the surrounding air.

faqs.question: Must the atmosphere become harmful to health?
faqs.answer: Yes. The pollution must make the air noxious to health.

faqs.question: Does this apply to public places?
faqs.answer: Yes. It includes areas affecting the public or nearby residents.

title: Rash Driving or Riding on a Public Way
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever drives any vehicle, or rides, on any public way in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Driving or riding recklessly on a public road in a way that endangers people is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Driving at excessive speed through a crowded market.
Performing dangerous stunts on a public road.

faqs.question: Must an accident occur for this offence?
faqs.answer: No. Endangering life or likely injury is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does it apply to motorcycles?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies to driving or riding any vehicle on a public way.

title: Rash Navigation of Vessel
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever navigates any vessel in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Operating a boat or vessel recklessly in a way that endangers people is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Operating a ferry recklessly in crowded waters.
Ignoring navigation rules and risking collisions.

faqs.question: Must someone be injured?
faqs.answer: No. The risk of injury is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does this apply to all vessels?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any vessel operated negligently may attract liability.

title: Exhibition of False Light, Mark or Buoy
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever exhibits any false light, mark, or buoy, intending or knowing it to be likely that thereby any navigator may be misled, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Displaying false navigation signals that could mislead ships or navigators is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Placing a fake navigation light near a coastline.
Using false buoys that misdirect vessels.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to mislead?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent or knowledge of likely deception is required.

faqs.question: Is actual damage necessary?
faqs.answer: No. The likelihood of misleading navigators is sufficient.

title: Conveying Person by Water for Hire in Unsafe or Overloaded Vessel
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever knowingly or negligently conveys, or causes to be conveyed, any person for hire in any vessel which is in such a state or so loaded as to endanger the life of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Transporting passengers in an unsafe or overloaded vessel is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Operating an overloaded passenger boat.
Using an unseaworthy vessel to transport paying passengers.

faqs.question: Must there be an accident?
faqs.answer: No. Endangering life is enough.

faqs.question: Does the section apply to hired transport?
faqs.answer: Yes. It specifically covers transportation for hire.

title: Danger or Obstruction in Public Way or Line of Navigation
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever, by doing any act, or by omitting to take order with any property in his possession or under his charge, causes danger, obstruction, or injury to any person in any public way or public line of navigation, shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees.
simpleExplanation: Creating a danger or obstruction on a public road or navigational route is an offence.
punishment: Fine up to ₹200.
examples: Leaving construction materials on a public road.
Placing objects in a navigable waterway that obstruct boats.

faqs.question: Must someone actually be injured?
faqs.answer: No. Creating danger or obstruction is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does the section apply to omissions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Failing to manage property causing danger can attract liability.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Poisonous Substance
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever does, with any poisonous substance, any act in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or likely to cause hurt or injury to any person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any poisonous substance in his possession as is sufficient to guard against probable danger to human life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Handling or storing poisonous substances carelessly in a way that endangers others is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Leaving toxic chemicals unsecured in a public area.
Improperly storing poisonous pesticides.

faqs.question: Must injury actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Endangering life or safety is enough.

faqs.question: Does this apply to negligent storage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Negligent handling or storage is covered.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Fire or Combustible Matter
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever does, with fire or any combustible matter, any act in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or likely to cause hurt or injury to any person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with fire or combustible matter in his possession as is sufficient to guard against probable danger to human life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Carelessly handling fire or flammable materials in a way that endangers people is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Leaving an open fire unattended near combustible materials.
Improper storage of flammable liquids.

faqs.question: Is actual fire damage required?
faqs.answer: No. The risk of danger is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does negligent storage count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Negligent handling or storage is covered.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Explosive Substance
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever does, with any explosive substance, any act in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or likely to cause hurt or injury to any person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any explosive substance in his possession as is sufficient to guard against probable danger to human life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Handling or storing explosives carelessly in a way that endangers others is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
