examples: Improperly storing explosive materials.
Negligently transporting explosives without safety precautions.

faqs.question: Must an explosion occur?
faqs.answer: No. The risk to human life is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does this cover negligent storage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both handling and storage are covered.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Machinery
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever does, with any machinery, any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life, or likely to cause hurt or injury to any person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any machinery in his possession or under his care as is sufficient to guard against probable danger to human life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Careless operation or maintenance of machinery that endangers people is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Operating factory equipment without safety guards.
Failing to maintain dangerous machinery properly.

faqs.question: Does this apply in workplaces?
faqs.answer: Yes. Industrial and workplace machinery are commonly covered.

faqs.question: Must injury occur?
faqs.answer: No. Endangering life is sufficient.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Pulling Down or Repairing Buildings
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever, in pulling down or repairing any building, knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with that building as is sufficient to guard against probable danger to human life from the fall of that building or any part thereof, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Failing to take proper safety precautions while demolishing or repairing a building, thereby endangering people, is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Demolishing a building without securing the surrounding area.
Carrying out repairs without preventing debris from falling onto pedestrians.

faqs.question: Must someone actually be injured?
faqs.answer: No. Creating probable danger to human life is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does the section apply to both demolition and repairs?
faqs.answer: Yes. It covers both activities.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Animal
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any animal in his possession as is sufficient to guard against probable danger to human life, or probable grievous hurt from such animal, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person may be punished for failing to properly control an animal that could endanger human life or cause serious injury.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Allowing a dangerous dog to roam freely in a public place.
Failing to secure a violent animal known to pose a risk.

faqs.question: Must the owner know the animal is dangerous?
faqs.answer: Knowledge or negligent failure to control the animal can attract liability.

faqs.question: Is actual injury necessary?
faqs.answer: No. The probable danger itself is sufficient.

title: Punishment for Public Nuisance in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever commits a public nuisance in any case not otherwise punishable by this Code shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for public nuisances when no specific punishment is provided elsewhere in the IPC.
punishment: Fine up to ₹200.
examples: Creating a nuisance affecting the public without falling under a more specific offence.
Maintaining a condition that causes widespread public inconvenience.

faqs.question: When does this section apply?
faqs.answer: When a public nuisance exists but no other IPC provision specifically prescribes punishment.

faqs.question: What is a public nuisance?
faqs.answer: It is defined under Section 268.

title: Continuance of Nuisance After Injunction to Discontinue
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever repeats or continues a public nuisance after an injunction or order has been issued by a public servant lawfully empowered to direct its discontinuance shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Continuing a public nuisance even after being legally ordered to stop it is a more serious offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: Continuing an illegal obstruction after receiving an official removal order.
Ignoring a lawful direction to stop activities causing public nuisance.

faqs.question: Must there be a prior order to stop the nuisance?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence arises after disobeying such an order.

faqs.question: Can imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Unlike Section 290, imprisonment is possible.

title: Sale, etc., of Obscene Books, etc.
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: A book, pamphlet, paper, writing, drawing, painting, representation, figure, electronic material, or any other object shall be deemed obscene if it is lascivious or appeals to prurient interest, or if its effect tends to deprave and corrupt persons likely to read, see, or hear it. Whoever sells, lets to hire, distributes, publicly exhibits, imports, exports, advertises, or possesses for such purposes any obscene material shall be punished as provided by law, subject to statutory exceptions.
simpleExplanation: Publishing, selling, distributing, or displaying obscene material is an offence, subject to legal exceptions for art, literature, science, or public good.
punishment: For first conviction: imprisonment up to 2 years and fine up to ₹2,000; enhanced punishment for subsequent convictions.
examples: Selling obscene publications for commercial gain.
Distributing obscene digital content prohibited by law.

faqs.question: Are artistic or educational works exempt?
faqs.answer: Certain works published for the public good in the interests of science, literature, art, or learning may fall within statutory exceptions.

faqs.question: Does the section cover electronic content?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic material is included.

title: Sale, etc., of Obscene Objects to Young Person
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever sells, lets to hire, distributes, exhibits, circulates, or attempts to do so, any obscene object to any person under the age of twenty years, shall be punished with imprisonment and fine as prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: Providing or selling obscene material to a person below twenty years of age is a criminal offence.
punishment: First conviction: imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹2,000. Subsequent conviction: imprisonment up to 7 years and fine up to ₹5,000.
examples: Selling obscene magazines to a 17-year-old.
Distributing obscene digital content to minors.

faqs.question: What age group is protected under this section?
faqs.answer: Persons under 20 years of age.

faqs.question: Does the section cover digital material?
faqs.answer: Yes, obscene electronic content may also be covered.

title: Obscene Acts and Songs
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever, to the annoyance of others, does any obscene act in any public place, or sings, recites, or utters any obscene song, ballad, or words in or near any public place, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Performing obscene acts or using obscene language, songs, or expressions in public places that annoy others is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine, or both.
examples: Performing obscene acts in a public park.
Using obscene songs or language loudly in a public place causing annoyance.

faqs.question: Must the act occur in a public place?
faqs.answer: Yes. Public location or proximity to a public place is essential.

faqs.question: Is annoyance to others required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act or words must cause annoyance.

title: Keeping Lottery Office
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever keeps any office or place for the purpose of drawing any lottery not being a State lottery or a lottery authorized by the Government, or publishes proposals to pay sums on tickets, chances, or shares in such lottery, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Running or promoting an unauthorized lottery operation is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: Operating an illegal lottery business without government authorization.
Publishing advertisements for unauthorized lottery schemes.

faqs.question: Are government-authorized lotteries covered?
faqs.answer: No. The section applies to unauthorized lotteries.

faqs.question: Is advertising an illegal lottery punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Publishing lottery proposals is specifically covered.

title: Injuring or Defiling Place of Worship with Intent to Insult Religion of Any Class
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever destroys, damages, or defiles any place of worship, or any object held sacred by any class of persons, with the intention of thereby insulting the religion of any class of persons or with the knowledge that such destruction, damage, or defilement is likely to be considered as an insult to their religion, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Damaging a place of worship or sacred object with the intention of insulting a religion is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Defacing a temple, mosque, church, or gurudwara to insult followers of that religion.
Destroying a sacred religious symbol with the intention of offending believers.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to insult religion?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent or knowledge that the act is likely to insult religion is required.

faqs.question: Does the section protect sacred objects as well as places?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sacred objects are expressly covered.

title: Deliberate and Malicious Acts Intended to Outrage Religious Feelings
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever, with deliberate and malicious intention of outraging the religious feelings of any class of citizens of India, by words, either spoken or written, or by signs, visible representations, electronic communication, or otherwise, insults or attempts to insult the religion or religious beliefs of that class, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Deliberately and maliciously insulting a religion or religious beliefs with the intent to outrage religious feelings is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Publishing content specifically intended to outrage religious sentiments.
Making malicious statements targeting a religion to provoke believers.

faqs.question: Is mere criticism of religion enough for this offence?
faqs.answer: No. The act must be deliberate and malicious with intent to outrage religious feelings.

faqs.question: Does the section apply to online content?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic communications are included.

title: Disturbing Religious Assembly
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes disturbance to any assembly lawfully engaged in the performance of religious worship or religious ceremonies shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally disrupting a lawful religious gathering or ceremony is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Interrupting a religious prayer gathering.
Creating a disturbance during a lawful religious ceremony.

faqs.question: Must the assembly be lawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. The gathering must be lawfully engaged in worship or religious ceremonies.

faqs.question: Is accidental disturbance punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The disturbance must be voluntary.

title: Trespassing on Burial Places, etc.
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever, with the intention of wounding the feelings of any person, or of insulting the religion of any person, or with the knowledge that the feelings of any person are likely to be wounded or that the religion of any person is likely to be insulted thereby, commits any trespass in any place of worship or on any place of sepulture, or offers indignity to any human corpse, or causes disturbance to persons assembled for funeral ceremonies, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Trespassing in burial grounds or places of worship, insulting a corpse, or disturbing funeral ceremonies with intent to offend religious feelings is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Vandalizing a cemetery to insult a religious group.
Disturbing a funeral procession with the intent to offend mourners.

faqs.question: Does the section cover burial grounds?
faqs.answer: Yes. Places of sepulture are specifically protected.

faqs.question: Is insulting a corpse covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Offering indignity to a human corpse is included.

title: Uttering Words, etc., with Deliberate Intent to Wound Religious Feelings
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever, with the deliberate intention of wounding the religious feelings of any person, utters any word or makes any sound in the hearing of that person, or makes any gesture in the sight of that person, or places any object in the sight of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Using words, sounds, gestures, or objects with the deliberate intention of hurting someone's religious feelings is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Making insulting gestures aimed at offending a person's religious beliefs.
Displaying offensive objects with the deliberate intention of hurting religious sentiments.

faqs.question: Must there be deliberate intent?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be done intentionally to wound religious feelings.

faqs.question: Can gestures alone amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gestures, sounds, words, or objects are all covered.

title: Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes death by doing an act with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, or with the knowledge that he is likely by such act to cause death, commits the offence of culpable homicide.
simpleExplanation: A person commits culpable homicide when they cause someone's death intentionally, or by causing an injury likely to result in death, or while knowing that death is likely.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Sections 304 and 308 depending on the circumstances.
examples: A person intentionally inflicts a serious injury knowing it is likely to cause death.
Someone performs a dangerous act with knowledge that it may result in death.

faqs.question: Is every culpable homicide murder?
faqs.answer: No. Murder is a more serious form of culpable homicide defined under Section 300.

faqs.question: Can knowledge alone be sufficient?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge that death is likely may constitute culpable homicide.

title: Murder
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Except in the cases hereinafter excepted, culpable homicide is murder if the act by which death is caused is done with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows is likely to cause death, or is sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or if the person knows the act is so imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause death.
simpleExplanation: Murder is the most serious form of unlawful killing and involves a high degree of intention or knowledge regarding the likelihood of causing death.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 302.
examples: Intentionally shooting a person with the aim of killing them.
Inflicting injuries that are sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death.

faqs.question: How is murder different from culpable homicide?
faqs.answer: Murder requires a higher degree of intention or knowledge and falls within the conditions specified in Section 300.

faqs.question: Are there exceptions to murder?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section contains exceptions such as grave and sudden provocation and certain acts in private defence.

title: Culpable Homicide by Causing Death of Person Other Than Person Whose Death Was Intended
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: If a person, by doing anything which he intends or knows to be likely to cause death, commits culpable homicide by causing the death of a person whose death he neither intends nor knows himself to be likely to cause, the culpable homicide committed is of the description of which it would have been if he had caused the death of the person whose death he intended or knew himself to be likely to cause.
simpleExplanation: A person remains liable if they intend to kill one person but accidentally kill another instead.
punishment: Punishment depends on whether the act amounts to murder or culpable homicide.
examples: A shoots at B intending to kill him but accidentally kills C standing nearby.
A bomb intended for one person kills another person.

faqs.question: What is transferred malice?
faqs.answer: The intention to harm one person is legally transferred to the actual victim.

faqs.question: Does accidental death excuse liability?
faqs.answer: No. Liability remains based on the original intention or knowledge.

title: Punishment for Murder
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes the punishment for the offence of murder.
punishment: Death penalty or imprisonment for life, and fine.
examples: A person intentionally kills another with premeditation.
An individual commits murder falling within Section 300.

faqs.question: Can a court impose life imprisonment instead of death?
faqs.answer: Yes. Either punishment may be imposed depending on the facts and judicial principles.

faqs.question: Is a fine also possible?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender may also be liable to a fine.

title: Punishment for Murder by Life Convict
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: This section provided a mandatory death sentence for a person serving a sentence of imprisonment for life who committed murder. The section was declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court of India in Mithu v. State of Punjab (1983) and is no longer enforceable.
simpleExplanation: This provision previously imposed a mandatory death sentence on life convicts who committed murder, but it has been struck down as unconstitutional.
punishment: Not enforceable; declared unconstitutional.
examples: The section is of historical significance only.
Courts no longer apply mandatory death penalties under this provision.

faqs.question: Is Section 303 still valid law?
faqs.answer: No. It was declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court.

faqs.question: Can a life convict still be punished for murder?
faqs.answer: Yes. Punishment is determined under the applicable murder provisions, not Section 303.

title: Punishment for Culpable Homicide Not Amounting to Murder
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever commits culpable homicide not amounting to murder shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine, if the act is done with the intention of causing death or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death; or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, or with fine, or with both, if the act is done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause death but without any intention to cause death or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death.
simpleExplanation: This section applies when a person causes death but the case does not amount to murder under Section 300.
punishment: Part I: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine., Part II: Imprisonment up to 10 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person causes death under grave and sudden provocation.
Someone performs a dangerous act knowing it may cause death, but without intending to kill.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Part I and Part II?
faqs.answer: Part I applies when there is intention; Part II applies when there is only knowledge.

faqs.question: Is life imprisonment possible?
faqs.answer: Yes, under Part I.

title: Causing Death by Negligence
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Causing someone's death through negligence or rashness, without intention or knowledge required for culpable homicide, is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A driver causes a fatal accident through negligent driving.
A doctor acts with gross negligence leading to a patient's death.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to road accidents?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is frequently applied in fatal road accident cases.

faqs.question: Is intention to cause death required?
faqs.answer: No. The offence is based on negligence or rashness.

title: Abetment of Suicide of Child or Insane Person
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: If any person under eighteen years of age, any insane person, delirious person, idiot, or intoxicated person commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Abetting the suicide of a child or a person who is mentally incapable of making rational decisions is a very serious offence.
punishment: Death, or imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Encouraging a minor to take their own life.
Manipulating a mentally unsound person into committing suicide.

faqs.question: Why is punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because the victims are considered especially vulnerable.

faqs.question: What is abetment?
faqs.answer: Instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aid in committing suicide.

title: Abetment of Suicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: If any person commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: A person who encourages, assists, or instigates another person to commit suicide commits an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Repeatedly instigating a person to end their life.
Providing assistance with the intention of facilitating suicide.

faqs.question: Must there be a direct connection between the conduct and suicide?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts generally require evidence of abetment.

faqs.question: Is mere harassment enough?
faqs.answer: Not always. The facts must establish abetment as defined in law.

title: Attempt to Murder
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge, and under such circumstances that, if by that act he caused death, he would be guilty of murder, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if hurt is caused, the offender may be punished with imprisonment for life.
simpleExplanation: A person commits attempt to murder when they try to kill another person but death does not occur.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; if hurt is caused, imprisonment for life may be imposed.
examples: Shooting at a person with intent to kill but missing the target.
Stabbing a person in a vital area who survives due to medical treatment.

faqs.question: Is actual death required?
faqs.answer: No. The offence is complete even if the victim survives.

faqs.question: How is intention determined?
faqs.answer: From the circumstances, weapon used, injuries inflicted, and conduct of the accused.

title: Attempt to Commit Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of culpable homicide not amounting to murder, shall be punished with imprisonment up to three years, or with fine, or with both; and if hurt is caused, with imprisonment up to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section applies when a person attempts an act that would amount to culpable homicide if death had occurred.
punishment: Up to 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both; if hurt is caused, up to 7 years imprisonment, or fine, or both.
examples: A person causes serious injury during a sudden fight under circumstances that would amount to culpable homicide if death occurred.
An individual performs a dangerous act likely to cause death, but the victim survives.

faqs.question: How is this different from attempt to murder?
faqs.answer: The intention or knowledge is lower than that required for murder.

faqs.question: Can punishment increase if injury occurs?
faqs.answer: Yes. The maximum punishment increases to seven years.

title: Attempt to Commit Suicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit suicide and does any act towards the commission of such offence shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section criminalized attempting suicide. However, under the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, a person attempting suicide is generally presumed to be under severe stress and should not ordinarily be prosecuted.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both (subject to the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017).
examples: A person attempts to take their own life but survives.
An individual performs an act intended to result in self-harm leading to death.

faqs.question: Is attempted suicide still punishable?
faqs.answer: The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 creates a presumption of severe stress and generally discourages prosecution.

faqs.question: What is the focus today?
faqs.answer: Mental health care, treatment, and rehabilitation rather than punishment.

title: Thug
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, at any time after the passing of this Act, shall have been habitually associated with any others for the purpose of committing robbery or child-stealing by means of or accompanied with murder, is a thug.
simpleExplanation: This section historically defined a 'thug' as a person habitually involved in organized robbery or child-stealing accompanied by murder.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: A member of a criminal gang habitually engaging in murderous robberies.
A participant in organized child abduction accompanied by murder.

faqs.question: Does this section create an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It primarily provides a definition used by Section 311.

faqs.question: Is this provision of historical significance?
faqs.answer: Yes. It relates to historical anti-thuggee laws.

title: Punishment
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever is a thug shall be punished with imprisonment for life and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribed punishment for persons classified as thugs under Section 310.
punishment: Imprisonment for life and fine.
examples: A person proved to be a thug under the historical definition could be punished under this section.
Members of organized murderous robbery groups were prosecuted under this provision.

faqs.question: What offence does this section punish?
faqs.answer: Being a thug as defined in Section 310.

faqs.question: Is the provision mainly historical today?
faqs.answer: Yes. It originated from anti-thuggee legislation during colonial times.

title: Causing Miscarriage
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes a woman with child to miscarry, shall, if such miscarriage is not caused in good faith for the purpose of saving the life of the woman, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both; and if the woman is quick with child, with imprisonment which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing a miscarriage without legal justification is an offence, except when done in good faith to save the woman's life. Modern reproductive health laws such as the Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act must also be considered.
punishment: Up to 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both; if the woman is quick with child, up to 7 years imprisonment and fine.
examples: A person unlawfully administers substances to induce miscarriage.
An individual causes miscarriage without lawful medical grounds.

faqs.question: Is every termination of pregnancy illegal?
faqs.answer: No. Legal medical terminations are governed by the Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act.

faqs.question: What does 'quick with child' mean?
faqs.answer: It refers to a stage of pregnancy where fetal movement is perceptible.

title: Causing Miscarriage Without Woman's Consent
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever commits the offence defined in Section 312 without the consent of the woman, whether the woman is quick with child or not, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing a miscarriage without the woman's consent is a very serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Administering drugs to terminate a pregnancy without the woman's knowledge.
Forcing a woman to undergo an abortion against her will.

faqs.question: Does the stage of pregnancy matter?
faqs.answer: No. Lack of consent makes the offence serious regardless of pregnancy stage.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically allows life imprisonment.

title: Death Caused by Act Done with Intent to Cause Miscarriage
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, with intent to cause the miscarriage of a woman with child, does any act which causes the death of such woman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the act is done without the consent of the woman, shall be punished either with imprisonment for life, or with the punishment above mentioned.
simpleExplanation: If a woman dies as a result of an act intended to cause miscarriage, the person responsible commits a serious offence, even if there was no intention to cause her death.
punishment: Up to 10 years imprisonment and fine; if done without the woman's consent, imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine.
examples: An illegal abortion procedure results in the death of the pregnant woman.
A person administers substances to induce miscarriage and the woman dies.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to kill the woman?
faqs.answer: No. Intent to cause miscarriage is sufficient if death results.

faqs.question: Does lack of consent affect punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lack of consent can result in life imprisonment.

title: Act Done with Intent to Prevent Child Being Born Alive or to Cause It to Die After Birth
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever before the birth of any child does any act with the intention of thereby preventing that child from being born alive, or causing it to die after its birth, and does by such act prevent that child from being born alive, or causes it to die after birth, shall, if such act be not caused in good faith for the purpose of saving the life of the mother, be punished with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally preventing a child from being born alive or causing its death after birth, except to save the mother's life, is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years, or fine, or both.
examples: An act intentionally performed to prevent a viable child from being born alive.
Causing the death of a child immediately after birth through a pre-planned act.

faqs.question: Is there any exception?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acts done in good faith to save the mother's life are excepted.

faqs.question: Must the act succeed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The child must be prevented from being born alive or die as a result.

title: Causing Death of Quick Unborn Child by Act Amounting to Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever does any act under such circumstances that, if he thereby caused death, he would be guilty of culpable homicide, and thereby causes the death of a quick unborn child, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing the death of an unborn child capable of movement through an act that would amount to culpable homicide if a person died is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A violent assault on a pregnant woman causes the death of the unborn child.
A dangerous act results in the death of a viable fetus.

faqs.question: What does 'quick unborn child' mean?
faqs.answer: Traditionally, it refers to a fetus whose movements are perceptible.

faqs.question: Must the act amount to culpable homicide?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be of a nature that would amount to culpable homicide if death occurred.

title: Exposure and Abandonment of Child Under Twelve Years by Parent or Person Having Care of It
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, being the father or mother of a child under the age of twelve years, or having the care of such child, exposes or leaves such child in any place with the intention of wholly abandoning such child, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Abandoning a child under twelve years of age with the intention of permanently leaving the child without care is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Leaving a young child in a public place with no intention of returning.
Abandoning a child at an isolated location.

faqs.question: Does the child need to suffer harm?
faqs.answer: No. The offence is complete upon intentional abandonment.

faqs.question: Who can commit this offence?
faqs.answer: A parent or any person having care of the child.

title: Concealment of Birth by Secret Disposal of Dead Body
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, by secretly burying or otherwise disposing of the dead body of a child, whether such child died before, during, or after birth, intentionally conceals or endeavors to conceal the birth of such child, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Secretly disposing of the body of a child to conceal the fact of birth is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Secretly burying a newborn child's body to hide the birth.
Disposing of a child's body to prevent authorities from discovering the birth.

faqs.question: Does it matter whether the child died before or after birth?
faqs.answer: No. The section applies in either case.

faqs.question: What is the main offence here?
faqs.answer: Concealing the birth by secretly disposing of the body.

title: Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes bodily pain, disease or infirmity to any person is said to cause hurt.
simpleExplanation: A person causes hurt when they inflict physical pain, disease, or bodily weakness on another person.
punishment: Definition section only. Punishment is prescribed under later sections.
examples: Punching someone and causing pain.
Infecting another person with a disease through an unlawful act.

faqs.question: Does hurt require a visible injury?
faqs.answer: No. Bodily pain, disease, or infirmity is sufficient.

faqs.question: Is this an offence by itself?
faqs.answer: This section defines hurt; punishment is provided in later sections.

title: Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: The following kinds of hurt only are designated as grievous: emasculation; permanent privation of sight of either eye; permanent privation of hearing of either ear; privation of any member or joint; destruction or permanent impairment of powers of any member or joint; permanent disfiguration of head or face; fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth; and any hurt endangering life or causing severe bodily pain or inability to follow ordinary pursuits for twenty days.
simpleExplanation: This section lists the injuries considered serious enough to be classified as grievous hurt.
punishment: Definition section only. Punishment is prescribed under Sections 325, 326, etc.
examples: Breaking someone's arm.
Causing permanent loss of vision in one eye.

faqs.question: Is every injury grievous hurt?
faqs.answer: No. Only injuries falling within the categories listed in Section 320.

faqs.question: Is a fractured tooth grievous hurt?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fracture of a tooth is specifically included.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever does any act with the intention of thereby causing hurt to any person, or with the knowledge that he is likely thereby to cause hurt to any person, and does thereby cause hurt to any person, is said voluntarily to cause hurt.
simpleExplanation: A person voluntarily causes hurt when they intentionally or knowingly perform an act that results in hurt.
punishment: Punishment provided under Section 323.
examples: Intentionally slapping another person.
Throwing an object knowing it is likely to cause injury.

faqs.question: Is intention necessary?
faqs.answer: Intention or knowledge that hurt is likely is sufficient.

faqs.question: Must hurt actually occur?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must result in hurt.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt, if the hurt which he intends to cause or knows himself to be likely to cause is grievous hurt, and if the hurt actually caused is grievous hurt, is said voluntarily to cause grievous hurt.
simpleExplanation: A person voluntarily causes grievous hurt when they intend or know that a serious injury is likely and such grievous injury actually occurs.
punishment: Punishment provided under Sections 325 and 326.
examples: Intentionally causing a fracture to another person.
Inflicting injuries resulting in permanent loss of sight.

faqs.question: Must grievous hurt actually result?
faqs.answer: Yes. The injury caused must qualify as grievous hurt.

faqs.question: Is intention important?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intention or knowledge regarding grievous hurt is required.

title: Punishment for Voluntarily Causing Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, except in the case provided for by Section 334, voluntarily causes hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for intentionally causing ordinary hurt.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A person intentionally punches another causing pain.
An individual slaps another person during an argument.

faqs.question: Does this apply to grievous hurt?
faqs.answer: No. Grievous hurt is punished under separate sections.

faqs.question: Can a fine alone be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Depending on the facts, the court may impose a fine.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt by Dangerous Weapons or Means
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, except in the case provided for by Section 334, voluntarily causes hurt by means of any instrument for shooting, stabbing, or cutting, or any instrument which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, or by means of fire, heated substance, poison, corrosive substance, explosive substance, or any substance dangerous to the human body, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Causing hurt using dangerous weapons, poison, fire, explosives, or other dangerous means is a more serious offence than ordinary hurt.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Attacking someone with a knife causing injury.
Throwing a corrosive chemical that causes hurt but not grievous injury.

faqs.question: What are dangerous weapons?
faqs.answer: Weapons such as knives, firearms, and other instruments likely to cause death.

faqs.question: Is actual injury required?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hurt must be caused.

title: Punishment for Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, except in the case provided for by Section 335, voluntarily causes grievous hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for intentionally causing grievous hurt.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Breaking another person's arm during an assault.
Causing permanent loss of hearing in one ear.

faqs.question: What qualifies as grievous hurt?
faqs.answer: The injuries listed in Section 320.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender is also liable to fine.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt by Dangerous Weapons or Means
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, except in the case provided for by Section 335, voluntarily causes grievous hurt by means of any instrument for shooting, stabbing, or cutting, or by means of fire, poison, corrosive substances, explosives, or any means likely to cause death, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing grievous hurt using dangerous weapons or dangerous means is a very serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Using a sword to inflict a fracture.
Causing permanent disability through a corrosive substance.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically provides for life imprisonment.

faqs.question: Must grievous hurt actually occur?
faqs.answer: Yes. The injury caused must qualify as grievous hurt.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt by Use of Acid, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes permanent or partial damage, deformity, burns, maims, disfigurement, disability, or grievous hurt by throwing acid on, or administering acid to, any person, or by using any other means with the intention of causing such injury, shall be punished with imprisonment of not less than ten years, which may extend to imprisonment for life, and with fine.
simpleExplanation: Acid attacks causing serious physical damage are among the gravest offences against the human body.
punishment: Minimum 10 years imprisonment, extendable to life imprisonment, and fine.
examples: Throwing acid on a person's face causing permanent disfigurement.
Administering acid with intent to cause serious injury.

faqs.question: Can the sentence be less than 10 years?
faqs.answer: No. The law prescribes a minimum sentence of 10 years.

faqs.question: What injuries are covered?
faqs.answer: Burns, deformity, disability, disfigurement, and other grievous injuries.

title: Voluntarily Throwing or Attempting to Throw Acid
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever throws or attempts to throw acid on any person, or attempts to administer acid to any person, with the intention of causing permanent or partial damage, deformity, burns, maims, disfigurement, disability, or grievous hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of not less than five years but which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Attempting an acid attack is punishable even if serious injury does not occur.
punishment: Minimum 5 years imprisonment, extendable to 7 years, and fine.
examples: Attempting to throw acid at a person but missing the target.
Trying to administer acid with intent to cause disfigurement.

faqs.question: Must injury actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. An attempt itself is punishable.

faqs.question: What is the minimum punishment?
faqs.answer: Five years imprisonment.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt to Extort Property, or to Constrain to an Illegal Act
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt for the purpose of extorting from the sufferer, or from any person interested in the sufferer, any property or valuable security, or of constraining the sufferer or any person interested in the sufferer to do anything which is illegal or which may facilitate the commission of an offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing hurt to force someone to hand over property, valuables, or perform an illegal act is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Beating a person to force them to sign over property.
Injuring someone to compel payment of money.

faqs.question: Must property actually be obtained?
faqs.answer: No. The offence focuses on the purpose behind causing hurt.

faqs.question: Can forcing an illegal act also attract liability?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section covers coercion to commit illegal acts.

title: Causing Hurt by Means of Poison, etc., with Intent to Commit an Offence
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever administers to, or causes to be taken by, any person any poison, intoxicating, stupefying, or unwholesome drug, or other thing with intent to cause hurt, or with intent to commit or facilitate the commission of an offence, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Poisoning or drugging a person to cause harm or facilitate another offence is a serious crime.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Drugging a victim before committing robbery.
Administering poison to cause injury.

faqs.question: Does the offence require permanent injury?
faqs.answer: No. Administering the substance with the required intent is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does this apply to intoxicants?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intoxicating and stupefying substances are included.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt to Extort Property, or to Constrain to an Illegal Act
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt for the purpose of extorting property or valuable security, or of constraining any person to do anything illegal or facilitating an offence, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing grievous hurt to extort property or force illegal actions is an extremely serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Breaking a person's limb to force transfer of land ownership.
Inflicting grievous injuries to compel execution of a document.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically permits life imprisonment.

faqs.question: Must grievous hurt occur?
faqs.answer: Yes. The injury must qualify under Section 320.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt to Extort Confession, or to Compel Restoration of Property
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt for the purpose of extorting from the sufferer, or from any person interested in the sufferer, any confession or information which may lead to the detection of an offence or misconduct, or for compelling restoration of property or valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Using physical violence to force a confession, obtain information, or recover property is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Assaulting a suspect to force a confession.
Beating someone to reveal the location of stolen property.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to custodial violence?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is often relevant in cases involving coercive interrogation.

faqs.question: Must a confession actually be obtained?
faqs.answer: No. The purpose behind causing hurt is sufficient.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt to Extort Confession, or to Compel Restoration of Property
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt for the purpose of extorting confession, information, or restoration of property shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing grievous hurt to obtain a confession or information is a grave offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Inflicting serious injuries during custodial interrogation.
Breaking bones to force disclosure of information.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 330?
faqs.answer: Section 331 applies when grievous hurt is caused instead of ordinary hurt.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt to Deter Public Servant from His Duty
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt to any person being a public servant in the discharge of his duty as such public servant, or with intent to prevent or deter that person from discharging his duty, or in consequence of anything done by that person in the lawful discharge of his duty, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Assaulting or injuring a public servant to stop them from performing official duties is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Attacking a police officer during an arrest.
Injuring a government official to prevent official action.

faqs.question: Must the victim be a public servant?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically protects public servants.

faqs.question: Does it apply after the duty is performed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Retaliation for lawful official acts is also covered.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt to Deter Public Servant from His Duty
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt to any person being a public servant in the discharge of his duty as such public servant, or with intent to prevent or deter that person from discharging his duty, or in consequence of anything done by that person in the lawful discharge of his duty, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing grievous hurt to a public servant while they are performing official duties, or in retaliation for those duties, is a serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Breaking a police officer's arm during an arrest.
Attacking a government official in retaliation for lawful action.

faqs.question: Does the injury have to be grievous?
faqs.answer: Yes. The injury must qualify as grievous hurt under Section 320.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section expressly permits life imprisonment.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt on Provocation
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt on grave and sudden provocation, if he neither intends nor knows himself to be likely to cause hurt to any person other than the person who gave the provocation, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Where hurt is caused because of grave and sudden provocation, the law provides a lesser punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: A person immediately reacts to a serious insult and causes minor injury.
Someone strikes the person who gave grave and sudden provocation.

faqs.question: Can the provocation be self-created?
faqs.answer: No. The provocation must come from another person.

faqs.question: Does this apply if a third person is injured?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The provision is limited to the person who gave the provocation.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt on Provocation
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt on grave and sudden provocation, if he neither intends nor knows himself to be likely to cause grievous hurt to any person other than the person who gave the provocation, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to four years, or with fine which may extend to two thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: The law provides reduced punishment where grievous hurt is caused because of grave and sudden provocation.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 4 years, or fine up to ₹2,000, or both.
examples: A person fractures another's arm immediately after grave and sudden provocation.
An individual reacts violently to an extreme provocation causing grievous injury.

faqs.question: Why is punishment reduced?
faqs.answer: Because the law recognizes the impact of grave and sudden provocation.

faqs.question: Must grievous hurt occur?
faqs.answer: Yes. The injury must qualify under Section 320.

title: Act Endangering Life or Personal Safety of Others
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred and fifty rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Performing a rash or negligent act that endangers others is an offence even if no injury actually occurs.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹250, or both.
examples: Throwing heavy objects from a rooftop into a crowded street.
Driving recklessly in a populated area.

faqs.question: Must someone be injured?
faqs.answer: No. Mere endangerment is sufficient.

faqs.question: What kind of conduct is covered?
faqs.answer: Rash or negligent acts creating risk to human life or safety.

title: Causing Hurt by Act Endangering Life or Personal Safety of Others
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes hurt to any person by doing any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person commits this offence when a rash or negligent act causes hurt to another person.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: Negligent driving causing minor injuries to a pedestrian.
Unsafe handling of machinery resulting in injury.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 336?
faqs.answer: Section 337 requires actual hurt, while Section 336 only requires endangerment.

faqs.question: Must the act be rash or negligent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rashness or negligence is essential.

title: Causing Grievous Hurt by Act Endangering Life or Personal Safety of Others
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes grievous hurt to any person by doing any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person commits this offence when a rash or negligent act results in grievous hurt.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Negligent driving causing multiple fractures.
Unsafe industrial operations resulting in grievous injuries.

faqs.question: What type of injury is required?
faqs.answer: The injury must qualify as grievous hurt under Section 320.

faqs.question: How is it different from Section 337?
faqs.answer: Section 338 requires grievous hurt rather than ordinary hurt.

title: Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed, is said wrongfully to restrain that person.
simpleExplanation: A person commits wrongful restraint when they unlawfully prevent someone from moving in a direction they have a legal right to go.
punishment: Definition section only. Punishment is prescribed under Section 341.
examples: Blocking a person's path on a public road.
Standing in front of a doorway to prevent someone from leaving.

faqs.question: Does physical force have to be used?
faqs.answer: No. Any voluntary obstruction may amount to wrongful restraint.

faqs.question: Must the person have a right to proceed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The restriction must interfere with a lawful right of movement.

title: Wrongful Confinement
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person in such a manner as to prevent that person from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits, is said wrongfully to confine that person.
simpleExplanation: Wrongful confinement occurs when a person is completely restricted within a particular area and cannot leave.
punishment: Definition section only. Punishment is prescribed under Section 342.
examples: Locking someone inside a room against their will.
Preventing a person from leaving a building.

faqs.question: How is wrongful confinement different from wrongful restraint?
faqs.answer: Confinement completely restricts movement within a boundary, while restraint only prevents movement in a particular direction.

faqs.question: Must the confinement be total?
faqs.answer: Yes. The victim must be unable to leave the confined area.

title: Punishment for Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for unlawfully obstructing a person's movement.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: Blocking access to a public pathway.
Preventing someone from entering or leaving a place where they have a right to go.

faqs.question: What offence is punished here?
faqs.answer: Wrongful restraint as defined in Section 339.

faqs.question: Can imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to one month of simple imprisonment.

title: Punishment for Wrongful Confinement
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes unlawfully confining a person within fixed boundaries.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Locking a person inside a room.
Detaining someone in a building against their will.

faqs.question: What is required for wrongful confinement?
faqs.answer: The victim must be prevented from leaving a confined area.

faqs.question: Can both imprisonment and fine be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Wrongful Confinement for Three or More Days
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for three days or more shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Keeping a person wrongfully confined for three or more days attracts enhanced punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Detaining a person in a room for several days.
Holding someone captive for more than three days.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher?
faqs.answer: Because the confinement continues for a significant period.

faqs.question: Must the confinement be uninterrupted?
faqs.answer: Yes. The wrongful confinement must continue for three days or more.

title: Wrongful Confinement for Ten or More Days
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for ten days or more shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Wrongfully confining a person for ten days or longer is treated as a more serious form of confinement.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Holding a person captive in a building for two weeks.
Preventing someone from leaving a location for more than ten days.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because prolonged confinement results in a greater deprivation of personal liberty.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender is also liable to fine.

title: Wrongful Confinement of Person for Whose Liberation Writ Has Been Issued
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for whose liberation a writ has been issued, or whose liberation has been directed by a competent public authority, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years in addition to any other punishment to which he may be liable for such wrongful confinement.
simpleExplanation: Continuing to confine a person even after a court or lawful authority has ordered their release is a serious offence.
punishment: Additional imprisonment up to 2 years, besides punishment for wrongful confinement.
examples: Ignoring a court order directing the release of a confined person.
Refusing to release a detainee despite lawful instructions.

faqs.question: Must there be an official release order?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies when a writ or lawful release order exists.

faqs.question: Is this punishment separate from wrongful confinement?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is additional to other applicable punishment.

title: Wrongful Confinement in Secret
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person in such a manner as to indicate an intention that the confinement of such person may not be known to any person interested in that person, or to any public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years in addition to any other punishment to which he may be liable for wrongful confinement.
simpleExplanation: Secretly confining a person and concealing the confinement from family members or authorities is an aggravated offence.
punishment: Additional imprisonment up to 2 years, besides punishment for wrongful confinement.
examples: Keeping a person locked in a hidden room.
Concealing the location of a confined person from relatives and authorities.

faqs.question: What makes confinement secret?
faqs.answer: The intention to prevent others from knowing about the confinement.

faqs.question: Is this punishment additional?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is imposed in addition to punishment for wrongful confinement.

title: Wrongful Confinement to Extort Property, or Constrain to Illegal Act
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for the purpose of extorting from the confined person, or from any person interested in the confined person, any property or valuable security, or of constraining the confined person or any person interested in the confined person to do anything illegal or facilitating the commission of an offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Confining a person to force payment, transfer of property, or performance of illegal acts is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Holding a person captive until ransom is paid.
Confining someone to force them to sign property documents.

faqs.question: Must property actually be obtained?
faqs.answer: No. The purpose of confinement is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can this apply to ransom situations?
faqs.answer: Yes. Confinement for extortion is covered.

title: Wrongful Confinement to Extort Confession, or Compel Restoration of Property
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for the purpose of extorting from the confined person, or from any person interested in the confined person, any confession or information which may lead to the detection of an offence or misconduct, or for compelling restoration of property or valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Confining a person to force a confession, obtain information, or recover property is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Detaining a suspect illegally to obtain a confession.
Locking a person up until they reveal information about missing property.

faqs.question: Does this apply to custodial torture situations?
faqs.answer: It can apply where confinement is used to obtain confessions or information unlawfully.

faqs.question: Must a confession actually be obtained?
faqs.answer: No. The purpose of confinement is sufficient.

title: Force
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: A person is said to use force to another if he causes motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion to that other person, or to any substance, in such a manner that the motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion brings that substance into contact with any part of that person's body, clothing, or anything carried by that person.
simpleExplanation: This section defines 'force' as causing physical movement or contact affecting another person or things connected with them.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: Pushing a person.
Throwing an object that strikes someone's body.

faqs.question: Does force require direct physical contact?
faqs.answer: No. Force may be applied indirectly through objects or substances.

faqs.question: Is this itself an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It is a definition used in later sections.

title: Criminal Force
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever intentionally uses force to any person, without that person's consent, in order to commit any offence, or intending by the use of such force to cause injury, fear, or annoyance, is said to use criminal force.
simpleExplanation: Force becomes criminal force when it is intentionally used without consent to commit an offence or to cause injury, fear, or annoyance.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: Pushing someone during a dispute to intimidate them.
Forcibly grabbing a person without consent.

faqs.question: Is lack of consent necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Criminal force requires absence of consent.

faqs.question: Must injury occur?
faqs.answer: No. Intention to cause fear or annoyance may be sufficient.

title: Assault
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that criminal force is about to be used against that person, is said to commit an assault.
simpleExplanation: Assault occurs when a person's actions or gestures create a reasonable fear that criminal force is about to be used.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: Raising a fist as if to strike someone.
Pointing a weapon at a person in a threatening manner.

faqs.question: Must physical contact occur?
faqs.answer: No. Fear of imminent criminal force is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can gestures alone amount to assault?
faqs.answer: Yes. Gestures creating apprehension are covered.

title: Punishment for Assault or Criminal Force Otherwise Than on Grave Provocation
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation given by that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for assault or criminal force in ordinary circumstances.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: Slapping another person during an argument.
Using criminal force without legal justification.

faqs.question: What if there was grave and sudden provocation?
faqs.answer: Different provisions may apply where grave and sudden provocation exists.

faqs.question: Does this require injury?
faqs.answer: No. Assault or criminal force itself is punishable.

title: Assault or Criminal Force to Deter Public Servant from Discharge of Duty
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person being a public servant in the execution of duty as such public servant, or with intent to prevent or deter that person from discharging duty, or in consequence of anything done by that person in the lawful discharge of duty, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Assaulting or using criminal force against a public servant performing official duties is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Pushing a police officer during an arrest.
Assaulting a government official to prevent official action.

faqs.question: Must the victim be a public servant?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically protects public servants.

faqs.question: Does retaliation after official action count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Retaliatory assaults are also covered.

title: Assault or Criminal Force to Woman with Intent to Outrage Her Modesty
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any woman, intending to outrage or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby outrage her modesty, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term not less than one year but which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Using criminal force or assaulting a woman with the intention of violating her dignity or modesty is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment of not less than 1 year and up to 5 years, and fine.
examples: Inappropriately touching a woman without consent.
Using force against a woman with sexual intent.

faqs.question: Is physical contact necessary?
faqs.answer: The offence generally involves assault or criminal force directed at a woman.

faqs.question: Is intent important?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intent to outrage modesty, or knowledge that it is likely, is required.

title: Sexual Harassment and Punishment for Sexual Harassment
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: A man committing any of the following acts—physical contact and advances involving unwelcome and explicit sexual overtures; a demand or request for sexual favours; showing pornography against the will of a woman; or making sexually coloured remarks—commits the offence of sexual harassment.
simpleExplanation: This section criminalizes various forms of sexual harassment, including unwanted sexual advances, demands for sexual favours, showing pornography, and sexually coloured remarks.
punishment: Physical contact, sexual favours, pornography: Rigorous imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both., Sexually coloured remarks: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Demanding sexual favours in exchange for workplace benefits.
Making repeated sexually explicit comments toward a woman.

faqs.question: Does workplace harassment fall under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sexual harassment at the workplace may attract liability under this section and other applicable laws.

faqs.question: Are verbal remarks covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sexually coloured remarks are specifically included.

title: Assault or Use of Criminal Force to Woman with Intent to Disrobe
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Any man who assaults or uses criminal force to any woman or abets such act with the intention of disrobing or compelling her to be naked shall be punished with imprisonment of not less than three years but which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Attempting to remove a woman's clothes or force her to be naked through assault or criminal force is a grave offence.
punishment: Imprisonment of not less than 3 years and up to 7 years, and fine.
examples: Attempting to tear a woman's clothes during an assault.
Using force to compel a woman to undress.

faqs.question: Must disrobing actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. The intent and conduct are sufficient.

faqs.question: Can abetment also be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Abetting the act is specifically covered.

title: Voyeurism
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Any man who watches, captures the image of, or disseminates the image of a woman engaging in a private act where she would usually have the expectation of not being observed, shall be guilty of the offence of voyeurism.
simpleExplanation: Secretly watching, photographing, recording, or sharing images of a woman engaged in a private act without consent is an offence.
punishment: First Conviction: Imprisonment not less than 1 year and up to 3 years, and fine., Subsequent Conviction: Imprisonment not less than 3 years and up to 7 years, and fine.
examples: Secretly recording a woman in a changing room.
Sharing private images of a woman without consent.

faqs.question: What is a private act?
faqs.answer: An act where a person reasonably expects privacy.

faqs.question: Is sharing recorded content also punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Disseminating such images is covered.

title: Stalking
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
