officialText: Any man who follows a woman and contacts or attempts to contact such woman repeatedly despite a clear indication of disinterest, or monitors the use by a woman of the internet, email, or any other form of electronic communication, commits the offence of stalking, subject to statutory exceptions.
simpleExplanation: Repeatedly following, contacting, or electronically monitoring a woman against her wishes is an offence.
punishment: First Conviction: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine., Subsequent Conviction: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
examples: Repeatedly following a woman despite being told to stop.
Monitoring a woman's online activities without justification.

faqs.question: Can online stalking be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Monitoring electronic communications is specifically included.

faqs.question: Are there exceptions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain lawful activities such as crime prevention may be exempt.

title: Assault or Criminal Force with Intent to Dishonour Person, Otherwise Than on Grave Provocation
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person, intending thereby to dishonour that person, otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation given by that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Using assault or criminal force with the intention of insulting, humiliating, or dishonouring a person is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Slapping a person publicly to humiliate them.
Throwing an object at someone to insult and degrade them.

faqs.question: Must there be an intention to dishonour?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be intended to insult or humiliate the victim.

faqs.question: Does grave and sudden provocation affect this offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section excludes cases involving grave and sudden provocation.

title: Assault or Criminal Force in Attempt to Commit Theft of Property Carried by a Person
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person in attempting to commit theft of any property carried by or attached to the person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Using assault or criminal force while attempting to steal property being carried by a person is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Pushing someone while trying to snatch their mobile phone.
Using force to steal a handbag from a person's shoulder.

faqs.question: Must the theft succeed?
faqs.answer: No. An attempt combined with assault or criminal force is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does the property need to be carried by the victim?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically covers property carried by or attached to a person.

title: Assault or Criminal Force in Attempt Wrongfully to Confine a Person
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person in attempting wrongfully to confine that person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Using assault or criminal force while attempting to wrongfully confine a person is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Forcing someone toward a locked room against their will.
Using physical force to prevent a person from escaping confinement.

faqs.question: Must the confinement actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. An attempt combined with assault or criminal force is sufficient.

faqs.question: What underlying offence is involved?
faqs.answer: Wrongful confinement as defined under Section 340.

title: Assault or Criminal Force on Grave Provocation
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person on grave and sudden provocation given by that person shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees, or with both.
simpleExplanation: The law provides a lesser punishment when assault or criminal force is used immediately after grave and sudden provocation.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹200, or both.
examples: A person immediately reacts to a serious provocation by pushing the provocateur.
An individual strikes the person who gave grave and sudden provocation.

faqs.question: Why is punishment lower?
faqs.answer: The law recognizes that grave and sudden provocation may reduce moral blameworthiness.

faqs.question: Can the provocation come from another person?
faqs.answer: Yes. The provocation must be given by the person against whom force is used.

title: Kidnapping
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Kidnapping is of two kinds: kidnapping from India, and kidnapping from lawful guardianship.
simpleExplanation: This section classifies kidnapping into two categories: kidnapping from India and kidnapping from lawful guardianship.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: Taking a child away from lawful guardianship.
Removing a person from India without lawful authority.

faqs.question: Does this section create an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It defines the two forms of kidnapping.

faqs.question: Where are the punishments provided?
faqs.answer: Punishments are provided in subsequent sections.

title: Kidnapping from India
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever conveys any person beyond the limits of India without the consent of that person, or of some person legally authorized to consent on behalf of that person, is said to kidnap that person from India.
simpleExplanation: Taking a person out of India without lawful consent amounts to kidnapping from India.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 363.
examples: Taking a child abroad without parental consent.
Forcibly transporting a person outside India.

faqs.question: Is consent important?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lack of valid consent is essential to the offence.

faqs.question: Can guardians provide consent for minors?
faqs.answer: Yes. Legally authorized persons may provide consent where applicable.

title: Kidnapping from Lawful Guardianship
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever takes or entices any minor under sixteen years of age if a male, or under eighteen years of age if a female, or any person of unsound mind, out of the keeping of the lawful guardian of such minor or person of unsound mind, without the consent of such guardian, is said to kidnap such minor or person from lawful guardianship.
simpleExplanation: Taking or enticing a minor or a person of unsound mind away from their lawful guardian without consent amounts to kidnapping from lawful guardianship.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 363.
examples: Luring a minor away from parents without permission.
Taking a child from school without the guardian's consent.

faqs.question: Is force necessary for kidnapping?
faqs.answer: No. Enticement alone may be sufficient.

faqs.question: Who is a lawful guardian?
faqs.answer: A person legally entrusted with the care or custody of the minor or person of unsound mind.

title: Abduction
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever by force compels, or by any deceitful means induces, any person to go from any place, is said to abduct that person.
simpleExplanation: Abduction occurs when a person is compelled by force or induced by deception to move from one place to another.
punishment: Definition section only. Punishment depends on the purpose of abduction under later sections.
examples: Forcing a person into a vehicle.
Tricking someone into traveling somewhere against their interests.

faqs.question: How is abduction different from kidnapping?
faqs.answer: Abduction involves force or deceit, while kidnapping may occur through taking or enticing minors without guardian consent.

faqs.question: Is abduction itself punishable?
faqs.answer: Punishment depends on the purpose for which the abduction is committed.

title: Punishment for Kidnapping
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps any person from India or from lawful guardianship shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for kidnapping from India or from lawful guardianship.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Taking a child away from lawful guardians without consent.
Removing a person from India without lawful authority.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender is also liable to fine.

faqs.question: Which forms of kidnapping are covered?
faqs.answer: Kidnapping from India and kidnapping from lawful guardianship.

title: Kidnapping or Maiming a Minor for Purposes of Begging
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps or otherwise obtains custody of a minor in order that such minor may be employed or used for the purpose of begging, or maims a minor for the purpose of begging, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping, obtaining custody of, or maiming a child for the purpose of begging is a grave offence.
punishment: Kidnapping for begging: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine., Maiming for begging: Imprisonment for life and fine.
examples: Abducting children and forcing them to beg.
Deliberately injuring a child to increase sympathy while begging.

faqs.question: Who is considered a minor?
faqs.answer: A person under 18 years of age.

faqs.question: Is actual begging required?
faqs.answer: No. The intention to use the child for begging is sufficient.

title: Kidnapping or Abducting in Order to Murder
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps or abducts any person in order that such person may be murdered, or may be so disposed of as to be put in danger of being murdered, shall be punished with imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping or abducting a person with the intention that they be murdered is a very serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Abducting a person and transporting them to a place for execution.
Kidnapping someone and handing them over to persons intending to kill them.

faqs.question: Must murder actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. The intention or purpose is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Kidnapping for Ransom
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps or abducts any person or keeps a person in detention after such kidnapping or abduction and threatens to cause death or hurt, or causes apprehension of death or hurt, in order to compel the Government, any foreign State, international organization, or any other person to do or abstain from doing any act or to pay a ransom, shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping a person and demanding ransom or coercing action through threats is one of the most serious kidnapping offences.
punishment: Death penalty or imprisonment for life, and fine.
examples: Kidnapping a child and demanding money for release.
Abducting a person to force government action.

faqs.question: Is ransom necessary?
faqs.answer: Ransom or coercion through threats is a key element.

faqs.question: Can the death penalty be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically permits it.

title: Kidnapping or Abducting with Intent Secretly and Wrongfully to Confine Person
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps or abducts any person with intent to cause that person to be secretly and wrongfully confined shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping or abducting someone to secretly confine them is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Abducting a person and keeping them hidden in a remote location.
Kidnapping someone to detain them unlawfully.

faqs.question: Must confinement actually occur?
faqs.answer: The intent to secretly and wrongfully confine is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Kidnapping, Abducting or Inducing Woman to Compel Her Marriage, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps or abducts any woman with intent that she may be compelled to marry any person against her will, or knowing it to be likely that she will be compelled to marry, or forced or seduced to illicit intercourse, shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping or abducting a woman to force marriage or sexual exploitation is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Abducting a woman for forced marriage.
Kidnapping a woman for sexual exploitation.

faqs.question: Does the woman have to be married?
faqs.answer: No. The section covers compelling marriage against her will.

faqs.question: Is intent important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence depends on the unlawful purpose behind the kidnapping.

title: Procuration of Minor Girl
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever induces any minor girl under the age of eighteen years to go from any place or to do any act with intent that she may be forced or seduced to illicit intercourse with another person shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
simpleExplanation: Inducing a minor girl for sexual exploitation is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Luring a minor girl for trafficking purposes.
Persuading a minor to travel for sexual exploitation.

faqs.question: Who is protected under this section?
faqs.answer: Girls below 18 years of age.

faqs.question: Must exploitation actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. The unlawful intent is sufficient.

title: Importation of Girl from Foreign Country
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever imports into India from any country outside India or from the State of Jammu and Kashmir any girl under the age of twenty-one years with intent that she may be, or knowing it to be likely that she will be, forced or seduced to illicit intercourse with another person shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to ten years and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Bringing a young woman or girl into India for sexual exploitation is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Trafficking a young woman from another country for sexual exploitation.
Arranging transport of a girl into India knowing she will be exploited.

faqs.question: What is the age limit under this section?
faqs.answer: Girls under 21 years of age.

faqs.question: Must exploitation actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Intent or knowledge of likely exploitation is sufficient.

title: Kidnapping or Abducting in Order to Subject Person to Grievous Hurt, Slavery, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps or abducts any person in order that such person may be subjected, or may be so disposed of as to be put in danger of being subjected, to grievous hurt, slavery, or the unnatural lust of any person, shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping a person for slavery, grievous hurt, or sexual exploitation is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Abducting a person for forced labour.
Kidnapping a person to subject them to severe physical abuse.

faqs.question: Is actual harm required?
faqs.answer: No. The unlawful purpose behind the kidnapping is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does slavery include forced labour?
faqs.answer: Yes. Forced labour may fall within the scope of slavery-related exploitation.

title: Wrongfully Concealing or Keeping in Confinement Kidnapped or Abducted Person
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, knowing that any person has been kidnapped or abducted, wrongfully conceals or confines such person, shall be punished in the same manner as if he had kidnapped or abducted such person with the same intention or knowledge.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly hides or confines a kidnapped or abducted person is treated similarly to the kidnapper.
punishment: Same punishment as applicable to the underlying kidnapping or abduction offence.
examples: Providing a secret location to keep a kidnapped victim.
Knowingly hiding an abducted person from authorities.

faqs.question: Must the person know about the kidnapping?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is an essential element of the offence.

faqs.question: How is punishment determined?
faqs.answer: It depends on the offence for which the victim was kidnapped or abducted.

title: Kidnapping or Abducting Child Under Ten Years with Intent to Steal from Its Person
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps or abducts any child under ten years of age with the intention of taking dishonestly any movable property from the person of such child shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping a young child for the purpose of stealing property carried by the child is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Abducting a child to steal jewellery worn by the child.
Taking a child away to remove valuable items from their possession.

faqs.question: What age of child is covered?
faqs.answer: Children under 10 years of age.

faqs.question: Must the theft actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. The intention to steal from the child is sufficient.

title: Trafficking of Persons
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever, for the purpose of exploitation, recruits, transports, harbours, transfers, or receives a person by using threats, force, coercion, abduction, fraud, deception, abuse of power, or inducement, commits the offence of trafficking. Exploitation includes physical exploitation, sexual exploitation, slavery, servitude, forced labour, or removal of organs.
simpleExplanation: Human trafficking involves recruiting, transporting, or controlling people for exploitation through force, deception, coercion, or abuse of power.
punishment: One person: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 7 years, up to 10 years, and fine., More than one person: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years, up to life imprisonment, and fine., Minor victim: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years, up to life imprisonment, and fine., Multiple minor victims: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 14 years, up to life imprisonment, and fine., Public servant involved: Life imprisonment and fine.
examples: Recruiting people with false job promises and forcing them into labour.
Transporting women for sexual exploitation.

faqs.question: Is consent a defence in trafficking cases?
faqs.answer: No. Consent obtained through exploitation or coercion is not a valid defence.

faqs.question: What forms of exploitation are covered?
faqs.answer: Sexual exploitation, slavery, servitude, forced labour, and organ removal.

title: Exploitation of a Trafficked Person
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever knowingly engages a trafficked minor for sexual exploitation shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment of not less than five years but which may extend to seven years and fine. Whoever knowingly engages a trafficked person for sexual exploitation shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment of not less than three years but which may extend to five years and fine.
simpleExplanation: Using or exploiting a person known to be a trafficking victim is a punishable offence.
punishment: Trafficked minor: Rigorous imprisonment 5 to 7 years and fine., Trafficked adult: Rigorous imprisonment 3 to 5 years and fine.
examples: Knowingly exploiting a trafficked child.
Using services of a trafficked person for sexual exploitation.

faqs.question: Must the offender know the person was trafficked?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is an essential element.

faqs.question: Are minors given greater protection?
faqs.answer: Yes. Punishments are more severe for offences involving minors.

title: Habitual Dealing in Slaves
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever habitually imports, exports, removes, buys, sells, traffics, or deals in slaves shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Regular involvement in slave trading or dealing in human beings as property is a grave offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Operating a network that trades people as property.
Repeatedly buying and selling persons for exploitation.

faqs.question: What does habitual dealing mean?
faqs.answer: Repeated or regular involvement in slave-related activities.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Selling Minor for Purposes of Prostitution, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever sells, lets to hire, or otherwise disposes of any person under eighteen years of age with intent that such person shall be employed or used for prostitution, illicit intercourse, or any unlawful and immoral purpose shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Selling or transferring a minor for prostitution or other immoral purposes is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Selling a child to a prostitution network.
Transferring custody of a minor for sexual exploitation.

faqs.question: Who is considered a minor?
faqs.answer: A person below 18 years of age.

faqs.question: Must prostitution actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. The unlawful intent is sufficient.

title: Buying Minor for Purposes of Prostitution, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever buys, hires, or otherwise obtains possession of any person under eighteen years of age with intent that such person shall be employed or used for prostitution, illicit intercourse, or any unlawful and immoral purpose shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Obtaining a minor for prostitution or immoral purposes is a serious criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Purchasing a child for sexual exploitation.
Obtaining custody of a minor for prostitution.

faqs.question: Is actual exploitation required?
faqs.answer: No. Intent is sufficient.

faqs.question: Does the law presume immoral intent in certain cases?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain circumstances may create a presumption of unlawful intent.

title: Unlawful Compulsory Labour
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever unlawfully compels any person to labour against the will of that person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Forcing a person to work against their will is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Forcing workers to work through threats.
Compelling labour without freedom to leave.

faqs.question: Does this include bonded labour?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bonded or forced labour may fall within this section.

faqs.question: Is consent important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The labour must be against the person's will.

title: Rape
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: A man is said to commit rape if he engages in specified sexual acts with a woman under circumstances including against her will, without her consent, by coercion, deception, abuse of authority, or where she is incapable of giving valid consent. The section also defines consent and statutory exceptions.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the offence of rape and explains the circumstances in which consent is absent or legally invalid.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 376 and related provisions.
examples: Sexual intercourse without consent.
Obtaining consent through threats or coercion.

faqs.question: What is consent?
faqs.answer: An unequivocal voluntary agreement communicated through words, gestures, or conduct.

faqs.question: Can silence be treated as consent?
faqs.answer: No. Lack of resistance alone does not imply consent.

title: Punishment for Rape
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever commits rape shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment of not less than ten years, which may extend to imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine, subject to statutory variations for aggravated circumstances.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes punishment for rape, with enhanced penalties in aggravated situations.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years, extendable to life imprisonment, and fine.
examples: Conviction for rape under Section 375.
Aggravated rape attracting enhanced punishment.

faqs.question: What is the minimum punishment?
faqs.answer: Generally 10 years of rigorous imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Unnatural Offences
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily has carnal intercourse against the order of nature with any man, woman, or animal shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment up to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. Consensual sexual relations between adults have been read down by the Supreme Court in Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018).
simpleExplanation: After the Supreme Court's 2018 decision, consensual sexual relations between adults are not criminal under this section. The provision primarily remains relevant for non-consensual acts and acts involving animals.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine (subject to constitutional interpretation).
examples: Non-consensual acts covered by the section.
Sexual acts involving animals.

faqs.question: Is consensual same-sex activity between adults a crime?
faqs.answer: No. The Supreme Court decriminalized consensual same-sex relations between adults in 2018.

faqs.question: Does the section still have legal relevance?
faqs.answer: Yes. It may still apply to non-consensual acts and bestiality.

title: Theft
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.
simpleExplanation: Theft occurs when someone dishonestly takes movable property belonging to another person without consent.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 379.
examples: Stealing a mobile phone from someone's bag.
Taking another person's vehicle without permission.

faqs.question: Must the property be movable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Theft applies to movable property.

faqs.question: Is intention important?
faqs.answer: Yes. There must be dishonest intention.

title: Punishment for Theft
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits theft shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for the offence of theft.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Stealing a wallet.
Taking goods from a shop without payment.

faqs.question: Can a fine alone be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Depending on circumstances, the court may impose a fine.

faqs.question: What offence is punished here?
faqs.answer: Theft as defined in Section 378.

title: Theft in Dwelling House, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits theft in any building, tent, or vessel used as a human dwelling, or used for the custody of property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Theft committed inside a house, residence, or place where property is stored is treated more seriously.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Stealing jewellery from a home.
Committing theft inside a warehouse.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher?
faqs.answer: Because the offence involves intrusion into protected premises.

faqs.question: Does it apply to rented houses?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any dwelling house is covered.

title: Theft by Clerk or Servant of Property in Possession of Master
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, being a clerk or servant, commits theft in respect of any property in the possession of his master or employer shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: An employee who steals property belonging to their employer commits a more serious form of theft.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: An employee stealing cash from company accounts.
A domestic servant stealing valuables from the employer's home.

faqs.question: Why is this offence treated more seriously?
faqs.answer: Because it involves breach of trust by an employee.

faqs.question: Does the offender need to be employed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically applies to clerks and servants.

title: Theft After Preparation Made for Causing Death, Hurt or Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits theft having made preparation for causing death, hurt, restraint, or fear of death, hurt, or restraint in order to commit the theft or escape after committing it, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: A theft becomes more serious when the offender prepares to use violence or threats to facilitate the crime or escape.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A thief carries weapons intending to use them if caught.
Planning to threaten victims during a theft.

faqs.question: Must violence actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation for violence is sufficient.

faqs.question: How is this different from robbery?
faqs.answer: This section focuses on preparation for violence, whereas robbery involves actual violence or threat during the offence.

title: Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person or to any other person, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to deliver to any person any property, valuable security, or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits extortion.
simpleExplanation: Extortion occurs when someone uses threats or fear to force another person to hand over money, property, or valuable documents.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 384.
examples: Threatening to harm a person unless money is paid.
Forcing someone to sign a valuable document through intimidation.

faqs.question: Is actual harm required?
faqs.answer: No. Fear of injury and dishonest inducement are sufficient.

faqs.question: What kinds of property are covered?
faqs.answer: Money, property, valuable securities, and similar items.

title: Punishment for Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits extortion shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for the offence of extortion.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Obtaining money through threats.
Forcing a person to transfer property by intimidation.

faqs.question: Can both imprisonment and fine be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

faqs.question: What offence is punished here?
faqs.answer: Extortion as defined under Section 383.

title: Putting Person in Fear of Injury in Order to Commit Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, in order to commit extortion, puts or attempts to put any person in fear of injury shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Attempting to extort by creating fear of injury is punishable even if property is not actually obtained.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening a person to demand money, even if no payment is made.
Sending intimidating messages to force payment.

faqs.question: Must extortion succeed?
faqs.answer: No. Attempting to create fear is sufficient.

faqs.question: What kind of fear is required?
faqs.answer: Fear of injury to a person or another individual.

title: Extortion by Putting a Person in Fear of Death or Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits extortion by putting any person in fear of death or grievous hurt shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Extortion involving threats of death or serious injury is a much more serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Demanding money while threatening to kill the victim.
Threatening grievous bodily injury unless property is handed over.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher?
faqs.answer: Because the threat involves death or grievous hurt.

faqs.question: Must the victim actually be injured?
faqs.answer: No. The threat itself is sufficient.

title: Putting Person in Fear of Death or Grievous Hurt in Order to Commit Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, in order to commit extortion, puts or attempts to put any person in fear of death or grievous hurt shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Attempting extortion by threatening death or grievous hurt is punishable even if property is not delivered.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Threatening to kill someone unless they pay money.
Sending messages threatening serious harm to obtain property.

faqs.question: Must property be delivered?
faqs.answer: No. The attempt itself is punishable.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 386?
faqs.answer: Section 386 applies when extortion is completed; Section 387 covers attempts.

title: Extortion by Threat of Accusation of an Offence Punishable with Death, Life Imprisonment, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits extortion by putting any person in fear of an accusation against that person or another of an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
simpleExplanation: Using threats of serious criminal accusations to obtain money or property is a serious form of extortion.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Demanding money while threatening to falsely accuse someone of murder.
Forcing a payment by threatening a serious criminal complaint.

faqs.question: Does the accusation have to be true?
faqs.answer: No. The offence concerns the use of the threat itself.

faqs.question: Why is punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because threats involving serious criminal accusations can cause significant harm.

title: Putting Person in Fear of Accusation of Offence in Order to Commit Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, in order to commit extortion, puts or attempts to put any person in fear of an accusation of an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
simpleExplanation: Threatening serious criminal accusations in an attempt to extort property is punishable even if extortion is unsuccessful.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Threatening to file a false murder complaint unless money is paid.
Attempting to obtain property through threats of serious prosecution.

faqs.question: Must the extortion succeed?
faqs.answer: No. The attempt itself is punishable.

faqs.question: What type of accusations are covered?
faqs.answer: Accusations relating to very serious offences carrying severe punishments.

title: Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: In all robbery there is either theft or extortion. Theft becomes robbery when, in order to commit theft, or while committing theft, or while carrying away property obtained by theft, the offender voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof. Extortion becomes robbery when the offender is in the presence of the victim and induces delivery of property by putting the victim in fear of instant death, instant hurt, or instant wrongful restraint.
simpleExplanation: Robbery is an aggravated form of theft or extortion involving immediate violence, threats, or fear.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 392.
examples: Snatching a chain while threatening the victim with a knife.
Forcing a person at gunpoint to hand over money.

faqs.question: How is robbery different from theft?
faqs.answer: Robbery involves violence, threats, or fear in addition to theft.

faqs.question: Can extortion become robbery?
faqs.answer: Yes. When accompanied by immediate threats in the victim's presence.

title: Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: When five or more persons conjointly commit or attempt to commit a robbery, or where the whole number of persons present and aiding such commission or attempt amounts to five or more, every person so committing, attempting, or aiding is said to commit dacoity.
simpleExplanation: Dacoity is robbery committed by a group of five or more persons acting together.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 395.
examples: A gang of six persons robbing a house.
Five armed individuals jointly carrying out a robbery.

faqs.question: Why is five persons important?
faqs.answer: The law defines dacoity as involving five or more persons.

faqs.question: Does aiding the group count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Persons aiding the robbery are also liable.

title: Punishment for Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. If the robbery is committed on a highway between sunset and sunrise, the imprisonment may extend to fourteen years.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for robbery and imposes higher punishment for highway robbery at night.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; up to 14 years for highway robbery at night.
examples: Robbing a pedestrian using threats.
Highway robbery committed during nighttime.

faqs.question: Is robbery more serious than theft?
faqs.answer: Yes. Robbery involves violence or intimidation.

faqs.question: Why is highway robbery punished more severely?
faqs.answer: Because it creates greater danger to public safety.

title: Attempt to Commit Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Attempting to commit robbery is punishable even if the robbery is unsuccessful.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Trying to rob a person at knifepoint but being stopped.
Attempting to forcibly take money but failing.

faqs.question: Must property actually be taken?
faqs.answer: No. The attempt itself is punishable.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt in Committing Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: If any person, in committing or attempting to commit robbery, voluntarily causes hurt, such person and every other person jointly concerned in the robbery shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment up to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Causing hurt while committing or attempting robbery significantly increases the severity of the offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Injuring a victim during a robbery.
Assaulting a shopkeeper while attempting robbery.

faqs.question: Who can be punished under this section?
faqs.answer: Both the person causing hurt and those jointly involved in the robbery.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Punishment for Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits dacoity shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes punishment for dacoity, which is robbery committed by five or more persons.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A gang of six persons carrying out an armed robbery.
Five or more persons jointly committing robbery.

faqs.question: What distinguishes dacoity from robbery?
faqs.answer: Dacoity requires the involvement of five or more persons.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Dacoity with Murder
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: If any one of five or more persons who are conjointly committing dacoity commits murder in the course of such dacoity, every one of those persons shall be punished with death, imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: When a murder occurs during a dacoity, every member of the dacoity group can be held responsible, even if only one person actually committed the murder.
punishment: Death penalty, or imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A gang commits dacoity and one member kills a house owner.
A victim is murdered during an armed gang robbery.

faqs.question: Must every accused personally commit the murder?
faqs.answer: No. Participation in the dacoity can make all members liable.

faqs.question: Can the death penalty be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Robbery or Dacoity with Attempt to Cause Death or Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: If, at the time of committing robbery or dacoity, the offender uses any deadly weapon, causes grievous hurt, or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt, the imprisonment shall not be less than seven years.
simpleExplanation: Using deadly weapons or attempting to cause death or grievous hurt during robbery or dacoity attracts enhanced punishment.
punishment: Minimum imprisonment of 7 years.
examples: Using a firearm during a robbery.
Attempting to stab a victim while committing dacoity.

faqs.question: What is a deadly weapon?
faqs.answer: A weapon capable of causing death or serious injury.

faqs.question: Is there a minimum sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The minimum punishment is seven years.

title: Attempt to Commit Robbery or Dacoity When Armed with Deadly Weapon
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: If, at the time of attempting to commit robbery or dacoity, the offender is armed with any deadly weapon, the imprisonment shall not be less than seven years.
simpleExplanation: Attempting robbery or dacoity while carrying a deadly weapon is punishable even if the offence is not completed.
punishment: Minimum imprisonment of 7 years.
examples: Attempting a bank robbery while carrying a firearm.
Trying to commit dacoity with a loaded weapon.

faqs.question: Must the weapon be used?
faqs.answer: No. Being armed with a deadly weapon is sufficient.

faqs.question: Must the robbery succeed?
faqs.answer: No. An attempt is enough.

title: Making Preparation to Commit Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever makes any preparation for committing dacoity shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Preparing to commit dacoity is itself a punishable offence, even before any attempt is made.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Gathering weapons and planning a gang robbery.
Organizing a group and equipment for a dacoity.

faqs.question: Must the dacoity actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation itself is punishable.

faqs.question: Why is preparation criminalized?
faqs.answer: Because dacoity is considered a highly dangerous offence.

title: Belonging to Gang of Persons Associated for Purpose of Habitually Committing Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever belongs to any gang of persons associated for the purpose of habitually committing dacoity shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Membership in a gang that habitually commits dacoity is a serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Being a member of an organized dacoity gang.
Participating in a criminal group known for repeated dacoities.

faqs.question: Must the person commit a specific dacoity?
faqs.answer: No. Membership in the gang itself is punishable.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Belonging to Gang of Thieves
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever belongs to any wandering or other gang of persons associated for the purpose of habitually committing theft or robbery, and not being a gang associated for committing dacoity, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Membership in a gang that habitually commits theft or robbery is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Being part of an organized pickpocketing gang.
Joining a group that repeatedly commits robberies.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 400?
faqs.answer: Section 400 deals with dacoity gangs; Section 401 covers theft and robbery gangs.

faqs.question: Must a theft actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Membership itself is punishable.

title: Assembling for Purpose of Committing Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, at any time after the passing of this Act, shall be one of five or more persons assembled for the purpose of committing dacoity, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Merely assembling in a group of five or more persons with the intention of committing dacoity is an offence.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A gang gathering with weapons to commit a planned dacoity.
Five persons meeting to execute a robbery plan.

faqs.question: Must the dacoity actually be attempted?
faqs.answer: No. Assembly with the required intention is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is this punishable?
faqs.answer: To prevent serious organized crimes before they occur.

title: Dishonest Misappropriation of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: A person commits this offence when they dishonestly use or keep movable property belonging to another person as their own.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Keeping a lost wallet and spending the money inside.
Using another person's property for personal benefit without permission.

faqs.question: How is this different from theft?
faqs.answer: In misappropriation, the property may come into possession lawfully but is later dishonestly retained or used.

faqs.question: Must the property be movable?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies to movable property.

title: Dishonest Misappropriation of Property Possessed by Deceased Person at Time of Death
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use property possessed by a deceased person at the time of that person's death, before it has come into the possession of a person legally entitled to it, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Dishonestly taking or using property belonging to a deceased person before it reaches the rightful heirs or legal representatives is an offence.
punishment: General: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both., If committed by clerk or servant: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Taking cash from a deceased person's home before the heirs receive it.
A servant secretly appropriating valuables belonging to a deceased employer.

faqs.question: Who is protected by this section?
faqs.answer: The legal heirs or persons entitled to inherit the property.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher for servants?
faqs.answer: Because it involves abuse of trust.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, being entrusted with property or with dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates, converts, uses, or disposes of that property in violation of any legal contract or direction of law, commits criminal breach of trust.
simpleExplanation: A person commits criminal breach of trust when property entrusted to them is dishonestly misused, converted, or dealt with contrary to legal obligations.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 406.
examples: An employee entrusted with company funds uses them personally.
An agent sells entrusted property and keeps the proceeds.

faqs.question: What is entrustment?
faqs.answer: Giving someone responsibility, custody, or control over property.

faqs.question: Is breach of contract alone enough?
faqs.answer: No. Dishonest intention is required.

title: Punishment for Criminal Breach of Trust
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits criminal breach of trust shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for criminal breach of trust.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Misusing entrusted money.
Wrongfully disposing of property held in trust.

faqs.question: What offence is punished here?
faqs.answer: Criminal breach of trust defined in Section 405.

faqs.question: Can both imprisonment and fine be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Carrier, Wharfinger or Warehouse Keeper
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, being entrusted as a carrier, wharfinger, or warehouse keeper, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of entrusted property shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Persons engaged in transporting or storing goods commit a more serious offence when they dishonestly misuse entrusted property.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A warehouse keeper selling stored goods without authority.
A transporter misappropriating cargo entrusted for delivery.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because the offender occupies a position of commercial trust.

faqs.question: What is a wharfinger?
faqs.answer: A person responsible for managing goods at a wharf or dock.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Clerk or Servant
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, being a clerk or servant, or employed as such, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of property entrusted to him shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Employees who dishonestly misuse property entrusted to them commit an aggravated form of criminal breach of trust.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: An accountant embezzling company funds.
A cashier misappropriating money entrusted by the employer.

faqs.question: Why is employee misconduct punished more severely?
faqs.answer: Because it involves betrayal of an employment-based trust relationship.

faqs.question: Must property be entrusted?
faqs.answer: Yes. Entrustment is a key element.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Public Servant, Banker, Merchant or Agent
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, banker, merchant, factor, broker, attorney, or agent, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of property entrusted to him shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Public servants, bankers, merchants, and agents who abuse entrusted property commit one of the most serious forms of criminal breach of trust.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A bank official misappropriating customer funds.
A public servant diverting entrusted public money for personal gain.

faqs.question: Why is punishment so severe?
faqs.answer: Because the offender occupies a position of high trust and responsibility.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Property, the possession whereof has been transferred by theft, extortion, robbery, criminal misappropriation, or criminal breach of trust, is designated as stolen property, whether the transfer has been made or the misappropriation or breach of trust has been committed within or outside India.
simpleExplanation: This section defines what is considered stolen property under criminal law.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: A mobile phone stolen during theft.
Money obtained through criminal breach of trust.

faqs.question: Can property become stolen through breach of trust?
faqs.answer: Yes. Criminal breach of trust can make property 'stolen property'.

faqs.question: Does the offence have to occur in India?
faqs.answer: No. Property may still be treated as stolen even if the offence occurred outside India.

title: Dishonestly Receiving Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Receiving or keeping stolen property while knowing or suspecting that it is stolen is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Buying a stolen laptop at a suspiciously low price.
Keeping jewellery known to be stolen.

faqs.question: Must the receiver know the property is stolen?
faqs.answer: Knowledge or reason to believe it is stolen is required.

faqs.question: Can retaining stolen property also be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Dishonestly Receiving Property Stolen in the Commission of a Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains property stolen in the commission of a dacoity, knowing or having reason to believe it to be so stolen, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Receiving property known to have been obtained through dacoity is a very serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Purchasing gold known to have been looted during a dacoity.
Hiding money obtained through a gang robbery.

faqs.question: Why is punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because dacoity is one of the most serious property offences.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Habitually Dealing in Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever habitually receives or deals in property which he knows or has reason to believe to be stolen property shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment up to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Regularly trading in stolen goods is treated as a grave offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Running a business that routinely purchases stolen electronics.
Acting as a fence for stolen goods.

faqs.question: What does 'habitually' mean?
faqs.answer: Repeated or regular involvement in dealing with stolen property.

faqs.question: Can a single transaction attract this section?
faqs.answer: Generally no. Habitual conduct is required.

title: Assisting in Concealment of Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever voluntarily assists in concealing, disposing of, or making away with property which he knows or has reason to believe to be stolen property shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Helping to hide, transport, or dispose of stolen property is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Helping a thief hide stolen jewellery.
Transporting stolen goods knowing they are stolen.

faqs.question: Must the person know the property is stolen?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Does merely helping count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Assistance itself is punishable.

title: Cheating
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces that person to deliver property, or intentionally induces that person to do or omit to do anything which causes or is likely to cause damage or harm, commits cheating.
simpleExplanation: Cheating occurs when a person deceives another and causes loss, damage, or transfer of property through that deception.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 417.
examples: Obtaining money through false promises.
Misleading someone into signing a harmful agreement.

faqs.question: Is deception essential?
faqs.answer: Yes. Deception is the core element of cheating.

faqs.question: Must property always be involved?
faqs.answer: No. Harm caused by induced action or omission may also suffice.

title: Cheating by Personation
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: A person is said to cheat by personation if he cheats by pretending to be some other person, or by knowingly substituting one person for another, or representing that he or another person is someone else.
simpleExplanation: Pretending to be another person in order to deceive someone amounts to cheating by personation.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 419.
examples: Impersonating a government officer to collect money.
Creating a fake identity to deceive victims.

faqs.question: Is impersonation necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence specifically involves pretending to be another person.

faqs.question: Can online impersonation be covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Digital impersonation may also fall within this section.

title: Punishment for Cheating
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever cheats shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for the offence of cheating.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Making false representations to obtain money.
Deceiving a person into a disadvantageous transaction.

faqs.question: Which section defines cheating?
faqs.answer: Section 415.

faqs.question: Can a fine alone be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Cheating with Knowledge that Wrongful Loss May Ensue to Person Whose Interest Offender Is Bound to Protect
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever cheats with the knowledge that wrongful loss may thereby ensue to a person whose interest he is legally bound, or by legal contract, to protect, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section applies when a person cheats someone whose interests they have a legal duty to protect.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: An agent deceiving a client for personal gain.
A fiduciary misleading a beneficiary.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because the offender violates a duty of trust or protection.

faqs.question: Must there be a legal obligation?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever cheats by personation shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes cheating committed through impersonation.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Pretending to be a government official to collect fees.
Using a false identity to obtain benefits.

faqs.question: What is personation?
faqs.answer: Pretending to be another person to deceive someone.

faqs.question: Which section defines it?
faqs.answer: Section 416.

title: Cheating and Dishonestly Inducing Delivery of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property, or to make, alter, or destroy a valuable security or anything capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Section 420 applies when cheating results in the dishonest delivery of property, money, or valuable documents. It is one of the most commonly invoked fraud provisions in India.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Obtaining money through a fake investment scheme.
Fraudulently inducing a person to transfer property.

faqs.question: Why is Section 420 so common?
faqs.answer: Because many frauds involve dishonest inducement leading to transfer of money or property.

faqs.question: Is mere breach of contract enough?
faqs.answer: No. There must be dishonest intention from the beginning.

title: Dishonest or Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property to Prevent Distribution Among Creditors
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently removes, conceals, transfers, or delivers any property, or causes or permits the same to be done, with intent to prevent the distribution of that property among creditors in accordance with law, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Hiding or transferring assets to avoid paying creditors is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Transferring assets to relatives to avoid repayment of debts.
Concealing property before insolvency proceedings.

faqs.question: Who are creditors?
faqs.answer: Persons or entities to whom money is legally owed.

faqs.question: Is intent important?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulent or dishonest intent is essential.

title: Dishonestly or Fraudulently Preventing Debt from Being Available for Creditors
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently prevents any debt or demand due to him from being available according to law for the payment of his debts, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: A debtor cannot intentionally prevent money owed to them from being used to satisfy their creditors.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Waiving a recoverable debt to avoid creditor claims.
Concealing receivables during insolvency proceedings.

faqs.question: Does this involve money owed to the offender?
faqs.answer: Yes. It concerns debts owed to the accused.

faqs.question: Why is this an offence?
faqs.answer: Because it unfairly defeats creditor rights.

title: Dishonest or Fraudulent Execution of Deed of Transfer Containing False Statement of Consideration
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently signs, executes, or becomes a party to any deed or instrument relating to property which contains a false statement regarding consideration shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Creating or signing property documents that falsely state the value or consideration involved is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Showing a false sale value in a property deed.
Creating documents that misrepresent consideration paid.

faqs.question: What is consideration?
faqs.answer: The value or payment exchanged in a transaction.

faqs.question: Must the statement be false?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence concerns knowingly false statements.

title: Dishonest or Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently conceals, removes, transfers, delivers property, or assists in doing so, with intent to defeat lawful claims or rights, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Fraudulently hiding, moving, or disposing of property to defeat lawful claims is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Concealing assets from a court-appointed receiver.
Moving property to prevent lawful recovery.

faqs.question: Is actual loss required?
faqs.answer: No. Fraudulent intent is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can assisting another person be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, destroys property or causes any change in property that diminishes its value, utility, or affects it injuriously, commits mischief.
simpleExplanation: Mischief occurs when a person intentionally damages or alters property to cause loss or harm.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under subsequent sections.
examples: Damaging another person's vehicle.
Destroying crops belonging to another person.

faqs.question: Is intent necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be intentional or done with knowledge of likely harm.

faqs.question: Must property be completely destroyed?
faqs.answer: No. Any harmful reduction in value or usefulness is sufficient.

title: Punishment for Mischief
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the basic punishment for mischief.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine, or both.
examples: Damaging another person's property.
Defacing public property.

faqs.question: Which section defines mischief?
faqs.answer: Section 425.

faqs.question: Can both imprisonment and fine be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief Causing Damage of Fifty Rupees or More
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief and thereby causes loss or damage amounting to fifty rupees or more shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Causing property damage above the prescribed value attracts enhanced punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Breaking vehicle windows.
Damaging equipment causing financial loss.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because the damage exceeds the specified value threshold.

faqs.question: Does the value matter?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically requires a minimum amount of damage.

title: Mischief by Killing or Maiming Animal of Value Ten Rupees or More
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming, or rendering useless any animal valued at ten rupees or more shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally harming or killing another person's animal is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Poisoning a neighbour's livestock.
Maiming another person's animal.

faqs.question: Does the animal need to belong to someone else?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence concerns wrongful loss caused to another person.

faqs.question: Is poisoning covered?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief by Killing or Maiming Valuable Animal
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming, or rendering useless any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow, ox, or any animal of significant value shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Harming valuable animals attracts more severe punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Poisoning a farmer's cow.
Maiming a valuable horse.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher?
faqs.answer: Because the animals involved are economically valuable.

faqs.question: Is poisoning specifically covered?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief by Injury to Works of Irrigation or by Wrongfully Diverting Water
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by causing diminution of the supply of water for agricultural purposes, food, drink, human use, or by interfering with irrigation works, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Damaging irrigation systems or unlawfully diverting water supplies is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Breaking an irrigation canal.
Diverting water supply to deprive farmers.

faqs.question: Why is this offence serious?
faqs.answer: Because it affects agriculture and public resources.

faqs.question: Does it apply to irrigation works?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief by Injury to Public Road, Bridge, River or Channel
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by doing any act which renders or which he knows to be likely to render any public road, public bridge, navigable river, or navigable channel impassable or less safe for travel or conveyance shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Damaging roads, bridges, rivers, or channels used by the public is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Damaging a bridge used by the public.
Obstructing a navigable waterway.

faqs.question: Must the road become completely unusable?
faqs.answer: No. Making it less safe is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is this offence serious?
faqs.answer: Because it affects public safety and transportation.

title: Mischief by Causing Inundation or Obstruction to Public Drainage
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by causing inundation or obstruction to any public drainage attended with damage shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Causing flooding or blocking public drainage systems is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Blocking municipal drains causing flooding.
Damaging drainage channels resulting in waterlogging.

faqs.question: Must damage occur?
faqs.answer: Yes. The obstruction or inundation must be attended with damage.

faqs.question: Does this apply to public drains?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief by Destroying or Moving Lighthouse or Sea-Mark
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by destroying, moving, or rendering less useful any lighthouse or sea-mark, or by exhibiting any false light, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Interfering with navigation aids or displaying misleading signals that may endanger vessels is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Damaging a lighthouse.
Displaying false navigation signals to mislead ships.

faqs.question: Why is punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because navigation safety is critical to life and commerce.

faqs.question: Are false lights covered?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief by Destroying Landmark Fixed by Public Authority
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by destroying or moving any landmark fixed by public authority shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Removing or damaging officially established boundary markers or landmarks is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Removing government boundary stones.
Destroying officially marked land survey points.

faqs.question: What is a landmark under this section?
faqs.answer: A marker officially fixed by public authority.

faqs.question: Why is it protected?
faqs.answer: Because it helps define official boundaries and public records.

title: Mischief by Fire or Explosive Substance with Intent to Cause Damage
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by fire or any explosive substance intending to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, damage to property shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Setting fire to property or using explosives to cause damage is a serious criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Burning a parked vehicle.
Using explosives to damage a warehouse.

faqs.question: Must the damage exceed a minimum value?
faqs.answer: The section applies where fire or explosives are used to cause property damage.

faqs.question: Is intent important?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief by Fire or Explosive Substance with Intent to Destroy House, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by fire or explosive substance intending to destroy any house, dwelling, place of worship, or place used for custody of property shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Burning or blowing up homes, religious places, or buildings used to store property is a grave offence.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Setting fire to a residence.
Using explosives to destroy a temple or warehouse.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

faqs.question: Does it apply to places of worship?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Mischief with Intent to Destroy or Make Unsafe Decked Vessel
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief with intent to destroy or make unsafe any decked vessel or vessel of twenty tons burden or upwards shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Damaging large vessels or ships in a way that endangers their safety is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Damaging a ship's structure.
Sabotaging a vessel's navigation system.

faqs.question: Why is punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because such acts may endanger lives and commerce.

faqs.question: Does it apply only to large vessels?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically refers to decked vessels and larger ships.

title: Punishment for Attempt to Commit Mischief by Fire or Explosive Substance
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit an offence described in Sections 435, 436, or 437 shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Attempting serious arson or explosive-related offences is itself punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Attempting to set fire to a building but being stopped.
Preparing an explosive device to damage property.

faqs.question: Must the property actually be damaged?
faqs.answer: No. An attempt is sufficient.

faqs.question: Which offences are covered?
faqs.answer: Sections 435, 436, and 437.

title: Punishment for Intentionally Running Vessel Aground or Ashore
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever intentionally runs any vessel aground, ashore, or upon rocks with intent to commit theft or mischief shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Deliberately grounding or wrecking a vessel to facilitate theft or damage is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Intentionally causing a shipwreck to steal cargo.
Running a vessel onto rocks to damage it.

faqs.question: Must theft occur?
faqs.answer: No. Intent to facilitate theft or mischief is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is this offence serious?
faqs.answer: Because it may endanger lives and property.

title: Mischief Committed After Preparation Made for Causing Death, Hurt or Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
