officialText: Whoever commits mischief having made preparation for causing death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof, in order to commit such mischief or facilitate escape, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Property damage becomes a more serious offence when the offender prepares to use violence or threats.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
examples: Damaging property while carrying weapons to intimidate victims.
Preparing to assault anyone who tries to stop the mischief.

faqs.question: Must violence actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation for violence is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because the conduct creates a greater risk to human safety.

title: Criminal Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever enters into or upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence, or to intimidate, insult, or annoy any person in possession of such property, or having lawfully entered remains there unlawfully with such intent, commits criminal trespass.
simpleExplanation: Entering or remaining on another person's property with criminal intent, or to intimidate, insult, or annoy, amounts to criminal trespass.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 447.
examples: Entering a neighbour's land to threaten them.
Refusing to leave private property after entering lawfully and then causing harassment.

faqs.question: Is mere entry enough?
faqs.answer: No. Criminal intent or intent to intimidate, insult, or annoy is required.

faqs.question: Can a lawful entry become trespass?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the person remains unlawfully with the required intent.

title: House Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent, or vessel used as a human dwelling, place of worship, or place for custody of property commits house trespass.
simpleExplanation: Criminal trespass becomes house trespass when it involves a residence, place of worship, or place where property is stored.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 448.
examples: Entering someone's home to intimidate them.
Trespassing into a warehouse with criminal intent.

faqs.question: What places are covered?
faqs.answer: Homes, places of worship, and places used for custody of property.

faqs.question: Is criminal trespass required first?
faqs.answer: Yes. House trespass is a specific form of criminal trespass.

title: Lurking House Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits house trespass having taken precautions to conceal such trespass from a person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser commits lurking house trespass.
simpleExplanation: Secretly entering or remaining in a building while hiding the trespass from those entitled to prevent it is called lurking house trespass.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under later sections.
examples: Sneaking into a house through a concealed route.
Hiding inside a building to avoid detection.

faqs.question: What makes it 'lurking'?
faqs.answer: The concealment of the trespass from persons entitled to prevent it.

faqs.question: Must house trespass exist first?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Lurking House Trespass by Night
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits lurking house trespass after sunset and before sunrise commits lurking house trespass by night.
simpleExplanation: Lurking house trespass committed during nighttime is treated more seriously.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under later sections.
examples: Secretly entering a house at midnight.
Hiding inside a residence during the night.

faqs.question: What is considered night?
faqs.answer: The period between sunset and sunrise.

faqs.question: Why is punishment more severe?
faqs.answer: Because nighttime offences pose greater danger.

title: House Breaking
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: A person commits house-breaking when he effects entry into or exit from a house trespass through any of the methods specified in this section, including creating passages, opening locks, using criminal force, or entering through unintended passages.
simpleExplanation: House-breaking occurs when entry or exit is achieved through force, lock-breaking, creating openings, or other specified unlawful methods.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under subsequent sections.
examples: Breaking a lock to enter a house.
Entering through a hole made in a wall.

faqs.question: Is every house trespass house-breaking?
faqs.answer: No. House-breaking requires one of the specified methods of entry or exit.

faqs.question: Can breaking a lock amount to house-breaking?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: House Breaking by Night
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise commits house-breaking by night.
simpleExplanation: House-breaking committed during nighttime is treated as a more serious offence.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under later sections.
examples: Breaking into a residence after midnight.
Forcing entry into a warehouse during the night.

faqs.question: What time period is covered?
faqs.answer: Between sunset and sunrise.

faqs.question: Must house-breaking already be established?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Punishment for Criminal Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits criminal trespass shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for the offence of criminal trespass.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both.
examples: Entering private land to intimidate the owner.
Remaining unlawfully on property to annoy the occupant.

faqs.question: Which section defines criminal trespass?
faqs.answer: Section 441.

faqs.question: Can a fine be imposed without imprisonment?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Punishment for House Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits house trespass shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes house trespass involving homes, places of worship, or places used for storing property.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Entering a house with intent to intimidate the occupants.
Trespassing into a warehouse to cause annoyance.

faqs.question: Which section defines house trespass?
faqs.answer: Section 442.

faqs.question: Is house trespass more serious than criminal trespass?
faqs.answer: Yes, because it involves protected premises.

title: House Trespass in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Death
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits house trespass in order to commit any offence punishable with death shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: Entering a house with the intention of committing an offence punishable with death is a very serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Entering a residence intending to commit murder.
Trespassing into a home to carry out a capital offence.

faqs.question: Must the intended offence actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. The intention at the time of trespass is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: House Trespass in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment for Life
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits house trespass in order to commit any offence punishable with imprisonment for life shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: House trespass committed with the intention of committing an offence punishable with life imprisonment attracts severe punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Entering a building intending to commit a life-imprisonment offence.
Trespassing into a residence to commit a grave felony.

faqs.question: Must the intended crime be completed?
faqs.answer: No.

faqs.question: What matters most?
faqs.answer: The intent existing at the time of house trespass.

title: House Trespass in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits house trespass in order to commit any offence punishable with imprisonment shall be punished. Where the intended offence is theft, the punishment may extend to seven years and fine.
simpleExplanation: Entering a house intending to commit any imprisonable offence is punishable, with enhanced punishment where theft is intended.
punishment: General: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine., If intended offence is theft: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Entering a house intending to commit assault.
Breaking into a residence intending to steal valuables.

faqs.question: Does the intended offence need to be committed?
faqs.answer: No. The intention is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is theft treated more seriously?
faqs.answer: Because burglary-related offences present higher risk to property and occupants.

title: House Trespass After Preparation for Hurt, Assault or Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits house trespass having made preparation for causing hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof to any person shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: House trespass becomes more serious when the offender prepares to use violence, assault, or restraint.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Entering a house carrying weapons to threaten occupants.
Trespassing into a residence after planning to assault someone.

faqs.question: Must violence actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation for violence is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because of the increased danger to occupants.

title: Punishment for Lurking House Trespass or House Breaking
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits lurking house trespass or house-breaking shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the basic punishment for lurking house trespass and house-breaking.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
examples: Secretly entering a house to avoid detection.
Breaking a lock to unlawfully enter a building.

faqs.question: What offences are covered?
faqs.answer: Lurking house trespass and house-breaking.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Lurking House Trespass or House Breaking in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits lurking house trespass or house-breaking in order to commit any offence punishable with imprisonment shall be punished. If the intended offence is theft, punishment may extend to ten years.
simpleExplanation: House-breaking or lurking trespass committed with intent to commit another crime attracts enhanced punishment.
punishment: General: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine., If intended offence is theft: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Breaking into a home intending to commit assault.
Entering a house to steal valuables.

faqs.question: Must the intended offence occur?
faqs.answer: No. The intention is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is theft treated more seriously?
faqs.answer: Because burglary for theft presents greater danger to persons and property.

title: Lurking House Trespass or House Breaking After Preparation for Hurt, Assault or Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits lurking house trespass or house-breaking after making preparation for causing hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: The offence becomes more serious when preparations have been made to use violence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Breaking into a house while carrying weapons.
Secretly entering a building with plans to assault occupants.

faqs.question: Must violence actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation for violence is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because of the increased risk to human safety.

title: Punishment for Lurking House Trespass or House Breaking by Night
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits lurking house trespass by night or house-breaking by night shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: House-breaking or lurking trespass committed during the night attracts enhanced punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Entering a house secretly after midnight.
Breaking into a building during the night.

faqs.question: What period is considered night?
faqs.answer: Between sunset and sunrise.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher?
faqs.answer: Because night offences create greater danger and fear.

title: Lurking House Trespass or House Breaking by Night in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits lurking house trespass by night or house-breaking by night in order to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment shall be punished. If the intended offence is theft, punishment may extend to fourteen years.
simpleExplanation: Night-time burglary committed with intent to commit another offence is treated very seriously.
punishment: General: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine., If intended offence is theft: Imprisonment up to 14 years and fine.
examples: Breaking into a home at night intending to steal.
Secretly entering a residence to commit assault.

faqs.question: Can punishment reach 14 years?
faqs.answer: Yes, where theft is the intended offence.

faqs.question: Must the intended crime be completed?
faqs.answer: No.

title: Lurking House Trespass or House Breaking by Night After Preparation for Hurt, Assault or Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits lurking house trespass by night or house-breaking by night after preparation for causing hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Night-time house-breaking combined with preparation for violence is one of the most serious trespass offences.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 14 years and fine.
examples: Armed burglars entering a house at night.
Breaking into a residence with weapons to intimidate occupants.

faqs.question: Must violence actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because of the heightened threat to life and safety.

title: Grievous Hurt Caused While Committing Lurking House Trespass or House Breaking
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: If any person, while committing lurking house trespass or house-breaking, causes grievous hurt to any person or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt, that person shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Causing grievous injury during house-breaking attracts very severe punishment.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A burglar seriously injures a resident.
Attempting to kill an occupant during a break-in.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

faqs.question: Does attempted grievous hurt also count?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: All Persons Jointly Concerned in Lurking House Trespass or House Breaking by Night Punishable Where Death or Grievous Hurt Caused
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: If, during lurking house trespass by night or house-breaking by night, death or grievous hurt is caused by one participant, every person jointly concerned in the offence is liable.
simpleExplanation: All participants in a night-time house-breaking may be punished if death or grievous hurt occurs during the offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: One burglar injures a resident while others assist.
A gang break-in results in grievous injury.

faqs.question: Must every accused personally cause injury?
faqs.answer: No. Joint participation may create liability.

faqs.question: Why does the law impose joint liability?
faqs.answer: Because all participants contribute to the dangerous offence.

title: Dishonestly Breaking Open Receptacle Containing Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief breaks open or unfastens any closed receptacle containing or believed to contain property shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Breaking open a box, locker, safe, or similar container containing property is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Breaking open a safe to access valuables.
Forcing open a locked storage chest.

faqs.question: Must property actually be inside?
faqs.answer: No. Belief that property is inside may be sufficient.

faqs.question: What is a receptacle?
faqs.answer: A container such as a box, safe, locker, or chest.

title: Punishment for Same Offence When Entrusted with Custody
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, being entrusted with custody of a receptacle containing property, dishonestly breaks it open or allows it to be broken open shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: A person responsible for safeguarding a container commits a more serious offence if they dishonestly break it open or permit it to be opened.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A custodian opening a sealed locker for personal gain.
A caretaker allowing unauthorized access to a secured safe.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because the offender was entrusted with custody.

faqs.question: Is entrustment important?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is the key element of this offence.

title: Forgery
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever makes any false document or false electronic record or part thereof with intent to cause damage or injury, support a claim or title, cause any person to part with property, enter into a contract, or commit fraud, commits forgery.
simpleExplanation: Forgery means creating a false document or electronic record to deceive others, commit fraud, or gain an unlawful advantage.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 465.
examples: Creating a fake property deed.
Altering an electronic contract to gain money.

faqs.question: Can electronic records be forged?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section covers both physical and electronic documents.

faqs.question: Is fraudulent intent required?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Making a False Document
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: A person is said to make a false document if dishonestly or fraudulently he makes, signs, seals, executes, alters, or causes a document or electronic record to appear as though made by another person, or by authority not actually given.
simpleExplanation: This section explains when a document becomes a false document for the purposes of forgery.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: Signing another person's name without authority.
Altering a document after it has been signed.

faqs.question: Must the document be completely fake?
faqs.answer: No. Altering a genuine document can also create a false document.

faqs.question: Does unauthorized signing qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Punishment for Forgery
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever commits forgery shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the general punishment for forgery.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Forging a certificate.
Creating a fake identification document.

faqs.question: Which section defines forgery?
faqs.answer: Section 463.

faqs.question: Can both fine and imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Forgery of Record of Court or Public Register
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever forges a record of a court, a public register, or a certificate issued by a public servant shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Forging court records or government records is treated as a more serious form of forgery.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Forging a court order.
Creating a fake birth certificate.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because public records are essential for governance and justice.

faqs.question: Are government certificates covered?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Forgery of Valuable Security, Will, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever forges a valuable security, a will, an authority to adopt a son, or any document giving authority to transfer valuable property or receive money shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Forging wills, securities, or documents involving significant legal or financial rights is a grave offence.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Forging a will to obtain inheritance.
Creating a fake bond certificate.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

faqs.question: Why is this offence severe?
faqs.answer: Because it affects important legal and financial rights.

title: Forgery for Purpose of Cheating
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever commits forgery intending that the forged document or electronic record shall be used for the purpose of cheating shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Creating forged documents specifically to cheat another person is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Forging educational certificates to obtain employment.
Creating fake invoices to deceive customers.

faqs.question: Must cheating actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Intent that the forgery be used for cheating is sufficient.

faqs.question: Which cheating section is related?
faqs.answer: Section 420.

title: Forgery for Purpose of Harming Reputation
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever commits forgery intending that the forged document or electronic record shall harm the reputation of any person shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Creating forged documents to damage another person's reputation is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Creating fake letters to defame someone.
Publishing forged emails to damage a person's reputation.

faqs.question: Must reputation actually be harmed?
faqs.answer: No. The intention is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can electronic records be involved?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Forged Document or Electronic Record
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: A false document or false electronic record made wholly or partly by forgery is designated a forged document or forged electronic record.
simpleExplanation: This section defines what constitutes a forged document or electronic record.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: A forged property agreement.
A fabricated digital certificate.

faqs.question: Is this a punishment section?
faqs.answer: No. It provides a definition.

faqs.question: Does it apply to electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Using as Genuine a Forged Document or Electronic Record
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses as genuine any document or electronic record which he knows or has reason to believe to be a forged document or forged electronic record shall be punished in the same manner as if he had forged such document or electronic record.
simpleExplanation: Using a forged document as if it were genuine is treated as seriously as creating the forgery itself.
punishment: Same punishment as the forgery relating to that document.
examples: Submitting a forged degree certificate for employment.
Using a fake property deed in a legal proceeding.

faqs.question: Must the person create the forgery?
faqs.answer: No. Using a forged document knowingly is sufficient.

faqs.question: Is knowledge important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know or have reason to believe it is forged.

title: Making or Possessing Counterfeit Seal, Plate, etc. for Forgery of Valuable Security
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever makes, counterfeits, or possesses any seal, plate, or instrument intended for forging valuable securities, wills, or other important legal documents shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Creating or possessing tools intended to forge valuable legal or financial documents is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 7 years, and fine.
examples: Manufacturing counterfeit government seals.
Keeping equipment intended to create forged securities.

faqs.question: Must actual forgery occur?
faqs.answer: No. Possession with unlawful intent is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is punishment severe?
faqs.answer: Because valuable securities and wills affect significant legal rights.

title: Making or Possessing Counterfeit Seal, Plate, etc. for Forgery Other Than Valuable Security
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever makes or possesses any counterfeit seal, plate, or instrument for the purpose of committing forgery other than forgery described in Section 472 shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Possessing or creating tools for ordinary forgery is also a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Creating fake institutional seals.
Keeping equipment to forge certificates.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 472?
faqs.answer: Section 472 relates to valuable securities and similar important documents.

faqs.question: Is possession alone enough?
faqs.answer: Yes, if accompanied by the required intent.

title: Possession of Forged Document Described in Section 466 or 467
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever has possession of a forged document described in Sections 466 or 467, knowing it to be forged and intending to use it as genuine, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Keeping forged court records, public records, wills, or valuable securities with intent to use them is an offence.
punishment: Documents under Section 467: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 7 years, and fine., Documents under Section 466: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Possessing a forged will for future use.
Keeping forged government certificates intending to use them.

faqs.question: Must the document actually be used?
faqs.answer: No. Possession with intent is sufficient.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of forgery required?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Counterfeiting Device or Mark Used for Authenticating Documents
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever counterfeits any device or mark used by a public servant or other authorized person for authenticating documents shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Creating fake authentication devices or marks used to validate documents is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Creating fake notary authentication marks.
Counterfeiting official verification devices.

faqs.question: What is an authentication mark?
faqs.answer: A mark used to verify the genuineness of a document.

faqs.question: Why is this offence serious?
faqs.answer: Because it undermines trust in official documents.

title: Counterfeiting Mark Used for Authenticating Documents
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever counterfeits any mark used for the purpose of authenticating documents described in law shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Counterfeiting official authentication marks used on documents is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Using a fake government authentication stamp.
Creating forged verification marks.

faqs.question: How is this different from Section 475?
faqs.answer: It specifically addresses authentication marks themselves.

faqs.question: Is actual use required?
faqs.answer: No. Counterfeiting itself is punishable.

title: Fraudulent Cancellation, Destruction, etc. of Will, Authority or Valuable Security
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly cancels, destroys, defaces, conceals, or alters any valuable security, will, or authority to adopt, or any document giving authority to transfer property, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Destroying, altering, or hiding important legal documents for fraudulent purposes is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Destroying a will to prevent inheritance.
Altering a property transfer document.

faqs.question: Must the document be forged?
faqs.answer: No. Even genuine documents are protected.

faqs.question: Why is this offence serious?
faqs.answer: Because it affects legal and property rights.

title: Falsification of Accounts
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever, being a clerk, officer, servant, or person employed in any capacity, wilfully and with intent to defraud destroys, alters, mutilates, falsifies, or makes false entries in any book, account, electronic record, or document belonging to an employer shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Manipulating accounts, financial records, books, or electronic records to commit fraud is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Creating fake accounting entries to hide losses.
Altering company financial records to commit embezzlement.

faqs.question: Does this apply to electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes.

faqs.question: Is intent to defraud required?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Counterfeiting Currency Notes or Bank Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting, any currency note or bank note shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Creating fake currency notes or participating in their production is a grave offence against the financial system.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Printing fake ₹500 notes.
Participating in the manufacture of counterfeit currency.

faqs.question: Must a complete fake note be produced?
faqs.answer: No. Participation in any part of the counterfeiting process is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Using as Genuine, Forged or Counterfeit Currency Notes or Bank Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever sells, buys, receives, traffics in, uses as genuine, or otherwise deals with forged or counterfeit currency notes or bank notes, knowing or having reason to believe them to be counterfeit, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly using or circulating fake currency is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Using a fake ₹2000 note in a shop.
Selling counterfeit notes to another person.

faqs.question: Must the person know the note is fake?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Can merely circulating fake notes be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Possession of Forged or Counterfeit Currency Notes or Bank Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever has in his possession any forged or counterfeit currency note or bank note, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be forged or counterfeit and intending to use it as genuine, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Keeping fake currency with the intention of using it as real money is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Storing counterfeit notes for future circulation.
Possessing fake notes intending to spend them.

faqs.question: Is possession alone enough?
faqs.answer: Possession must be accompanied by knowledge and intent to use as genuine.

faqs.question: What if the person did not know the note was fake?
faqs.answer: Knowledge or reason to believe is a required element.

title: Making or Possessing Instruments or Materials for Forging or Counterfeiting Currency Notes or Bank Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever makes, performs any part of the process of making, buys, sells, disposes of, or possesses any machinery, instrument, material, or apparatus intended for counterfeiting currency notes or bank notes shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Possessing or manufacturing tools and materials used to create fake currency is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Operating a counterfeit currency printing setup.
Possessing specialized plates used for fake note production.

faqs.question: Must fake currency actually be produced?
faqs.answer: No. Possession of the equipment with unlawful intent is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can life imprisonment be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Making or Using Documents Resembling Currency Notes or Bank Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever makes, uses, or causes to be used any document or instrument resembling a currency note or bank note in a manner likely to deceive shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Creating documents that closely resemble real currency and may mislead people is an offence.
punishment: Fine up to ₹100, or as otherwise prescribed by law.
examples: Printing promotional vouchers that closely resemble currency notes.
Creating imitation notes capable of deceiving the public.

faqs.question: Must the document be actual counterfeit currency?
faqs.answer: No. Even deceptive imitations may attract liability.

faqs.question: Why is this section important?
faqs.answer: It helps prevent public confusion and misuse of currency-like documents.

title: Defamation
chapter: Chapter XXI - Of Defamation
officialText: Whoever, by words either spoken or intended to be read, by signs, or by visible representations, makes or publishes any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm the reputation of such person, is said to defame that person, subject to the exceptions provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: Defamation occurs when a person makes or publishes a statement that harms another person's reputation.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 500.
examples: Publishing a false allegation that a person committed fraud.
Posting unverified accusations on social media that damage someone's reputation.

faqs.question: Must the statement be communicated to others?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reputation can only be harmed if the statement is communicated to someone other than the person concerned.

faqs.question: Can truth be a defense?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it is made for the public good and falls within the statutory exceptions.

title: Punishment for Defamation
chapter: Chapter XXI - Of Defamation
officialText: Whoever defames another shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the punishment for the offence of defamation.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Publishing defamatory articles.
Circulating false statements that damage a person's reputation.

faqs.question: Which section defines defamation?
faqs.answer: Section 499 IPC.

faqs.question: Can only a fine be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court may impose fine, imprisonment, or both.

title: Printing or Engraving Matter Known to be Defamatory
chapter: Chapter XXI - Of Defamation
officialText: Whoever prints or engraves any matter knowing or having good reason to believe that such matter is defamatory of any person shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly prints or produces defamatory content can be punished even if they are not the original author.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Printing pamphlets containing false defamatory allegations.
Publishing defamatory posters knowing the content is false.

faqs.question: Must the printer know the content is defamatory?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge or reason to believe is required.

faqs.question: Can a publisher be prosecuted?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the required knowledge exists.

title: Sale of Printed or Engraved Substance Containing Defamatory Matter
chapter: Chapter XXI - Of Defamation
officialText: Whoever sells or offers for sale any printed or engraved substance containing defamatory matter, knowing that it contains such matter, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Selling or distributing defamatory material while knowing its contents is an offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Selling books containing knowingly defamatory content.
Distributing defamatory pamphlets for profit.

faqs.question: Is knowledge required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The seller must know the material contains defamatory matter.

faqs.question: Does merely possessing such material amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: This section specifically targets sale or offering for sale.

title: Criminal Intimidation
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
officialText: Whoever threatens another with injury to his person, reputation, property, or to the person or reputation of anyone in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause alarm or to cause that person to do or omit any act which he is not legally bound to do or omit, commits criminal intimidation.
simpleExplanation: Criminal intimidation occurs when a person threatens another to create fear or force them to act against their will.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 506.
examples: Threatening to kill someone unless money is paid.
Threatening to damage property to force compliance.

faqs.question: Must the victim actually be frightened?
faqs.answer: The threat must be intended to cause alarm.

faqs.question: Can threats to reputation qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Intentional Insult with Intent to Provoke Breach of the Peace
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
officialText: Whoever intentionally insults another and thereby gives provocation, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause the person to break the public peace or commit any other offence, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Deliberately insulting someone to provoke violence, public disorder, or another offence is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Provoking a person with abusive language to start a fight.
Intentionally insulting someone to incite violence.

faqs.question: Is every insult an offence?
faqs.answer: No. The insult must be intended to provoke a breach of peace or offence.

faqs.question: Must violence actually occur?
faqs.answer: No.

title: Statements Conducing to Public Mischief
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
officialText: Whoever makes, publishes, or circulates any statement, rumour, or report with intent to cause fear, alarm, mutiny, public disorder, or incitement against classes or communities shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Spreading rumours or false statements that may cause public disorder, panic, or social unrest is an offence.
punishment: General: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both., Aggravated forms: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
examples: Spreading false rumours causing panic among the public.
Publishing messages intended to create communal tension.

faqs.question: Does this apply to social media posts?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic communications can be covered.

faqs.question: Why is this section important?
faqs.answer: It protects public order and social harmony.

title: Punishment for Criminal Intimidation
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
officialText: Whoever commits criminal intimidation shall be punished. Enhanced punishment applies where the threat relates to death, grievous hurt, destruction of property by fire, or imputations affecting reputation.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for threatening another person.
punishment: General: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both., Aggravated threats: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening to kill someone.
Threatening to burn down a person's house.

faqs.question: Which section defines criminal intimidation?
faqs.answer: Section 503.

faqs.question: Are death threats treated more seriously?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Criminal Intimidation by Anonymous Communication
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
officialText: Whoever commits criminal intimidation by anonymous communication, or having taken precautions to conceal the identity or residence of the person making the threat, shall be punished with additional punishment.
simpleExplanation: Anonymous threats attract additional punishment because the offender hides their identity.
punishment: Punishment under Section 506 plus imprisonment up to 2 additional years.
examples: Sending anonymous death threats.
Using fake accounts to issue threats online.

faqs.question: Does this apply to anonymous emails?
faqs.answer: Yes.

faqs.question: Why is punishment increased?
faqs.answer: Because concealment makes the offence more serious.

title: Act Caused by Inducing Person to Believe That He Will Be Rendered an Object of Divine Displeasure
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes or attempts to cause a person to do something which that person is not legally bound to do, by inducing a belief that divine displeasure will result if the act is not done, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Using fear of divine punishment or supernatural consequences to force someone to act is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Forcing donations by claiming divine punishment will occur otherwise.
Threatening supernatural consequences to compel action.

faqs.question: Is religious coercion covered?
faqs.answer: Yes, where it is used to force conduct.

faqs.question: Must the victim actually act?
faqs.answer: An attempt is also punishable.

title: Word, Gesture or Act Intended to Insult Modesty of a Woman
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
officialText: Whoever, intending to insult the modesty of any woman, utters any word, makes any sound or gesture, exhibits any object, intrudes upon the privacy of such woman, or causes such act to be seen or heard by her, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Insulting, harassing, or invading the privacy of a woman through words, gestures, or acts is a criminal offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Making obscene gestures toward a woman.
Sending offensive messages intended to insult a woman.

faqs.question: Does online harassment qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes, depending on the circumstances.

faqs.question: Is privacy intrusion covered?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Misconduct in Public by a Drunken Person
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
officialText: Whoever, in a state of intoxication, appears in any public place or in any place which it is a trespass to enter, and there conducts himself in a manner causing annoyance to any person, shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: A drunken person causing annoyance or disturbance in a public place commits an offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 24 hours, or fine up to ₹10, or both.
examples: Creating a public disturbance while intoxicated.
Harassing people in a public area while drunk.

faqs.question: Is mere drinking an offence?
faqs.answer: No. The misconduct or annoyance is the offence.

faqs.question: Must it occur in a public place?
faqs.answer: Generally yes.

title: Punishment for Attempting to Commit Offences Punishable with Imprisonment for Life or Other Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Of Attempts to Commit Offences
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable by this Code with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, or attempts to cause such offence to be committed, and in such attempt does any act towards the commission of the offence, where no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such attempt, shall be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-half of the imprisonment for life or one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.
simpleExplanation: A person who tries to commit a crime and takes a direct step towards committing it can be punished even if the crime is not completed. This section applies when the IPC does not provide a separate punishment for the attempt.
punishment: imprisonment: Up to one-half of the maximum imprisonment prescribed for the completed offence., fine: Fine may also be imposed if the completed offence carries a fine., both: Court may impose both imprisonment and fine.
examples: A person attempts to steal money from a locker but is caught before taking it.
A person tries to commit cheating by submitting forged documents, but the fraud is detected before money is transferred.
A person fires a gun intending to kill someone but misses the target.
A person attempts to break into a house to commit theft but is apprehended before entering.

faqs.question: What is the difference between preparation and attempt?
faqs.answer: Preparation involves planning or arranging to commit a crime, whereas attempt begins when a person takes a direct step towards committing the offence.

faqs.question: Can someone be punished even if the offence was not completed?
faqs.answer: Yes. An attempt itself is punishable under Section 511.

faqs.question: When does Section 511 apply?
faqs.answer: It applies when there is no specific IPC section providing punishment for attempting that particular offence.

faqs.question: Is intention important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The prosecution must establish the intention to commit the offence and an act towards its commission.
